top_profane.text:
The opera DIE SCHULDIGKEIT DES ERSTEN GEBOTS K. 35, is Mozart's first full-scale opera, written in 1767 in Salzburg, labeled a "cantata spirituale" only because secular operas were ruled out during the Lent season, replaced by sacred dramas. As the first part of a trilogy, it was performed on March 12, 1767, repeated on April 2, 1767.

Philips's "[[ASIN:B000050GG5 Complete Mozart Edition]]" (1991) labels it an "oratorio".
But it is a real opera, with action onstage, dramatic tension in the confrontation between two parties competing for a man's soul, and development of one character (Christian). The preaching and sermonizing is light and caricatural, and not the subject of the plot.
The 2006 Salzburg Festival included it in its production of Mozart's 22 operas. So did Deutsche Grammophon's [[ASIN:B000I8OFKU M-22 Boxset]] (2007), and Decca's CD box of "[[ASIN:B001TH28EY The Complete Operas]]" (2009).
In both Mozart's life, and the history of opera, it is an extraordinary historical achievement.

Mozart, age 11, came to this work as a full-fledged, accomplished, composer.
He already had written 6 vocal pieces, including three important arias:
- A motet in G minor K. 20, "God is Our Refuge" (July 1765, London)
- A long, 6'30", aria for tenor K. 21, "Va dal Furor portata" (London, 1765), a song of anger.
- A second substantial, also 6' 30", aria for soprano, "Conservati fedele" K. 23, (The Hague, 1765).
- A kyrie for choir and strings K. 33, (2d. visit to Paris, June 1766).
- A "Stabat mater" for 4 voices in G K. 33c, (Paris or Salzburg, 1766), (lost).
- A third aria for tenor K. 36, "Or che il dover m'astringe" (Salzburg, Dec. 1766), for the archbishop's anniversary. This important, 9' 30" long, aria gained Mozart his first operatic commission.
Mozart had demonstrated advanced instrumental skills by composing, unstoppably, piano sonatas, various pieces for French horn, flute, cello, 16 violin sonatas, and 5 symphonies.

During Mozart's 2d. visit to Paris, Melchior Grimm wrote a glowing encomium about the Mozart children in his Correspondance Littéraire, of July 15, 1766,. Commenting on Mozart's remarkable progress, Grimm predicted: "He has even written several Italian arias, and I have little doubt that before he has reached the age of twelve, he will already have had an opera performed at some Italian theatre."

Daines Barrington told the story that the Archbishop, to verify the authenticity of Mozart's gifts, had him locked up for a week to write his short "oratorio".

A VERY UNUSUAL TITLE AND LIBRETTO

Amazingly, the 208-page autograph score lies in the Royal Collection at the Windsor Library.

The libretto is attributed to Ignaz Anton Weiser, a textile merchant, councillor and future mayor of Salzburg. His grand-daughter was Josepha Duschek, for whom Mozart later wrote 2 great concert arias: "Ah, lo previdi," K. 272 (1777), and "Bella mia fiamma, addio," K. 528 (1787).

The mouth-filling title is from the Gospel of Mark 12:28-31, introducing "The Great Commandment".

28. And one of the scribes came up and heard them disputing with one another, and seeing that he answered them well, asked him, "Which commandment is the most important of all?"
29. Jesus answered, "The most important is, `Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one.
30. And you shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind and with all your strength.'
31. The second is this: `You shall love your neighbor as yourself.' There is no other commandment greater than these."

The preface of the libretto says the aim is "not only to delight the mind but also to elevate the soul", as "there is no more dangerous state for the soul than a lack of alacrity in the work of salvation".

There's no sexual entanglement, no crime. The tension derives from a fight of Christianity against Worldly Spirit to win over an innocent mortal (Christian) to the First Commandment of "loving God with all your heart and soul", a love which is, surprisingly, not spontaneous. The targeted convert is reluctant to venture on this forbidding and strange route.

5 characters are involved:

- Christgeist: The "Spirit of Christ", translated as "Christianity" -- a static name, leaving unclear how this allegory can motivate human souls. In German, "geist", or Spirit, preserves the idea of a spiritual force.
- Barmherzigkeit: "Mercy"
- Gerechtigkeit: "Justice"
- Weltgeist: "Worldly Spirit". ("Worldliness" is too abstract a label, losing the dynamic meaning of "geist"). Traditionally, seductive Worldly Spirit should be a "she". Cf Goethe's Faust. She is the most fascinating character.
Der Christ: "Christian". But this "mortal" is not a christian yet, not "lapsed", not "half-hearted", not even a hesitant fence-sitter. He is an innocent, ordinary Joe, ignorant of the issue of "salvation". The Christianity party wants to inject this new obsession into his inexperienced soul.

This conflict remains obscure to modern viewers, but was burningly actual to the cultural elite in 18th c Europe, when the rising Enlightenment with its new secularism threatened the entrenched dogmas of Christianity.
This libretto is far from conventional, not openly religious, more like a mystery play, giving attractive colors to Worldly Spirit, and certainly not Hell's black or red wrongly used by John Dew in the M-22 DVD.

A VERY UNUSAL PLOT

A "half-hearted" (?) Christian is asleep in a garden near some bushes.
Near him are debating Christianity, Mercy, and Justice. Christianity is eager to save souls from the seductive lure of Worldly Spirit. He appeals to Justice, who can do nothing for the unrighteous, only Mercy can, but she explains that men are responsible for their choice: They ignore the duty of obeying the First Commandment, as ordained by "nature" and "reason", because they are lured by the pleasures offered by Worldly Spirit. Thus, they ignore death, the Last Judgment, and the horrors of Hell.

Christianity looks for a way to palliate this human weakness and restore the assumed "call of nature and reason" (which is not working), by giving man an advance taste of the horrors of Hell. Mercy, and then Justice, agree: "Look, I shall now carry out the experiment on this mortal, whom a false sense of security has plunged into a deep sleep."
Christian, in spite of his name, is not a previous christian. He is a simple mortal not even aware of the First Commandment. Justice will use sleeping Christian as a test, with a serious exhortation about the day of reckoning in the Last Judgment. Mercy and Justice depart in the clouds, while Christianity hides in the bushes to observe the effect of their warning.

Christian awakes, still under the shock of the warning heard in his sleep. Worldly Spirit appears to reassure him. The warning he's heard was constructed to lead him astray, or it was a dream that should not frighten him (No.4 ARIA). She eloquently proclaims the credo of life:
"The Creator has given us this life and free run of the world, so rejoice, laugh, have fun, and let dreams be dreams."

Christian is still haunted by the exhortation heard in his sleep, with the last Trumpet (a solo trombone) still resounding. Worldly Spirit assures him that these terrors were fabricated by a common enemy, who wants to deprive men of happiness.
Christianity overhears, outraged, this slander, and decides to return, disguised as a doctor. Worldly Spirit exhorts Christian to be wary of "severe and morbid philosophers".

Christianity reappears disguised as a doctor. Christian begs the doctor for a remedy to preserve happiness and health in old age. Christianity mentions he knows the best doctor, and his great "book" of cures. Worldly Spirit finds this spiel boring, and withdraws to prepare breakfast. Christianity gives Christian a sealed paper containing the cure. Worldly Spirit comes back to take Christian away to breakfast.

Christianity laments that the pleasures offered by Worldly Spirit will defeat his project. Mercy and Justice reappear, blaming Christian's failure as his own doing, but patience is needed. The signs are there of hope and possible success.

So, this first part leaves things open. The dice have not been (yet) obviously loaded in favor of Christianity.

THE PUZZLE OF CHRISTIANITY -- THE FEAR OF HELL IS ITS MAJOR TOOL TO SECURE THE "SALVATION" OF SOULS

To win the prize, Christianity uses disguise and deceit. Worldly Spirit comes on straight, showing her goods and her temptations -- life, joy, pleasures -- which Goethe's Faust will also later encounter.
Christianity has to pretend he is a doctor of souls. But for this role, he first has to inject the very malaise -- the fear of hell -- to start poisoning poor Christian's soul, and only then re-appear in his trappings of spiritual care-giver and promise to deliver the cure for the very torment he has initially injected.

The librettist presents the dilemma of the story in a credibly dramatic fashion. There's a big puzzle: Why does Christian not respond spontaneously to the First Commandment, but needs to be exhorted and motivated? Paradoxically, the First Commandment, promoted by Christianity, does NOT immediately appeal to man. This remains a mystery, "explained" by the doctrine of his "free will".
Then the challenge, as for any new ideology to gain traction, is how to convince ordinary mortals to believe in the strange new moralistic Commandments and to follow their leader.

The most direct method is through inspiring FEAR -- the fear of not being "saved". Saved from what? -- mostly saved from the risk of ending up in hell. The FEAR OF HELL is the psychological tool that Christianity will use to win over the unconvinced mortal.

In the RECITATIVE following No.2 ARIA, Christianity explains the method for securing salvation, starting with "Es würde des Verstandes Licht":

"The light of understanding could soon perhaps appear in all its brightness, and the topsy-turvy mind could soon yield, if the tormenting and horrifying vision of the gaping chasm of Hell should be visible suspended before their eyes (switching to an accompanied recitative) if the hideous howling from so many mouths were to resound, if one of the damned himself were to rise up from his grave, in order to teach them through his unhappy fall the solemn duty to maintain the great First Commandment, the zeal, the devotion, the knowledge of salvation."

Christianity has to counteract the natural appeal of Worldly Spirit -- which the seductive enchantment of Inga Nielsen's crystal-like voice makes irresistible.

The librettist uses this engine cleverly to animate the action. Some Boeotian reviewers dismiss this text with superficial epithets and pretentious comments, having never read the libretto, entirely missing the point of its dynamics.

You cannot undervalue and dismiss this opera, once you have a clear insight into its plot and its central conflict.

MOZART'S RAVISHING MUSIC

(Critical quotes from Hermann Abert's "[[ASIN:0300072236 W.A. MOZART]]")

In any commentary of any of Mozart's "early" operas, including "Schuldigkeit", one unavoidably keeps meeting the same two clichés:
1)The work is an accomplished and professional score, but not comparable to the "great" scores of "maturity";
2) but it still is an astonishing, amazing achievement, for an 11- (or 14-, or 15-, or 19-)year old boy (which it truly is).

"Schuldigkeit" is ravishing music, with delicate seductive beauty, expressing fresh and spontaneous youthful energy. Mozart delights in "word-painting", producing exciting effects that evoke souls falling towards the gaping abyss of hell, rivers bursting their banks, a lion roaring and prowling for preys, and vivid music in the accompanied recitatives to illustrate the sleeper's awakening, and the persistent memory of his nightmares.

Mozart takes the emotional turmoils seriously. He keeps the drama alive, maintaining the suspense and the feeling of urgency, never letting the tension abate.
He focuses on the basic conflict: evangelical Christianity, who brandishes the fear of the torments of hell to counter the appeal of Worldly Spirit, who promotes the pursuit of pleasure and extols the joy of living and happiness -- and the mental disarray of troubled Christian pushed by one, pulled by the other.
Christianity enrolls the help of Mercy and Justice, who do their best, but cannot overrule Christian's free will, on which depends his concern for salvation. They assist, but can only observe the conversion efforts of Christianity.
Nothing is settled in advance, the dice are not pipped here in favor of Christianity. Worldly Spirit looks like a winning contender in the contest for the soul of Christian.

There is action, surprises, and dramatic confrontations. The music accurately differentiates the 5 characters, Mercy full of compassion, Justice imbued with sanctimonious severity, while Worldly Spirit gets the most exciting music with seductive and vibrant arias of great virtuosity and complexity.

Mozart, in the tradition of Northern Germanic music, fortifies orchestral support, to "help characterize the dramatis personae", over the Italian preference for pure vocalism. His love for instrumental effects and his predilection for winds (oboes, horns, bassoons, and, here, trombone) are manifest.
He learnt from Johann Christian Bach how to separate viola parts for enhanced dramatic expression.
"The violas are regularly divided throughout the work as a whole: in this respect, too, the boy was clearly keen to show that he was fully aware of the times" (Abert).
In Christian's only aria, No.5 "Jener Donnerworte Kraft", the haunting warning of a solo trombone floats as the trumpet of the last Judgment. Mozart adds two flutes to the second aria of Worldly Spirit, No.6 "Schildre einen Philosophen", in which she derides Christianity's pomposity and arid exhortations.

Note Mozart's remarkably "careful handling" of the recitatives, whereas "under Italian influence, Mozart later adopted an increasingly negligent approach to secco recitative." He switches to three accompanied recitatives to heighten the emotional impact, for instance in describing Christian's nightmares.

Comparing young Mozart's first effort at musical drama to his late period is unfair and misguided. The focus should be on young Mozart's exceptionally seductive music, and his dramatic sense, which stand on their own. We can already hear the unmistakable features that will be embellished in future works, reappearing as amplified echoes of Mozart's youthful style.

Three things stand out in this superbly competent first opera:
- First, the remarkable technical knowledge of music already acquired by the young composer able to produce the most exquisite music;
- Second, his extraordinary sensitivity to human perceptions and emotions, and his spontaneous ability to express this sensitivity through music;
- Third, his talent at sustaining the pace and suspense of dramatic action. Once you know the libretto and get into the existential positions of the characters, the drama remains engrossing to the end.
Thus, Mozart was already manifesting three essential skills needed for musical drama that made him ideally suited to the career of a professional opera composer. That was his ultimate desire in life, and the tragedy of his life (and his father Leopold's intense bitterness) is that he was never able to realize it.

The overall impression in "Schuldigkeit" is one of youthful lightness and freshness, charming sincerity, seduction, and suspense. It is advanced music, "highly accomplished, full of descriptive flair and artistry".

INTOXICATING SINGING

To project Mozart's exciting vibrancy, Marriner has recruited a balanced cast of 5 magnificent singers: 2 tenors versus 3 sopranos, all with distinctly different voices, thus achieving a good differentiation of their characters.
- Margaret Marshall, as lamenting Mercy, projects her compassion, while her intoxicating voice intones her beautiful No.2 Aria "Ein ergrimmter Löwe brüllet", with her refined coloratura;
- Ann Murray, as severe Justice, delivers her supremely enchanting aria No.3 "Erwache, fauler Knecht", full of high-road sanctimoniousness;
- Inga Nielsen, as key Worldly Spirit, has two arias with the most seductive and challenging music, proclaiming her "carefree hedonism". We would not like to live in a world without Nielsen's voice.
- Hans Peter Blochwitz, as dour Christianity, carries the brunt of the performance with Worldly Spirit, each with two arias.
- Aldo Baldin, perfect as fumbling Christian, has only one aria, No.5 "Jener Donnerworte Kraft", his golden voice enhanced by the haunting obbligato trombone, betraying his confusion and anguish. His awakening is tellingly expressed by the exquisite music in the recitative "Er reget sich".

This quintet of world-class voices works wonders to restore our "joie de vivre", our belief in sound emotions, and our irresistible enthusiasm for Mozart's music. I, for one, could listen to this music all day, every day, and I have, for many weeks.

MODERN OR PERIOD INSTRUMENTS?

Neville Marriner (May 1988, Stuttgart) tackles the score with all seriousness, in the same intense manner as if he was conducting one of the great Mozart masterpieces, with a heavier orchestra and modern instruments. Same right approach by Nikolaus Harnoncourt (2006, staging at the Theater an der Wien), in view of the world-class singers in the cast. Marriner can thus bring the music to its total bloom.
Ian Page (2013, ([[ASIN:B00ET4RH0Y Signum]]) prefers a smaller orchestra, closer to the baroque style, with period instruments, which sound crisper and more buoyant -- creating more liveliness than Marriner does, but losing instrumental richness and density.

WORLDLY SPIRIT IS NO "DEVIL"

To stage Worldly Spirit as a "devil", as is done to excite the interest of spectators, is nearly downright absurd, and a gross distortion of the play. It defuses the immanent tension between the joy of life and the grim severity of Christian duty, that Goethe's Faust will preserve.

Harnoncourt, however, like Joseph Wallnig in the [[ASIN:B000ICL3PG M-22 DVD]], could not overcome the distraction of the comic gags on stage, blurring the focus on the ravishing beauty of the music, still the essence of the work.
Playing this opera as a comedy is very pleasant for an audience looking for fun time -- in the relaxation of an evening entertainment or a summer festival -- avoiding what would seem a rebarbative subject to an unknowing audience. But this misses the intention of the librettist and of Mozart himself, who never inject contrived comic elements but preserve a sense of solemnity and seriousness throughout.

We still have to wait for an authentic staged interpretation that does not transmogrify this "seria" work into a farce of pure "buffa". True, respecting the spirit of "Schuldigkeit" remains a challenge to modern producers eager to draw the public to the theater, but it can be done. For instance, both "Apollo et Hyancinthus", and "[[ASIN:B000I8OFLE Il Re Pastore]]" in the M-22 collection, were able to convey the authentic play while making it visually interesting to the audience.

NUMBERS & ARIAS -- SYNOPSIS & COMMENTS

8 numbers:
1 terzetto, Finale No.8 (Christianity, Mercy, Justice)
7 arias distributed as follows:

- Christianity: 2 arias, No.1, No.7 (tenor)
- Mercy: 1 aria, No.2 (soprano)
- Justice: 1 aria, No.3 (soprano)
- Worldly Spirit: 2 arias, No.4, No.6 (soprano)
- Christian: 1 aria, No.5 (tenor)

All arias are "cast in the older form, with a binary A-section, a B-section to different words, and a da capo of the A-section...It is however, with astonishing assurance that Mozart operates within this framework, successfully creating a number of outstanding atmospheric images that make up for their lack of originality with the authenticity of their emotion" (p. 73).

The sinfonia is a single-movement, following Johann Christian Bach. "Mozart's declamatory style is not only outstanding in its own term, but also reveals a genuine sympathy with the subject matter, while his harmonies generally reflect the course of the developing dialogue. Indeed he even attempts from time to time to flesh out the individual characters." (p. 71)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------

Initial dialogue: Justice answers Christianity's request for help in his mission to save souls. She doesn't want to help the lazy ones. "My duty is to reward the devout and to punish those who do not through atonement and repentance free themselves of guilt". Christianity then appeals to Mercy. He worries about "the small number who make the effort to walk the narrow path" while the blind masses "run along the wide, flower-strewn street into the open jaws of Hell."

No.1 ARIA: "Mit Jammer muss ich schauen" (Christianity = tenor, HANS PETER BLOCHWITZ)

"I have to watch all the souls falling into my enemy's claws. Their unbridled senses, like flooding rivers, sweep them away."

[Christianity pleads with Justice and Mercy to show forgiveness for sinners."Sustained note of anguish of its opening section". Mozart delights in word-painting: Beginning: Impressive description of the souls "falling" towards Hell; middle: "rivers that burst their banks". Christianity carries the burden of preaching wisdom, while living in anxiety.]

------------------------------------------------------------------

Mercy: It's their own fault. They ignore "The First, greatest, indeed the most important Commandment - with all their soul, heart and strength to love their Lord and God". Justice: They don't think of their salvation, of Heaven, Hell, Death, and Judgment. Mercy: They don't want to hear my warning. Then I cannot find compassion for them.

No.2 ARIA "Ein ergrimmter Löwe brüllet" (Mercy = soprano, MARGARET MARSHALL)***

"An enraged lion roars and prowls around looking for prey. But the hunter wants to keep on sleeping, laying down his weapons and forgoing any defense."

[This aria is "remarkable for its concertante winds and an independent onomatopoeic figure in the second violin, raging lion prowling." Mozart takes delight in depicting the roaring lion with horns and strings.]

------------------------------------------------------------------

Recitative "Was glaubst du" (Mercy, Justice, Christianity) -- "What do you think?" [Christianity] "They're irresponsible as if numbed. A remedy can be found to waken the minds of sinners, if the tormenting and horrifying vision of the gaping chasm of Hell should be visible".

Mozart first describes the vision of the abyss of Hell. ACCOMPANIED RECITATIVE: "wenn aus so vieler Tausend Mund" -- "if the hideous howling from so many mouths were to resound
if one of the damned himself were to rise up from his grave,
in order to teach them through his unhappy fall the solemn duty to maintain the great First Commandment, the zeal, the devotion, the knowledge of salvation."

Mozart now describes the effect of the tortured screams of the damned with an energetic orchestra and sonorous brass, concluded with the dramatic description of the ghostly image of the "damned rising from the grave". "He exaggerates to make his point".

Christianity: "A warning, a shock, has to be given." -- Justice: OK, I'll go along, but men remain free in their choice of following my call or fleeing towards the abyss of Hell. She introduces Christian: "Look, I shall now carry out the experiment on this mortal, whom a false sense of security has plunged into a deep sleep."

No.3 ARIA: "Erwache, fauler Knecht" (Justice = soprano, ANN MURRAY)***

"Wake up, lazy scoundrel, for your severe judgment. There Hell and Death beckon, you must give an exact reckoning of your life to the Lord your God!"

[Justice leaves the final decision of how to live his life to Christian. He bears the final responsibility of his actions and salvation. This is the Christian doctrine of "free will"]

------------------------------------------------------------------

RECITATIVO: "Er reget sich" (Christianity, Mercy, Justice)*** -- "He is stirring"
Mercy and Justice leave on clouds. Christianity hides to watch the effect of their speech.

ACCOMPANIED RECITATIVE: "Wie, wer erwecket mich?" (Christian) -- "What's going on, who woke me up? I can't see anyone here. Was this an illusion? Was it real or a trick? Death, Hell, Judgement - tell me, you senses of mine...Worldly Spirit: What judgement, what death, what hell? What are these whims? This was a dream, an illusion, nothing to fear. Trust me instead."

Mozart has a unique talent to describe physical events in music, here the changes in consciousness as Christian awakes, while remembering his dream. "The accompanied recitative of Christian stretching himself as he wakes from a deep sleep is realistic in the extreme. The Christian then palpably feels all the terrors of death and the Last Judgment and even hears ghostly reminiscences of motifs from Justice's previous aria (No.3 `Erwache, fauler Knecht').

No.4 Aria "Hat der Schöpfer dieses Leben" (Worldly Spirit = soprano, INGA NIELSEN)***

"The Creator has given us this life and free run of the world, so rejoice, laugh, have fun, and let dreams be dreams. Enjoy the world, and pursue pleasure."

[Worldly Spirit's lovely first aria, "notable for its Handelian subject." Worldly Spirit is "a figure remarkable for its inchoate characterization: for all his wicked worldly ways, there is almost a sense of chivalry and true style about him." So, Abert using "wicked" here is a questionable rhetorical exaggeration. Neither the libretto or Mozart depicts Worldly Spirit as "wicked". Mozart is full of sympathy for her. The violas play in octaves with the violins in the Viennese tradition.]

------------------------------------------------------------------

ACCOMPANIED RECITATIVE: "Daß Träume Träume sind" (Christian) -- "Dreams are dreams. This was more than a dream. The words made me shiver. I feel only half alive."
"Christian is accompanied by a concertante alto trombone as the voice ("trumpet") of the Last Judgment." The sound of the obbligato trombone signifies that Christian is beginning to hear the call of the Last Day, and it heralds the stir of his Christian conscience. The trombone solo is haunting and gripping. Mozart always adored winds and poured his soul into many concertos for horns, flutes, clarinets.

No.5 ARIA: "Jener Donnerworte Kraft" (Christian = tenor, ALDO BALDIN)***

"The power of those words call for my reckoning. I can hear the sound of the last Trumpet."

[This is Christian's only aria. The concertante obbligato trombone punctuates all his words, creating a haunting atmosphere. Christian conveys a certain innocence about life, and his lack of concern about the issues of death and salvation, which is what Christianity wants to activate.]

------------------------------------------------------------------

RECITATIVO: "Ist dieses, o so zweifle nimmermehr" (Worldly Spirit, Christian, Christianity)
(Christianity): "Er ist ein Mückenfänger, der andern, wie ihm selbst fast keine Freude gönnt," --
"He's a whimsical crank
who allows others, or himself,
almost no pleasure,
who shuns all company and conversation
with stimulating people,
who measures the length, depth and breadth
of every stupid notion of conscience,
who seeks to force
on everyone his moral philosophy,
which is full of naïve simple-mindedness
but very annoying and inflexible;
his words, thoughts and deeds
are nothing but priests' mumbo-jumbo.
In a word,
he's a completely peculiar sort." ("er ist von ganz besondrer Art.")

Worldly Spirit does not hesitate to treat her enemy in sarcastic and dismissive terms. She paints the believer's lifestyle in rebarbative terms and grim colors, his moral claims as spurious, his arguments as hokum-pokum. (Same idea in Bastienne mocking the "Zauberei" of Colas.) Christianity, hidden, is scandalized by Worldly Spirit's "shameless lies". To carry out his plan, he goes to disguise himself as a doctor.

No.6 ARIA "Schildre einen Philosophen" (Worldly Spirit)***

"Picture a philosopher with saddened eyes, austere and timid of gesture and of pallid countenance. Then you have him."

[Worldly Spirit is "joined by two flutes". The only aria that has no middle section. "Nor is she lacking in sarcasm: Compare the eloquent melisma at the words 'mit betrübten Augenlichtern,' ('with saddened eyes,') with the gentle humour of the brief andante section 'Dann hast du ein Bild getroffen,' ('Now you have conjured an image')." Worldly Spirit is mocking Christianity's "pomposity". Philosophers lose out on the joy of living. Same idea in Goethe's Faust. Inga Nielsen's lovely voice carries the day for her promotion of the superiority of the joys of life.]

------------------------------------------------------------------

RECITATIVO: "Wen hör' ich nun hier in der Nähe?" (Worldly Spirit, Christ, Christianity) -- "Whom do I now hear close by? It must be the trickster that went into hiding "

Christianity re-appears as a doctor, (of souls). Christian asks for a medicine for aging healthily, and overcoming his fear and anxiety. Christianity knows the greatest doctor and his "book" which has the best cure. Worldly Spirit finds the talk too long, and says it's time for breakfast. She leaves, saying she knows of a better cure "a loving look from his fair beloved, or good food, drink, gambling and hunting".

No.7 ARIA "Manches Übel will zuweilen" (Christianity)***

"Many a disease sometimes needs a scalpel, scissors and flame before it can be healed by medicine. That summons which awakened you, that voice which startled you, was necessary for you, was good for you."

[Christianity's second ARIA. He advises on the need for surgery, but a spiritual one. Although the violins put some funny accent on the words "scalpel, scissors", the effect is not really sinister and does not suggest any painful surgical procedure. The aria is remarkable for its "warm and heartfelt appeal". Mozart liked is so much that he re-used it the following year in "La Finta Semplice", in "Cosa ha mai la donna indosso" to describe tender feelings!]

------------------------------------------------------------------

RECITATIVO: "Er hält mich einem Kranken gleich" (Christian, Christianity, Justice) -- "He regards me as a sick man, he knows what's happened to me"
Christianity gives a document to Christian, which holds the remedy to restore him completely. Worldly Spirit reappears, announcing all is ready. Christianity complains that the rush to pleasure hinders the progress of the soul. Mercy and Justice come back, extolling the value of their help for the salvation of Christian.

No.8 TERZETTO "Laßt mich eurer Gnade Schein" (Christianity, Mercy, Justice)

- Christianity: "May the radiance of your grace never desert me, so that I can gain new courage. My task is to win dear souls for my Creator."
- Mercy, Justice: "The radiance of our grace will never desert you, if man does his duty ("wenn der Mensch das Seine tut.")

[Christianity's mission is to save souls for his Creator. Mercy and Justice promise to help, but leave the final outcome up to men. Abert: "The final trio is no more than an aria sung by each of the voices in turn, a pleasant enough piece with simple imitative writing".
Abert has never heard the opera sung live or performed on stage. His appreciation is too bookish. We, however, exposed the beautiful voices of real singers find it delightful.]

CONCLUSION

Thus, at the end of Mozart's work, Christian is free to go on with the "joy of living", following Worldly Spirit's call. The future remains wide open. So, the subject of "Schuldigkeit" is the problem of man's choice, of his "free will". It is closer to Paul and Kierkegaard than Augustine.
It is too bad that part II is lost. It is only in final part III (by Anton Adlgasser), lost as well, that Christianity will win Christian over, as he repents, embraces the First Commandment, and is saved.

Mozart's first dramatic work, surprising in its beauty and musical brio, will go a long way towards stimulating more interest in and better appreciation of Mozart's early operas, so far grossly neglected and undervalued by an ignorant public and opera houses.
The final commandment becomes: "Love young Mozart with all thy heart, all thy soul, and with all thy mind."

ROO BOOKAROO
Sept. 1, 2014
top_profane.title: A stunning revelation: The most ravishing, seductive opera by the youngest composer ever

top_profane.text:
It's rare for a band to come along that has a unique sound. Garbage was one such band many years ago. Their Garbage 2.0 is just as fresh today as they day it came out. Yes, we all suffered through Beautiful Garbage, but all bands have a CD where they try new things.

Bleed Like Me take Garbage back to what made them different in the first place. Garbage has such a unique sound that it is incorrect to call them a Rock n Roll band. We should just stop right here and call it something that no one else is doing. They play "Garbage Rock." When a band plays something that is very different, it usually stands the test of time. People will be playing Garbage music long after we are gone.

Bleed Like Me is tight. It is Garbage refined. Purely tested, knuckle dragging hell in the studio hard core genius. Shirley Manson has more emotion on this CD than in the past. Pain and tragedy always brings out the best in bands, but we won't go there. We've all read the stories, but that's personal and we only get to enjoy the music and hope the best for all band and contributing artists on this CD.

Bad Boyfriend introduces us back to the Garbage we have missed and always loved. This is a subtle Garbage coy to bring us in. Garbage has their usually surprise waiting for us. This just wets our appetite for more. Go ahead listen. The guitars are clean, tight and sure on this track.

Run Baby Run begins with a simple guitar playing. Garbage makes it sound so easy. One guitar and then the drums come in and it all works so well.

Shirley belts out an anthem of how hard things can be in life up front and personal. Her vocals have gotten better over the years and she is at the top of her game now.

OK. Get ready for the best song on a CD in the last ten years. Put that seat belt on real tight as Garbage takes you for a ride. Right Between the Eyes is Garbage at its ultimate best. Garbage puts rock in your face, kicks your ass and takes your name. The song pulls you in and then spits you out at the end. The drums are just perfection. The power of the band members was all worth the effort. This is Garbage's best song ever. This one song will make Garbage live forever. Garbage speaks to everyone with this song. So many people being slammed by someone else out there and so many other people who don't care or laugh when you are down. Such an uplifting song speaks volumes to people around the world.

Metal Heart has just the right mix of tecno sound. They got it right on this one.

Sex Is Not the Enemy is one of those songs where you hear Shirley's voice and just plain get excited. There are many people out there that can sing. That's great, but they have no depth, no range. Shirley has so much range that she can project the sound and the feeling at the same time; a feat not accomplished by many young singers today. Shirley gets it.

It's All Over but The Crying is another song that could be used by anyone that is just about to lose someone. This is such a great song. If you get past the sadness, the song has perfect pitch and balance. The sound has been refined into a very clean, tight machine. Pure magic.

Boys Wanna Fight begins as a very ruff sounding track, but the band cuts in and you're off for another roller coaster Garbage rock ride. I want to go again!

Why Don't You Come Over shows that just when you think the ride is over, Garbage is ready to dish out more pure power.

If you've been waiting for an album to come out worth its weight in rock, run don't walk to your local record shop and get this one. You won't be disappointed. This is one of those CDs that will be played for years to come.

Every generation has its musical heroes. Thank God we have Garbage.
top_profane.title: Review: Bleed Like Me

top_profane.text:
When one looks back at the alternative rock scene that dominated the first half of the 90s, Garbage's self-titled debut album immediately comes to mind as a defining album from that movement. A decade old as of this writing, "Garbage" (1995) has stood the test of time and not only contains some of the best singles from the 90s, but is a modern-day classic.

"Garbage" has many elements in its sound which can explain its broad appeal. The songs are so incredibly infectious and danceable, fans of dance-pop (Madonna, Janet Jackson) are sure to enjoy the album. But "Garbage" is undeniably a rock album, first and foremost. With crunching guitars, intense drumming, and a charismatic frontwoman, a more rock inclined audience is sure to dig "Garbage" as well. Garbage's music also encompasses an element of 70s/80s cool new-wave. In a sense, Garbage was like an updated version of Blondie for the 1990s, so fans yearning for a new Debbie Harry are sure to appreciate Garbage.

Another reason "Garbage" struck such a chord with the public is there really wasn't another band quite like them. There were bands that were precursors to Garbage, like Curve, and My Bloody Valentine, among others, but they didn't take off and explode as Garbage did, for whatever reason, probably due to marketing and timing. Garbage's debut came out at the perfect time. In an era of post-grunge, where many up-and-coming bands were clearly derivative of Nirvana and Pearl Jam, Garbage's brand of rock/pop/retro offered a refreshing change of pace. While Garbage's cynical themes fit the disparaging alt. rock 90s like a glove, the lyrics are often tongue-in-cheek. In this sense, Garbage had the best of both worlds: cynical lyrics for cynical times, but with the playful, sexy trimmings of Blondie.

Because the musicians of Garbage, Butch Vig, (drums) Steve Marker, (guitar) and Duke Erickson, (guitar) were made up of first-rate producers, namely Butch Vig, they knew how to craft songs meticulously and methodically. They also happened to be great songwriters' to-boot. Great songwriters and producers' aside, the band still needed the perfect singer...enter Shirley Manson.

While working on songs for their new band, Vig, Marker, and Erickson spotted the Scotch-born Shirley Manson on MTV. At the time Manson was the lead singer for (the underrated) Angelfish. Knowing Manson would be the perfect singer, the band sent out the word and a short time later Manson joined the group, thus Garbage was born.

It's one thing to have great songs and producers, but the perfect singer is needed to put a face on the music and the image. Truth be told, Garbage without Manson would have been like Van Halen without David Lee Roth. Without Manson, Garbage most likely would have been a good, but untimely forgettable band, a footnote in the 90s. While the songs on "Garbage" are excellent, no doubt, it is Manson that truly makes them shine. Truly a gifted singer, she can change from sounding sensitive to indifferent, seductive to vengeful, passionate to passive, all in a single breath. While her beautiful voice can enchant, it can also scorn. Much like Nico before her, Manson is a true femme fatale. Manson proved to be very popular with males and females alike. Her self-confidence, integrity, and self-empowerment no doubt struck a chord with a female audience. Being extremely attractive, a femme fatale, a sort of black widow, was no doubt appealing to men.

Garbage's unique brand of electronic pop/rock is demonstrated immediately with the seductive "Supervixen." Manson sounds as though she gets sadistic pleasure as she plays with the listener, telling her audience to "bow down to me." The off-beat "Queer" is equally seductive, and quite mesmerizing, leaving a lasting impression. The album's massive hit, the super infectious "Only Happy When it Rains" is without doubt, one of the greatest rock songs from the 1990s. It both epitomizes and mocks the self-involvement and narcissism of generation X. If Manson had toyed with the listener up to this point, she now shows her teeth with the aggressive "as Heaven is Wide." With its almost Black Sabbath-like riff, with electronic trimmings, it makes for an intriguing and captivating listen. The good-but-not-great "Not my Idea" is cut from the same cloth and keeps up the momentum. The somewhat spacey, "Stroke of Luck" shows traces of both beauty and darkness, as Manson lets her guard down as she sings "did you know I was lost until you found me." One of the catchiest, most memorable songs on the album, the vindictive "Vow" is both unforgiving and seductive. An ode to female empowerment, Manson shows her contempt for the dimwitted in "Stupid Girl." The hard-rocking but danceable "Dog new Tricks" keeps the album moving along. "Garbage" saves some of its best songs for last and demonstrates its mastery of pop-craft with the lush "My Lovers Box," and "Fix Me." Like most Garbage albums, the self-titled debut ends on a morose note with the cheerless "Milk," which leaves a lasting feeling.

Ten years old, Garbage's self-titled debut album has aged well and sounds as good today as it did back in '95. "Garbage" stands as one of the finest albums from the 1990s and is an essential cornerstone to add to any modern rock collection. People unfamiliar with Garbage are best served starting off here with the self-titled debut. But Garbage is no one-album-wonder band, and people who like what they hear here are advised to buy "Version 2.0," (1998) "Beautiful Garbage," (2001) and "Bleed Like Me" (2005).
top_profane.title: The classic debut

top_profane.text:
With that said, I now go onto the review: Metallica: the Black Album!m A truly awesome album! Metallica's first diamond selling album: 15 million strong today. How did they do it you ask? Well, it all begins with a change in style; they couldn't stay thrashy forever and expect to become gods, come on people: thrash is great but it will not get any band to godhood. Just look at Slayer, they have been doing thrash for 20+ years now and their greatest album went 4+ platinum and they think cursing is awesome and that saying: "oh! god hates us all!" they are living in a van down by the river compared to Metallica!

Track Listing:
1.Enter Sandman: What alot of people consider Metallica's trademark song, putting this track first best shows Metallica's evolution from crashy thrash since Dyer's Eve on ..And Justice for All. Even though this track is hard and heavy anybody who is anybody will dig this track. 10/10
2.Sad But True: You know how great a song is when even crappy rock posers like Kid Rock use the riffs in their songs (I still think "American Bad@ss" or whatever that track is called sucks, dont get me wrong). Kirk's riffs are outstanding, they are not like the overly heavy riffs from Justice, it sounds like hard rock guitar. If you could look up hard rock sound in the encyclopedia, this would be right next to it. 10/10
3.Holier than Thou: Even though they were no longer thrash, the transition isn't always a good thing. Take Holier than Thou for example: its a hard rock track but it is also obviously filler material. The guitar on here sounds too much like Scott Ian rhythm (which isn't bad, but this is Metallica) and the lyrics aren't really great ("No more, the crap rolls out your mouth again"? WTC!!) 6/10
4.The Unforgiven: Metallica's first true power ballad. I wouldn't call Fade to Black or Sanitarium power ballads, they are more darker songs, of subject material that is frightening while this song is about.... wait, what the crap is it about? It makes no friggn sense! The lyrics are inspired, Hammetts guitar work is moving, but I still don't know the message behind it! Unlike other so called "old metallica fans", I actually like this song better than their previous efforts; it shows real evolution for a band to go from "ahh, F#$k you!" to "want to join us at Borders?" 9.5/10
5.Wherever I may Roam: Another Tallica classic, the guitar work form Kirk on this track is ART. Thank god Lars and James finally let him wear the pants for at least this track or who knows how long he would have been doing thrashy riff after thrashy riff. If I learned anything from this track it is that if it weren't for Kirk's artistic playing in this album and the ones before it Metallica would sound like every other overtly heavy thrash band. 10/10
6.Don't Tread on Me: Unlike the whole Justice album, at least this song doesn't leave the listener thinking: "its about justice, ladefriggn DA!" Its a short song for Metallica in this age and time but the lyrics actually make freedom sound good. I think Kirk has a great guitar piece in this, which he probably picked up from lessons with Joe Satriani when he just joined Metallica. 10/10
7.Through the Never: This song sometimes seems like they added it just to make an album over an hour in length. First, the guitar work is weird, so almost everything else on this. Sounds like they were TRYING TO MAKE FILTER. Fluff: 6/10
8.Nothing Else Matters: This track is what I would call the centerpiece of the album. Sure, Enter Sandman may be the biggest single from this album and Unforgiven may be Tallica's first power ballad, but this is hard rock classic defined. Everyone is like:" Man, One is Metallica's best ever!" Whatever! One is nowhere near their best! It's great but not the best. The string arrangement adds a whole dimension to Metallica's playing and enriches Kirks playing. Kirks guitar spots are almost reminiscent of Randy Rhoads' spots in "Diary of a Madman" or "Revelation (Mother Earth)". Just when I though we would never hear what Kirk always called his "Rhoads influenced guitar playing", not only does the song sound great, but it ends up being what many call Metallica's greatest work. 11/10
9.Of Wolf and Man: Loud, Heavy, straight-out metal. The intro bang drum assault and loud tempo rings are stringing to the soul. Also, these lyrics scream "James Heitfeld!" so loud it isn't even funny. 10/10
10.God that Failed: A good song, not filler but not "Wow!" 8/10
11.My Friend of Misery: Long, complex songwriting with a bit of zucato instument work. Not very impressive, I think it is a little overated. 8/10
12.Struggle Within: It is really hard to write reviews for albums with more than 10 tracks, that is why these latter track reviews suck like crap, so I will try as hard as I can to do a good job on this one. This is the fast paced allout rocker on this album, the vocals are fast paced and aggressive, the drums and bass loud, but the standout on here is definitely Kirks solo: it is pure awesomeness. It sounds like classic VH virtuoso mixed with with heavy Motorhead-like tempo, the first obvious virtuoso guitar work i have heard from Kirk. 10/10

Anyone who calls this album a sell-out are idiots. Just because a band makes an album that goes diamond and get a ton of air time on the radio does not make it a sellout. If that were the case then Led Zeppelin would have 'sold out' after Led Zeppelin fourth, or the Eagles after Hell Freezes Over, or Van Halen after their first album(how can they sell out if it is their first album, dum@sses?)! If anything, Metallica got better after this album. Thrash is good, but did anyone actually think for one second that Metallica could become icons by remaining in the tharsh zone? no friggn way! In order for one to prosper in the music biz they must obtain art, and thrash is not art, it is extremely heavy bass, loud drums, and really distorted guitar noise. I'm not saying that everything before this album sucked, it just wasn't art. I am also not justifying St. Anger's existence, I think it was a stupid piece of crap that is only exceeded by Nevermind. If you guys want to call any Metallica album the sellout, choose Load; at least it makes sense because they cut their hair and wore mascera! But don't blame the Black album, which is by far their greatest ever.

Metallica album ranking:
1.Master of Puppets
2.Ride the Lightning
3.Black Album
4.And Justice For All (even though the production is crappy as hell)
5.Live S#$% and Purge!
6.Kill em' All
7.S&M
8.Reload
9.Load
10.Garage Days, Inc.
That piece of crap album St. Anger doesn't deserve to be on here! It doesn't even deserve to be in the toilet or a cup coaster.
top_profane.title: Leatherface, what the #$#@ are you talking about?!

top_profane.text:
A Superb collection of memories from yester-year covering many of the great-names from the
late 30's up to the late 50's, including favourites of indeed my parents such as 'Bing Crosby' - 'Frank Sinatra' and indeed 'Perry Como'
i myself remember listening to many of the songs on the radio as a child
Most of the material on-board is from the 50's era....though some a little earlier and a few indeed beyond.
Many music fans will notice that artists such as 'Jim Reeves' - 'Matt Monro' and 'Paul Anka' are also featured among the crooners on
the track-listings, these, though most definitely crooners are associated in the main during the emergence of the rock-era beyond the mid
-fifties.
This is a superb collection of timeless classics still played to this day including favourites such as - 'Bing Crosby' - 'Don't Fence Me In'....
..'Perry Como' - 'Catch a Falling Star'......'Al Martino' - 'Here inm my Heart'......'Jim Reeves' - 'I Love you Because' (recorded by many down
the years, though nobody ever done it better than 'Gentleman Jim')......'Harry Belafonte' - 'Day-O The Banana Boat Song'......and......'Pat
Boone' - 'Love Letters'......just some of the timeless classics to be found upon disc one......and among the track listings on disc two......
'Frank Sinatra' - 'Love So Well On You' (all-time great crooner and indeed an accomplished actor)......'Mel Torme' - 'Born To Be Blue' ......
'Nat King Cole' -'Mona Lisa'......'Jack Jones' - 'This Love of Mine'......and......'Sammy Davis jr' - 'That Old Black Magic'......again just a small
sample of the memories that can be found among the track-listings on disc two......and on disc three......'Bing Crosby' - Deep in the Heart of
Texas'......'Frankie Laine' - 'Jealousy'......'Tony Bennett' - 'The Good Life' (He still performs to this day, he's 89)......'Matt Monro' - 'Portrait of
my Love'......'Nat King Cole' - 'Unforgettable'......and......'Tony Martin' - Begin the Beguine'......again just some of the gems from yesterday to
be found upon disc three.......and on disc four......'Frank Sinatra' - 'So Long, My Love'......'Dean Martin' - 'Memories are made of This'......
'Buddy Greco' - 'Touch me in the Morning'......'Andy Williams' - 'Moon River'......'Billy Eckstine' - 'Boulevard of Broken Dreams'......and......
'Roy Hamilton' - 'I Believe'......just some of then great memories to be found among the tracks on disc four....
I have quite simply highlighted a few of the many golden memories that can be found on-board of this 4-CD Compilation to offer a flavour of
what can be found on-board, many more of course equally worthy of a mention.
Of course, almost all of the numbers found among the listings were indeed chart-hits of there day which many will of course remember, overall
this is a superb collection of memories...well worthy of being checked out.

top_profane.text: The fact that the vinyl version of "Lazaretto" has sold so insanely well proves once again the power of MILLIONAIRE MARKETING.  This record, at best, sounds like a sad ripoff of Beck's "Mellow Gold" from 1994, and at worst, like wannabe Mumford & Sons.  In sum, this record is lavishly-packaged, brilliantly-marketed, and UTTER CRAP.  If you want to listen to CRAP, then CHECK OUT what is TRULY the album of 2014:  TOTAL BULL CRAP "[[ASIN:B00LW5AZ7K Do Not Rebuy Your Old Favorites yet Again [Explicit]]]" (you can stream the whole album for free at Spotify).  Two things you can't deny--at least Total Bull Crap didn't record their album in a Voice-A-Graph booth, and secondly, they truly look to the future, to the days when you will potentially be able to DOWNLOAD YOUR FOOD ("[[ASIN:B00LW5BMB8 Downloadin' My Food [Explicit]]]").  Stop being slaves to million dollar marketing.  You want CRAP?  Then make it TOTAL BULL CRAP, where we TAKE A STAND against proverbial bull crap.  We are confident that you'll agree: TOTAL BULL CRAP never sounded so good.
top_profane.title: stop lining the appallingly-overrated rich man's pocket

top_profane.text:
I have to agree with a couple of the reviews that I read. One stating that this is the cheeziest GU mix to date!! I agree with that all the way!! There are way too many cheesy vocal tracks on this album set as well as that retro disco crap. Another reviewer states "It's been a while since GU released something worth purchasing." I agree with this also. What ever happened to the real moody, or dark, or straight up slamming progressive house that made all of us just drool for more???? I can't believe that a reviewer calls this crap progressive house!! And yet another is calling this dark progressive house!! I don't think they've heard or even own a real progressive house album.

If you like vocally and retro disco sounding crap, then this might be your album. I have one that I'll even give you just because this is only going to take up precious space on my CD rack that could be used for a real progressive house album!! One thing's for sure, this is going to be a dust collector as it's not going to be listened to any more. There is nothing on this album that makes me want to listen to it again, and again, and again....!!!! Only reason that I gave this album one more star than it really deserved was because of CD two. Eight tracks into it, it finally starts sounding good, then the last track spoils it all. WTF???

I really hope GU finds themselves before they flush what has been for the most part a good series right down the toilet. This pop style vocal, retro disco and tech house crap has to stop!!! As for pop music, I'm sorry, I don't listen to a 13 year olds music style and it shouldn't be incorporated into trance music!! I hate pop music!! It's just choreographed, no real talent cookie cutter crap.

To answer a reviewer, "What is commercial???". Well, the first name that comes to mind, UGH, sorry for this one, is Oakie!!! You should be able to guess from there, "What is commercial???", by this example. Also, "If it's not on MTV......". Question, when was there music on MTV??? Last time I checked there were some bimbos driving an RV, and a lot of other "reality" garbage. Some people need to get a life if they watch that crap. Talk about rotting your brain, just like this album does. Quote from another reviewer "What will GU#30 bring?" Exact question I have after hearing this trash. Hey GU, do us all a favor. Take out the trash because it's really starting to stink!! And for the last reviewer quote "Get it, you will not be dissapointed!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!" Well, I did, and I surely was disappointed!!!
top_profane.title: Not even close to being the best GU!!!

top_profane.text:
Van Halen's debut forever changed the landscape of rock n' roll. Of course it brought guitar rock back to the charts but it is also responsible for bringing concert monsters like Led Zeppelin and Aerosmith off the top of the mountain with Eddie's guitar work that made guys like Eric Clapton, Jimi Paige and Joe Perry seem like babies with broken fingers playing guitar. Also, guitar tapping is harder than it looks, i've tried it before and ended up in the hospital for cutup fingers. Also the pure virtuositiy of ALex and Micheal made up for the band's amazing rhythm section. Most rhythm sections just add ambience to a band but in Van Halen theirs pratically played like guitar noise.
Track Listing:

1.Runnin' with the Devil: The quintessential opener and great rocker. In the era of this song most rock acts were blues inspired but this made everything straight out rock riffs.
2.Eruption: PERHAPS THE GREATEST GUITAR SOLO EVER! IT IS IMPOSSIBLE, JUST TRY IT! I WOULD LOVE TO SEE "LEGENDS" LIKE PAIGE, PERRY, CLAPTON, OR COBAIN TRY TO DO THIS.
3.You Really Got Me: THe first real cover version of this song to get it right. Almost every version except this one is crappy, this one adds another dimension to it, making it twice as rockin as before.
4.Aint talkin' bout love: The quintessential Rothera song. All the others are great and all but this one defines the late 70's era where Van Halen just started their disco-killing.
5.I'm the One: Great boogy beat guitar rock! DLRs partyman lyrics mixed with bigband inspired riffs make this a must listen.
6.Jamie's Cryin': This is probably the song that can be created with the creation of hair metal in the early 80's. With the womp bass continual and the medium-high pitched guitar work and sex-driven vocals, it would go onto inspire party bands of the 80's.
7.Atomic Punk: Perhaps an attempt to throw salt at the wound of the punk movement. Punk used to be the big think until this happened, it prided itself on fast guitar but seeing that Eddie's is like 8 times as fast it is reasonable to call it the Atomic Punk. One of Van Halen's most metal songs ever.
8.Feel Your Love Tonight: Partyman boogy with incessant guitar riffs. The trademark chant "feel your love tonight" is also another great influence to the rise of hair metal.
9.Little Dreamer: A surprisingly Aero-influenced song. THe beginning riffs and vocal work seem reminiscent of late 70's influenced Asmith work. A genuinely great song.
10.Ice Cream Man: Begins with an acoustic intro that quickly turns into hard rockin plasma blast balls to the walls rock n roll. The greatest part of the track would be the suggestive lyrical structure: what the author is selling? (hahahahaha!)
11.On Fire: All out rocker. Van Halen at their meanest and heaviest. Plus Dave saying: "In your head" "In your Pants" in the high falsetto voice of his makes this a VH classic you must hear to believe.

Further note: Some unaccepting 70's rock fan made a dumb@ss remark about how crappy Eddie's playing was and that its hair rock. FIrst off, VH is not hair rock. For your information Led Zeppelin and Deep Purple are crap next to this. I would love to see Ritchie Blackmore or Jimi Paige attempt to play an intro to any CVH song and see if they last the first 15 seconds. The difference between Eddie and earlier guitarists is that he wasn't spending half his time "Dazed and Confused" while inhaling "Smoke in the Water". THe only reason why Paige is even in the top 10 guitarist list is because he played a friggn Les Paul with an bum@ss violin string! Face it 70's fans, you are just bitter that VH kicked the living Hell out of all of your bands and that Joe Perry and Jimi Paige could no longer sell crap at stadiums or venues. Quit living in the past and accept the fact that sure classic rock is good but Metal kicked the living crap out of it. You should move on with your life, if a trend of music takes the main stage dont bash it to death without a second listen. When Nirvana hit the scene was I pissed? Yes because they absolutely suck. But all the other alternative music I was cool with because I gave them all second chances (I gave Nevermind 4 chances that is like $40 over 10 years on a piece of crap record so don't say I didn't give Nirvana a chance). If you give this piece of rock history a chance your music taste will develop more.

Here is how I rank Van Halen from best to worst:
1.Fair Warning
2.Women and Children First
3.Van Halen
4.1984
5.Van Halen II
6.Diver Down
7.5150
8.For Unlawful Carnal Knowledge
9.Balance
10.OU812
11.Live: Right Here, Right Now
Yes, I will even put VHIII on this list 12.Van Halen III
top_profane.title: Possibly one of the greatest debuts of all time

top_profane.text:
This is the real thing - 30 slabs of savage rock `n' roll. I had selections of this release on a tape many many many years ago, but it left off 10-12 numbers that I'd never heard until I finally finally got both CDs. The funny thing is that I couldn't get Use Your Illusion in any shop in Singapore, I had to ask my wife to bring them back from Japan with her. Go figure!

I love all the songs. Use Your Illusion I (gold color) starts of with "Right Next Door To Hell", not a great to song, but it does have a great bass intro, like so many of the band's best songs (a punk layover - Duff played in punk bands, which tend to enjoy using the bass to set up songs). "Dust N' Bones" is a weird, slow sleazy groaner, whiskey-soaked, really perfect, although maybe Axl does lay the experimental vocal stuff on a bit thick (some of it sounds like Tuvan throat singing - what is this, the Mekons?!?). "Live And Let Die" is the inexplicable Paul McCartney cover... well, it sounds okay, but I tend to skip over it a lot, since it is very repetitive. "Don't Cry" on the first disc is nice, sorta, but over-played (especially since we get a second version of it on disc two (I can't tell them apart, maybe need a few more listens - musically they're identical, but with different verses and vocal metre...). Nice, I should try the same thing with my band, just burn a bit more studio time. "Perfect Crime" kicks out the jams, sounding screechy like underdeveloped songs from the 1986 EP, while "You Ain't The First" is some sort of weird cow punk novelty song that wouldn't quite feel out of place on the Doctor Demento show, her yuck. Hey, it's a double album, lots of room to try goofy new things, no reason to write a tight, punchy album like Appetite For Destruction. But it's still a fun-enough song. Hah. "Bad Obsession" is sleazy slide guitar-fuelled harmonica rock, also something a bit different. "Back Off Bitch" is a catchy song that rocks and steams. Nazty "bitch-bitch-bitch-bitch" thing happening at the end of that. Very dramatic. "Double Talkin' Jive" is a groovy tune that nearly sounds like it could have been on Appetite For Destruction. Great solo, love this song, especially the Spanish guitar at the end. Unfortunately, it's followed by the annoying "November Rain", with its orchestras, backup singers, grand pianos, seas of roses, and all sorts of other crap. Well, whatever. Confusingly, this is followed by two songs about gardens - "The Garden" and "Garden Of Eden." The former starts off less praising, but then Alice Cooper comes in and it gets sinister. Nice. "Garden Of Eden" is high octane, but not all that nice as Axl jibbers and jabbers and chatters. No matter, all of these songs, whether they're good or not, have a sensationally blustery Slash-driven solo. "Don't Damn Me" is a damn good song, even without the weird cartoon-character-getting-hit-on-the-head googly sound Axl makes at the beginning. "Bad Apples" gets off to a jazzy start before hitting out into some wicked honky tonk (this album sometimes sounds more like a Rolling Stones album than a Guns N' Roses album somehow, y'know?) "Dead Horse" is a catchy song that starts off mellow and then gets blazing. Nice. The last song on the album is probably the best - "Coma." It is also the longest, and with its tricky ending probably also the most challenging to perform - legend has it that the band only performed it four times (one of which was captured for the Live Era CD). Simply and spooky intro, weird drama through voices of medics and nurses, more drama with sighs and snatches of dialogue as the dying Axl Rose's life flashes before his eyes (and our ears). The breathless singing at the end is really a wonder to behold. Who would ever want this 10 minute-long song to end?
top_profane.title: I got used to their illusion

top_profane.text:
Just got this in 2014. I swear I have been looking for something new from Selene for years and the only thing I could find was a dead link for the Cistine song, which apparently was the only song recorded.

So I was very excited to hear this! Don't know how I missed it for four years. Some of the songs are very different from the Seven Year Bitch days, somewhat more produced than I would have liked. But when I was scanning the track listing the 2nd time, I noticed the song "Blue Side." In the waning days of Seven Year Bitch, they performed this song, including at the last three shows I saw, two in Seattle and one in Portland, in May of 1997. They played several new songs, that were to be on their next album and Blue Side was the only one I got the name of. That album never happened and it seemed those songs, which were said to have already been recorded, would never see the light of day.

I anguished, way back then, that the album which all us fans were looking forward to, never happened, and it was frustrating knowing they were sitting on a tape somewhere, never to be heard.

But after hearing it today, I believe this was the exact same version they had recorded for that record, that never happened. It really sounds like Seven Year Bitch, especially their last official album, Gato Negro.

Then I went back to Dangerous Dan's old website and read his review, and for one of those three shows he lists the names of two of the other songs as "Shining Armor" and "Limb." Sure enough, on this CD are the songs "Shining
Armor" and "On A Limb." There was one other song they played way back then that remains unnamed. I suspect it, too, is also on this CD, possibly "Seattle," which could also have been a late 90's era Seven Year Bitch song. Or maybe "Odd's End." Perhaps both were recorded?

So this is like a new Seven Year Bitch ep, of sorts. How fantastic, after all these years! Too bad there are no live shows.

After a second listening, I think every song except the first two could have been recorded by 7 Year Bitch.

Edit: I finally found the Cistine song on Dan's page. It was called "Breathe Heavy," which is the 9th track. I thought that was the name of it. So between that song and the 7 Year Bitch songs, there was very little new material on the record.
top_profane.title: Seven Year Bitch Songs On This Record!

top_profane.text:
Wow, I raced out today to get this since I'm a huge fan and there was only one CD left!!! So I said, "HOLY CRAP!" and grabbed it! Anyway, I started listening to it in the car and it was sooo good I said, "HOLY CRAP!" since it was so good. I loved the catchy tunes. A week previous I was mezmerized by Mezmerize, but now I'm mesmerized by this sparkeling masterpiece. "HOLY CRAP!" I can't describe this at all. It's too good for words. I mean, I can say that it makes me crap my pants it's so good. Alot of the songs are also like 80's dance songs, but extremely well done. 80's dance music sort of sucks...but this is awsome. Gorillaz are a sweet band. And when the last 2 songs tied together at the end I said, "HOLY CRAP!"

NOTE: THIS ALBUM DOES NOT HAVE EITHER 19-2000 OR CLINT EASTWOOD IN THE TRACK LISTING, THEREFORE IT'S VERY GOOD AND ORIGINAL.

NOTE #2: How many versions can you possibly make of a song?

19-2000 - 4 versions (one crappy one)

Clint Eastwood - 4 versions

Hmm, they're good songs but they're not the second coming if you know what I mean.

Note #3: BUY THIS CD!!!

"HOLY CRAP!"
top_profane.title: Holy Crap!

top_profane.text:
I'm a pretty objective Madge fan here. I dig most of her stuff, but some of her musical outings have been pretty crappy to say the least.

Ray of Light (1998) as an album is hard to surpass...it was an amazing album, one where Madge basically re-claimed her musical relevance. BUT...while definitely not reaching the perfect blend of emotion, lyrical content, and heady music that Ray of Light contained (and in a slightly lesser sense, '89's Like a Prayer album)...Confessions is far better then 2003's American Life, and overall, a stronger album then 2000's Music.

I'll just got track by track, and keep it simple. I rated the tracks on a scale of 1 to 5.

Hung Up-4 stars, this song gets pretty annoying after awhile, and I think she tries too hard to be "catchy" so to speak at times...but overall, it's a strong track, and an excellent choice for a first single.

Get Together-5 stars, I hated this song when I first heard it...the sample made me think..."why must you sample Madge? Rather pathetic." But, after repeated listenings, you realize this song is rather brilliant, beautiful, and that Madge does the sample of "Music Sounds Better With You" justice.

Sorry-3 stars, rumored to be the second single...probably the catchiest track off the album, but after some time, it loses its magic in my opinion. I dig Madge saying "sorry" in a bunch of international languages though...cool touch.

Future Lovers-3 stars...this track needs something extra to make it work. Not even the Donna Summer sample saves this song from mediocrity. Not the worst track on the album, but in this track, the album starts go downhill a little bit.

I Love New York-3 stars, if you listen to this track on her documentary "I'm Going To Tell You a Secret"...it's a funky, rock-inspired track that is destined to be a single. On this album, the track is re-worked to fit the album and they basically ruin the track. The only thing which saves this from getting two stars is the fact that it has "New York" in it, which is the city I live in, and love.

Let It Will Be-4 stars, excellent track...starts out slow, you're wondering when it'll pick up, and about three-fourths of the way into the song...it breaks into a beautiful mix of club sounds, and orchestra. We hear Madge sing about success, the rise/fall of it, etc. in this track (a little tiresome to hear after a bit).

Forbidden Love-1 star, basically, it's crap.

Jump-5 stars...my 2nd favorite track off the album. This is a very old-school Madge track...she sings about her arrival to NYC, her struggle to find herself, support herself...her willingness to take the risks she needed to take in order to make a name for herself. The production is beautiful and layered, but not overtly so. Definitely one of the best.

How High-1 star, crap again.

Isaac-3 stars, the lyrics are a bit trite, but well...Madge can be that way sometimes. The track is simply something that tries hard to be club-sounding and somewhat spiritual at the same time...the marriage doesn't quite come together unfortunately.

Push-1 star, crap once again...ahh Madge...when will you learn?

Like it or Not-4.5 stars, this track is a wonderful way to end the album. Although the lyrics fail at times, there's a brilliant part in the song where she sings, "I'll be the garden/You'll be the snake/All of my fruit is yours to take/Better the devil that you know/Your love for me will grow/Because.../This is who I am..."---it's mellow, but still has a dance beat to it. Excellent track.

Overall...what saves the album from a general 3 star mark is the fact that the standout tracks are truly standout. The filler stuff is so-so, but what keeps the album from truly reaching a valid 5 stars is the fact that the crap on this album is simply bad, bad, bad. Still, the crap on here is actually better then the crap tracks she had on American Life, and thank God for that. Since I can't give the album a 3.8, I'll default to a 4.
top_profane.title: Not her best...but nowhere near her worst.

top_profane.text:
I am a Sting ultra-mega-superfan. Have been for well over 30 years. The track by track review that follows hurts to write.
1- crap
2- crap
3- crap
4- crap
5- crap
6- crap
7- FANTASTIC!
8- crap
9- Great!
10- Very nice

It should be noted, the tracks I enjoyed just so happened to be the ones that Sting wrote himself or co-wrote with ANYONE OTHER THAN HIS BAND!!!

These favored tracks sharply contrast with the others in that they are not overproduced, not loud for loudness' sake, and not full of trite lyrics and the neo new wave jazz-pop chords that Dominic Miller seems to bring. (Don't misunderstand that to mean I dismiss Mr. Miller's talent. I don't at all. I just don't like his writing influence on Sting's melodies.)

One of Sting's many strengths I have enjoyed the most has been his ability to craft quiet introspection into an engaging lyric ("Consider Me Gone," "Fragile," Why Should I Cry For You?," "Fields of Gold," "Hounds of Winter," "A Thousand Years," etc.). It feels like Sting was stuck in a frat party and playing along so as not to be a party pooper (tracks 1-6), went outside for a smoke break (7), walked his way back through the frat house (8) and into his bedroom (9-10) where he passed out.

One can only hope he has a quiet day to himself to really gather his thoughts.

I love you, Sting. That's why I have to be honest with you.
top_profane.title: A fandom of diminishing returns

top_profane.text:
Two things are amazing with DICK HAYMES!

1) That this christmas cd is, as of this writing, discontinued.
2) That DICK HAYMES, to my knowledge, never recorded an actual christmas album in one session (perhaps spread over a couple of days). This is such a crazy thing it might be considered criminal!

What DICK HAYMES has done is record several christmas songs individually throughout the years and this cd is a compilation of them. The songs seem to all be from the 1940's to the early 1950's and so they carry that very old 'nostalgic' christmas sound to them reminding one of the old christmas recordings of BING CROSBY, PERRY COMO, and FRANK SINATRA. And believe me, DICK HAYMES is as ever great a singer as these three gentlemen who gave us some of our best christmas music. That's why I find it so wrong that DICK HAYMES never recorded a christmas album in one session like the others did. It boggles the mind.

Every song is great here and as DICK HAYMES sings them as great or greater than anybody, in general, at least. There are several different backing orchestra's and chorus' on this cd since the songs are from scattered recordings throughout the years. But everything compliments DICK HAYMES tot he finest.

LET IT SNOW and the two versions of WHITE CHRISTMAS are standouts here as are I'VE GOT MY LOVE TO KEEP ME WARM and IT CAME UPON A MIDNIGHT CLEAR. However, I could really name every song because they are just that good.

If you're looking for that old nostalgic christmas sound for the holidays, this is a perfect cd to get and it receives my fullest recommendation.
top_profane.title: Nostalgic Christmas At It's Best

top_profane.text: Goblin Cock is one of those rare treats that can be enjoyed equally by male and female fans. I introduced my girlfriend to Goblin Cock a while back and she was immediately hooked. I'd go as far to say that she is addicted to Goblin Cock, since she constantly brings up the subject. Whether in the morning when she first gets up, or right before bedtime it seems that her day isn't complete without her daily dose of Goblin Cock. I'll admit I'm a fan, but in a more passive way, i.e., if my Girlfriend is insistent on laying a little Goblin Cock action on me, I'll sit back and enjoy the experience. Maybe it's a chick thing, huh? But hey, if it makes the little woman happy, I'm all for it. Maybe you should introduce your sweetie to Goblin Cock. Who knows maybe your woman will love Goblin Cock as much as mine does!
top_profane.title: The Joy of Goblin Cock

top_rant.text:
Adam is the type of artist you either love or hate but cannot ignore...I started watching American Idol again after I saw Kara Dioguardi was to be the new 4th judge, and I'm a fan of her work...her Platinum Weird record with Eurythmic Dave Stewart was great and I love her writing and voice...like a hipper Diane Warren...why did I stop after Season 4?? Because Carrie Underwood won and I hated her then, still do (her latest "Play On" is one of 2009's worst), and could not support a show that supported and developed commercial crap like that, only Kara dragged me back, and I am THRILLED she did, because of Alison Iraheta (I will be buying her record pronto next week), Kris Allen (he was great on the show, but am unimpressed by what I heard so far on his album), and most importantly, ADAM LAMBERT...

Now, I'm not gay, but I knew he was from when he gushingly met Paula at the SF tryouts, and I didn't frikking care (I'm not at all homophobic and I LOVE lots of gay artists and believe in gay marriage)...when he did Queen's "Bohemian Rhapsody", I was sold...then on the show, he blew me away week after week with his adventurous song choices and bold arrangements he did with the band...I knew, based on that, he was a killer songwriter and had impeccable taste...plus his multi-octave range, playing with his image, etc...Adam was everything I hoped Idol would be regularly but they always come up with crap...and I still hope they have a songwriting element in the future...but Adam's covers were bold and damn cool...June Carter Cash's "Ring Of Fire" was awesome & wild, Tears For Fears' "Mad World" was beautiful and cool, Michael Jackson's "Black Or White" was like hard powerpop...all killer...and I've been anticipating his real debut album, "For Your Entertainment", and after months of waiting, WHEW, Adam did NOT disappoint...

THIS RECORD IS EFFING GREAT!!!!!!!! Not only that, it's one of the best albums of 2009, hands down, and definitely the most irresistible...

First off, his voice is like a glittering diamond...with impeccable range, soul, mood, wildness AND restraint...as someone said, he's almost not human...his tone reminds me a LOT of Alan Frew...who?? He's the Scottish, salad-dressing making, lead singer of Canadian band Glass Tiger...remember "Don't Forget Me When I'm Gone" and "Someday" from the mid-80s??? Yes, them, but Adam has more range and taste and coolness. And his record is like made-to-order with my discerning taste in music, from powerpop, 70s British glamrock, to 80s new wave synthpop, to moody electro ballads to Euro bubblegum dance, all with sublimely great songwriting with bullseye hooks that explore both towering highs and crushing lows...the range is like no other record in recent memory.

Out of the 14 songs here, Adam only co-wrote 4, which is OK, and in most cases, a bit disappointing...BUT the others were songs submitted by artists he admires (and who admire him) and honoringly covers...they weren't anonymous songs by Idol/19 boardroom members, not at all, and the beauty of these other songs is that they show who's pen it came from, YET they work in Adam's vision and he was able to put his stamp all over them, PLUS, with the variety in FYE, and the multiple producers, it doesn't sound like patchwork!!! This is completely beyond words...it sounds like the work of one artist who knows exactly who he is...in the wrong hands, it would be a complete mess...

Now, the songs...
1- "Music Again" (10 out of 10 PLUS)...could be song of the year, one of the most irresistible opening songs on ANY album EVER...written by Justin Hawkins, formerly of UK falsetto glam rockers The Darkness, and he plays guitar on the track too...EW called it a cross between Cheap Trick and Supertramp, and I agree, but I would throw some Sweet, Queen & ELO in there too...a totally irresistible keyboard-laced powerpop stomper of the highest order...I cannot stop playing this frikking song, and I'm still not sick of it...it's become a ritual, I have to play this song at least once a day to get my fix...it starts out a drop Van Halen in the opening guitar riff, but it quickly improves and launches like a rocket...and Adam's in AWESOME voice here, espesh the falsetto right before the chorus...it's just FANTASTIC!!! If FYE was just this song, it'd be one of the best of the year...seriously!!!
