2- "For Your Entertainment" (10 out of 10), a fun, catchy-as-frick new wave electro dance track with a killer chorus and great vocal by Adam...great song written and produced by Max Martin associate Dr Luke, who worked with Kelly Clarkson among others...

3- "Whattaya Want From Me" (10 out of 10), the collab here between P!nk and Max Martin (produced by him too), and it shows...AMAZING powerpoppish balladish style of song, with a chorus that just KILLS!!! Just as great is that little riff opener and repeater throughout the song that's really simple but totally effective...leading to that MONSTER chorus...woah!! And it works with Adam like those sequined gloves and chains...

4- "Strut" (7 out of 10), Adam's written collab with Kara DioGuardi, and it's a cool glam rocker, catchy too, but it feels a TAD pedestrian, just a minor quibble...

5- "Soaked" (9 out of 10), Adam's cover of an unused MUSE song that the band gave to him, and woah...completely epic...it works with Adam's theatrical background, and it comes off like a hybrid of a Muse or Radiohead song with a James Bond theme...at first, I was like, eh, but it grew on me immensely...

6- "Surefire Winners" (9 out of 10), another Rob Cavallo production, and it's really catchy...one of the better tracks on the album, but not my absolute favourite...

7- "A Loaded Smile" (8 out of 10), Linda Perry's contribution to the album...she wrote the song, produced it and played most of the instruments, if not all...this does NOT sound like a Linda Perry production...but an electro Radiohead-ish epic slowie, not catchy, but melodic and brooding/moody, and it works with Adam beautifully, as he can tap into his goth side and his theater background equally.

8- "If I Had You" (10 out of 10), WOW, this is one catchy, kickass tune, like Adam's version of "...Baby One More Time"...no surprise it was produced and co-written by Max Martin himself!!!

9- "Pick U Up" (8 out of 10), Adam's collabo with Weezer's Rivers Cuomo, and it sounds like a mindmeld of Adam and Rivers...not as catchy as Rivers' best songs for Weezer, but it's a grower and is a bit off-kilter, plus Adam's laugh at the end is a nice fun touch.

10- "Fever" (10 out of 10), Lady Gaga's contribution, and yep, produced by RedOne as well...it sounds like a Lady Gaga song (it was a demo from 3-4 years ago that she had in her vault that she gave to Adam), and Adam turns it into something like a fun, dancey Scissor Sisters tune, like off their debut record, like "Tits On The Radio"...great!!!

11- "Sleepwalker" (10 out of 10), another WOWEE...this one, suprisingly co-written and produced by OneRepublic's Ryan Tedder, and it's like a great moody New Wave electro ballad with a REALLY cool chorus...melodic, offkilter verses too...Adam OWNS this song (he sounds like A-ha's Morten Harket on the verses!!)...you don't think OneRepublic...but the REALLY winning touch is that KICKASS, searing, shredding guitar solo by Australian guitar whiz ORIANTHI, who played lead guitar in that Michael Jackson biopic "This Is It"!!!!! It would be PERFECT if she tours with Adam and/or plays in his touring band as lead guitarist...that would be a mindblower...a singer like Adam AND a guitarist like Ori??!!?? People's faces would melt right off from the sparks that would be generated and fires started...I can't even imagine...

12- "Aftermath" (8 out of 10) In the wrong hands, this song would be like an overblown, windswept balladish rocker by the likes of Nickelback or Bon Jovi, placed in some cheesy-ass movie...but Adam finds something in this song, which he co-wrote, and makes it work in his style...not an easy feat, but he makes it work, and his voice melts the song too...

13- "Broken Open" (10 out of 10)...aside from "Music Again", Adam's finest moment on the record, and he co-wrote this song too...this song shows what he's truly capable of as a songwriter, and I know we'll get more creative tunes like this...it's a moody, atmospheric, electro ballad with an epic arrangement and heartbreakingly beautiful melody and vocal...he's like in A-ha's Morten Harket's range here, as well as Keane's Tom Chaplin...just beautiful...it's as great as Tears For Fears' "Mad World" as well...

14- "Time For Miracles" (10 out of 10)...in the wrong hands, this would be a REALLY cheesy ballad, but it's really beautiful and moving...it was written by Eleven's 2 leaders, Alain Johannes and the late great Natasha Schneider, and it's truly heartfelt, raised to epic proportions for the cheeseball, ironically funny, movie "2012"...I LOVE it...and the song's verses start off like the Glass Tiger ballad "Rescued By The Arms Of Love" (check it out for yourself), but takes it somewhere greater and more epic, and the way Adam wails on the song and owns it...it's beyond words, and what a way to end the record...then you want to go back and spend YET ANOTHER 54 minutes with FYE and pop on "Music Again"...to paraphrase what Adam sings on that song, "Adam makes me want to play his record again"!!

If David Bowie was a new artist and first fell to Earth in 2009, he would not sound unlike Adam Lambert...now, I'm not religious or anything, but I would say this, thank God for Adam Lambert...he and Lady Gaga are prob the 2 most exciting, vital, creative artists of 2009...and he accomplishes the rare feat with FYE...what would be commercial dreck coming from most artists in a mainstream arena...ESPESH American Idol...Adam turns into memorable ART...in a way, he's compared to Susan Boyle where he can turn anything into gold, written by him or not, and make it sound like Adam Lambert...as producer Rob Cavallo said in a recent article, if he stays true to himself, and combines that with his glam style, soul, writing ability, killer pop sensibilies, rock cred, powerpop elements, wow, he will be one of THE greatest artists ever...

And wow, what a start and platform...one of THE great debut albums of all time...seriously!! Oh yes, the art is beautiful, all the pics...imagine if this gets released on VINYL??!!?? And yep, he's just as gracious in the liner notes as he was on Idol...completely...he has my admiration and respect, 100%...and it's great he doesn't apologize if people are offended by his live antics (as on the AMAs), so he has backbone...all the better...

GO ADAM!!!! Could be best album of the year, 2009!!! From an American Idol contstant??!!?? Actually F yeah!!
top_rant.title: Adam really is effing remarkable...the real deal...if Bowie first fell to Earth in 2009...

top_rant.text:
Ok I love King Diamond's music n I have yet to find an album of his that I don't like.
I have 10 King Diamond albums n 1 Mercyful fate album n even though they aren't all perfect songs I like at least 7 songs on each album so that's far from a bad album regardless!

Abigail 2 is the sequel to the Classic 1987 Abigail. If u don't own Abigail then u r not a true Kd Diamondbanger but if u do then u already know the story.

This album's story is about the spirit twin of Abigail who somehow finds her way to the Mansion where Johnathan Lafey lives n sorrow, mourning over his wife Miriam who died giving birth to Abigail n the first part of this story. The Abigail n this story is 18 years old she is the main protagonist n the original Abigail(baby abigail is know known as Little One, the spirit that lives down n the crypt of the Mansion)
In the 1st Abigail album u learn about O'brian of the Black Horsemen who tries to stop the borth of baby Abigail n tries to warn Johnathan Lafey n his Wife Miriam, because if she is born 18 will become 9 n they will regret Abigail being born. Ok if I lost u by now then that means that u need to get the 1st Abigail cd to really get the whole story, anyways u r here to c if any of the music on this album is good I just wanta give u a lil Insight into the story so far, its very convoluted but if u have the 2 albums then u will get it.

Let's begin

Spare this Life- O'Brian of the black horsemen tells u about sparing the life of 18 year old Abigail because she is the spirit twin of his stillborn half sister Little One(confusing? Huh)

The Storm-I give this a 5 outta 5, The guitars r perfect n this track they follow everything that Abigail does while the drums kick n as soon as she gets lost n the forest. Abigail is being chased by something so she makes a run for it, runnin n runnin til she stops at the Mansion...

Mansion In Sorrow- 5 outta 5, The chorus is really good here, Abigail arrives at the gates n suddenly Little One appears n goes straight through her body(this is the situation depicted on the cover of the album) the drums pick up( 1 note I really like the drummer I think this is the best drummer Kd has had since Mickey Dee) suddenly the door opens n out comes the caretaker Brandon Henry who invites Abigail into the Mansion. She enters a shrine with 18 Portraits hanging she goes upstairs where she sees Johnathan

Miriam- 5 outta 5. Ok I know 2 n a row? U bet, this is my second favorite song. The way it starts is so sick the bass n drums work together perfectly, Abigail wanders about a dark setting then Johnathan(who now is confined in a wheelchair) tells her to stand by the fireplace, He sees Miriam in Abigail so he approaches n Asks her who she is, Abigail tells him n he can't believe the resemblance so he asks her if he can call her Miriam...she agrees, but she has no idea what she's n for

Little One-5 outta 5. This song is an important one, its about Little One u find out that she has been searching for her mother every night throughout the Mansion.Abigail hears Little One crying. "Mommy, Mommy" So she goes down the stairs the stairs n finds Little One who tells Abigail that she is her spitit twin from years ago. Abigail remembers the name Johnathan from back then when she used to sneak outta bed when she was 6 n listen to stories about Johnathan n Little One. Abigail has made up her mind she is goin to stay in the Mansion to help Little One find her Mommy....

Slippery Stairs-5 outta 5, why u ask?, man, just listen to this sick situation! Ok first of all the Slippery Stairs are a very important element in both Abigail albums it is here where Johnathan saw Miriam possed by Little One in the 1st Abigail. I love how the guitars sound on this one n then mid break with heavy bass to let the drums play out n listen in on how Brandon Henry tells Abigail about the crypt n about Little one, she should never go down there at night where the Slippery Stairs are...But on the second day...Johnathan invites Abigail into his room,because the time is right to produce an heir! If u never heard this before I won't ruin it for u, just say Johnathan is a sick Bastard!!!

The Crypt-5 outta 5 Wow ! Abigail is pissed n is seeking revenge,so while Johnathan sleeps she goes down into the crypt. The bass is perfect here it gives u the sense that Abigail has had enough of this place after what happened. She finds a coffin with the name ABIGAIL(Little One) ahe opens it to find a mummified infant wearing a sharp necklace with wings( a nod to an earlier KD album "The Eye") she takes it n swears revenge on anybody who gets in her way. Brandon Henry sees Abigail n she cuts his throat with the necklace

Broken Glass-5 outta 5 By now u feel Abigails pain KD's vocals paint the picture perfectly for this one, while u hear the glass breaking n u wonder what's that for? All I say is Dinner is Served!!!

More than Pain- 5 outta 5 Ha Ha Ha Johnathan gets what he deserves here, he is so in love with his new Miriam that he has no clue what he's in for n this is just the beginning of Abigail's Revenge. A slow sick Song perfect guitars it gives u the sense that death is nearing. The best part is KD's maniacal laughter

The Wheelchair-once again 5 outta 5! Here we go My Favorite Track!!! Everything is perfect on this, the drums never let up n the guitars mimic everything Abigail does here. Torturing Johnathan while he slowly dies in his wheelchair. Abigail has Little One's spirit inside of her n they both are seekin revenge against him! They want to hear him scream for his life, The guitars let u know that Abigail has snapped n is goin to show no mercy! So what's next...

Spirits-5 outta 5 KD's haunting opening vocal "Johnathan is dead" Finally Abigail n Little One get what they wanted but at what cost...Abigail is so fascinated by the burning body of Johnathan that she doesn't care that she catches on fire n soon the whole Mansion is up in flames! Spirits are in the air the perfect chorus All the spitits that lived in this god forsaken mansion are now free!! Abigail is consumed happily by the flames, but... she forgets 1 thing...Little One is already a spirit she can't cross over to the other side!

Mommy- 5 outta 5 KD narrates the outcome of Little One. She wanders about the Mansion searching for her Mommy for eternity. KD tells you about the Countess(In the 1st Abigail you learn all about her) Will Little One ever find her Mommy???

Sorry Dear- The outro, Little One cries for her Mommy forever begging to go home n then a voice says
SORRY DEAR!!!!

There you have it a perfect sequel to a classic!!! This album is too good to put down n its one of my all time favorites from KD! If you are just getting into KD then this is not a good place to start, because you won't know have of the story, you will have a hard time trying to figure out the plot, So I reccomend getting the 1987 Classic Abigail. If you don't really care for the story n want this album by all means get it! Its a great addition to any MetalHead's collection Filled with memorable riffs n Sick bass lines top notch Vocals
KD music is mostly highpitched wails but they are not forced like a Crap Band from the 80's they are appropriate for this kind of Metal and you will learn to Love his voice. Its not an aqquired taste, it grows on you from the very first listen you will realize What A true Metal Singer is supposed to sound like!!!

Hope you find my review helpful, its a bit long, because I focused too much on the story, but that was important

Give King Diamond a try n you just might find your new favorite Metal band n then wonder to yourself
Where the hell have I been? I should have been listening to KD a long time ago!!!!
top_rant.title: Kd's sequel to the classic!!

top_rant.text:
Alright, this has got to be one of the best albums I have ever listen too. Avenged Sevefold completely turns the other way from their first album (hey I made a joke! course, you won't get it if you don't know of their first album) and finishes the work "Waking the Fallen" started. While "Waking the Fallen was good and contained some good songs that didn't contain much screaming in it, "City Of Evil" completely blows it out of the water and the only screaming you will hear will be fore intros like "Beast And The Harlet" or near the end of "Blinded in Chains", both which are awesome songs.
I first got the idea to pick up this album after playing "Beast and the Harlet" on guitar hero 2, and I was awestruck. After picking up "City Of Evil," my introduction to Avenged Sevenfold, it took me a while to get used to this new sound. At the time, I was intrested in punk bands like Fall Out boy, but it was this album that got me leaning over to the "heavier" side of music, eventually to Iron Maiden somehow...I don't something about this album has an 80's feel to me. While this album may take some time for you like, as with the case with me, and it may not please fans of "Sounding of the seventh trumpet", believe me, once you catch on, you will LOVE IT. So here's my review:

Track 1.) "Beast And The Harlet" (rating- 10 out of 10-AWESOME)
Hey, this sets the mood nicely. If you haven't yet experienced this song in gh2, prepare to band your head. It is perhaps the best song on this album, which is saying something, because, to be frank, nearly all the songs on this album are in the running to be best on this album! That's how good this album is! Anyway, "Beast And The Harlet" is surely entertaining and one of Avenged Sevenfolds best songs! Great listen!

Track 2.) "Burn It Down" ( Rating- 8.5 out of 10- awesome song)
Ok, while you may love "Beast and the harlet" and have it tatoo'ed on your back already even though it's only been about 5 minutes after you purchased the cd, be prepared for something a little different, but not much. Now, "Burn It Down" is one of those songs on here that takes some time to like. But believe me, once you give it a chance, you will love it. It's another great song! While it may not want you to pick up a torch and burn miniture models of houses to ashes as with "Beast and the Harlet," it's still going to give you that rush as the first track did, just in a little smaller dose. Great Listen too!

Track 3.) "Blinded in Chains" (rating- 9.5- Awesome song!)

Here we have another song that grows on you over time. As soon as you listen to it a few times, you realize how good this song it is! One of the best songs on this album! However, sometimes I feel as if the cuss words mentioned here ruin the song a bit, but then I realized "who cares, it's a great song!" Won't dissapoint and yet another note worthy song!

Track 4.) "Bat Country" (Rating- 10 out of 10- AWESOME!)

If you haven't seen the music video to this when it was literally fired from a machine gun from MTV and VH1 onto your tv screens when this album came out, be prepared for another song that takes some time to appreciate if you are unfamilar to this sort of sound( sort of like a mixture of punk lyrics and heavy guitar riffs and whatnot!). But soon, you realize this is the best song or one of the best songs on this album! It's packed with energy like "Beast and the harlet", has an amazing solo like "The Beast and the harlet," paints a picture like, once again, "the beast and the harlet", yet has a sound that belongs to its self and its self alone...like "the beast and th-"...you know...AWESOME SONG!!!

Track 5.) "Trash and Scatterered" ( Rating- 9 out of 10- Awesome song!)

Here we have, yet another great song! This song leans more to the "Burn it down" side than "Blinded in chains," but soon you may find yourself listening to this song regularly as soon as you open up to it. Once again, another great song on this epic album!

Track 6.) "Seize the Day" (Rating- 8.5 out of 10- great slow paced song)

Whoa! What the hell happened! Everything just slowed down and that guys voice became milky smooth all of a sudden! Ahh well, it's a good song...

Now, "Seize the Day" may not be Avenged Sevenfolds' best "sweet 'n slow" song, but it's still good. In fact, it's one of the few slow paced songs I actually like, and that's saying something! In fact, gernally, I hate all these slow songs and all this new stuff today like rascal flats and this r&b crap. Worthy listen to, and calms you down from the other 5 action packed song.

Track 7.) "Sidewinder" (Rating- 9.5 out of 10- awesome song!)

Here we have an intresting song that changes direction everynow and then. One moment, you think it's gona go right, but then goes in the opposite direction while saying "left is cooler, right can burn in hell for all I care!"...quite unpredictable, one moment following a climbing riff, this goes into this sidewinding sort of tune and the mood from the part before changes. The moderate tempo in the beginning, became fast paced, slowed down, and moderate. Still, just because it takes you on this roller coaster that not just goes up and down but left, right, diagonal right, hell even backwards, doesn't mean it's a great song. Yet another noteworthy song on here that's is a great listen too!

Track 8.) "The Wicked End" (rating- 8.5 of 10- great song)

Here we have another song that's intresting and great listen too. Quite different then what you normally hear I bet, but once again, I really can't dis this song. What can I say, it's a great song! Like "Sidewinder," you'll find some parts changing rapidly, but don't get me wrong, it's a great song.

Track 9.) "Strength Of The World" (rating- 10 out of 10- AWESOME!)

Alright, get some snacks and prepare for a dozy. Here we have an epic at about 9:15 and tells a story. It's quite different from the other songs on this album, but it's one of the best songs on here. In fact, it's my personal favorite! It's just so...epic! Listen to it, go on...don't be afraid, it's a song of awesomeness!
However, I don't mean to relate this to anything like rhasody or follow those lines, Avenged Sevenfold contains it's own special flare, leaves some parts missing, and allows the listener to fill in the gaps themselves, which frankly, works out well = ).

Track 10.) "Betrayed" (rating- 8.5 out of 10-good song!)

Here we have yet another...again...good song! While you may not be listening to this one and prefering to play the hell out of Bat Country or something, this song is still a great listen and worth your time.

Track 11.) "M.I.A." (rating- 10 out of 10- AWESOME!)

Ahh, here we go, a perfect way to close. This song starts up all quite with a simple yet pretty guitar part...you just know all hell is about to break lose, don't ya? While hell might not come roaring out of the gates, a perfect blend of harmony and melody does! This is, again, another one of Avenged Sevenfolds best songs. I consider it underated as it's quite overshadowed by "Beast and the Harlet" and "Almost Easy" from the newer album these days, but without out a doubt, it is one of Avenged Sevenfolds best. Turn it on, play it, learn it, love it!

Overall Review- See all those negative posts giving this album a 1 star rating? Ignore those, they are just fans of the first and second album who are taking this change in the right direction a little too hard. While "Waking the Fallen" contained some great songs yes, I can safely say "City Of Evil" kills it as nearly every song on here is...well, great! This is possibly Avenged Sevenfolds best album to date and well worth more than your 15 bucks so get it before it becomes some lost rare like Metallica's "Master Of Puppets" album at my wal-mart (keyword- *my*, because my wal-mart sucks and hardly restocks on cd's at all =( ) .

All and all, this album is a MUST BUY!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

Rating- 10.5 out of 10
top_rant.title: ~A Turn In The Right Direction~ possibly A7X's best album~

top_rant.text:
Ok, so this is the 4th album by Alternative Metal band Red. Well, I hope they're still Alternative Metal...this album seems to be moving away from the metal area and more towards rock; not even hard rock, just straight rock. This isn't bad, but it isn't what I'd like to see the band do. I think they could actually do more if they turned around and went into metal rather than riding the border of rock and metal. However, I will say that I don't think the band did anything very different this time around. I personally put all the blame for this album onto Howard Benson, the producer. He has a reputation for ruining bands and I'm afraid that he's ruined a talented band's 4th outing. Anyway, about the band itself, Mike Barnes did a nice job on vocals for this album. He sings very well on this one (ok...as well as he can around the production). Anthony did a solid job on the guitars here, Randy plays some good bass lines, and Joe has his moments. There are some pretty good moments on this album. A few of these moments are actually some of the best of Red's career. And then there are some decent moments (I feel that they would've been better if Howard wasn't the producer), and then there are a few songs that I just think are ok, but turned out to be TERRIBLE (thanks Howard). Just saying, it's a VERY mixed album.

Music:

1. Release the Panic- This is one of the best moments on the album. This is also one of Red's heaviest songs ever. It's got a very eerie feel to it and it's full of gritty vocals. It may lack orchestrations, but I like this song a lot! This is why Red is considered Alternative Metal; They can deliver some pretty freaking heavy tunes. The drums are good, but I personally think Joe should try playing more aggressively on songs like this. It'd suit the mood so much better! The guitar part is definitely doing some shifts! It starts off doing some interesting chords and does an eerie melody and then goes back to chords. Personally, this song is GREAT, but it's too short. I feel like they could've done more things in the middle to spice up the music (Thanks again Howard Benson!). Good song though, even if it is short.

2. Perfect Life- This is the main single from the album and it's alright. Lyrically, it's a brilliant song, but musically, I find it lacking the intensity shown on the previous track as well as previous singles. This one is closer to rock, but it still does rock. This isn't a song I'd listen to often, but I'd still listen to it. It does have some good guitar work in it, but Joe's drumming is what gives it more of a rock feel, to my ears anyway. I will say that even if I don't think it's a favorite song, it's still WAY TOO SHORT! Even as a single, 2:51 is way too short for an Alternative Metal or Metal song! The average song length in my library is 5:10! I personally think that it's ridiculous! This album is 12 short songs like this and it's only about 38 minutes. I have albums with 9 songs on them that are an hour long! This song is good, but Howard Benson is annoying. Notice how Red made mostly 4 minute songs in the past. Now the longest song on this album is 4:17. Howard makes bands loose their quality. This is a decent single, but it's not as good as it could be.

3. Die for You- Ok, really? Just really? Just looking at the length, I know that this won't be good; 2:46. Within the first 30 seconds, it sounds like a pop song that you'd hear on the radio. This is the Howard Benson special; take a band that makes good, heavy, purposeful music and then turn them into pop acts. Obviously, I hate this song. As catchy as it may be, I think it's a disgrace to Red's name. Everything they stood for is gone. The only plus of this song is a short like 3 second guitar solo. But I honestly think this song is an embarrassment for this band.

4. Damage- Ok, I see lots of people hating on this song. This song is one of the few good moments on this disc. It may be slow, but at least it's still heavy like the Red of old. People are comparing this to Nu Metal like Disturbed? I can see a little bit of the the comparison, but if this was Nu Metal, there'd be MUCH more synth work in it. The synths in here act as a backing thing, but in Nu Metal, they'd be more upfront. I have to say that while this isn't what I'd expect from Red, it's a good song. It's heavy, but it's good. This one is a very good moment for Red on this album. It shows signs of life, but it does create a sense of polarity to the album. It's sandwiched in between two of the worst and most pop-sounding songs on the album! But regardless of its position on this album, this is one of my favorites on this album! Good song! Great riff, too!!!

5. Same Disease- Ok, this is the worst one here. No doubt. The guitar part at the beginning sounds promising, actually, but after the drums and vocals come in, it's a DISASTER!!! This is the one song that I can't blame Howard Benson for! This one is actually very poorly written. The lyrics are very pop-sounding, very repetitive, and aren't even within 2 levels of the quality we're used to getting from this band. Radio-sounding or not, the music is actually decent on this one! But the lyrics just totally SPOIL it! I don't even know what they were aiming for with this one, but I don't like it.

6. Hold Me Now- This is a ballad (as if you couldn't tell from the title) and it has a very modern pop sound to it. I think that the pop sound is due to the producer on this one (Nice one Howard!), but past the production, I'd say this would be where Red finally starts to show that they are still the same band. This has a pop sound to it, but I feel like if they had a different producer, it'd sound more like Pieces or Start Again. But I'm not going to complain about the song it is. It's a decent track, but it's not one I'd like to listen to frequently. Better than the preceding one by a light year though!

7. If We Only- This is probably the song that shows Red is still alive! This starts off with a VERY driving riff and a heavy drum part and then slows into a brooding verse with much orchestration. One reviewer said the orchestration resembles Symphony X more than old Red's orchestration. I agree to an extent, but I'm thinking more Kamelot, but either or. I can actually say that this song has Joe's best drum work for the album! Great drumming and I also think it's just a great song! Why they didn't make the rest of the album with this song's quality is a mystery. This song honestly ranks with their old stuff and I'd say this is one of their top 10 best tracks! Great song!!! I hope there's more like this in the future!

8. So Far Away- Ok, I thought this was their worst ballad (until I heard track 12 of this album). Anyway, this has good music! Howard added some pop elements, but they don't get in the way like before. However, this song suffers like track 5; lyrics are absolute crap! The chorus is just odd. And this isn't the type of ballad I'd expect from Red and I don't like it. It's just not the Red I know and I will never accept a Red that makes ballads like this. I'm just not feeling the emotion they are trying to transfer to the listener. I hear the emotion in the vocals, but it's just so badly written that I don't feel it. Sorry Red, but this one and track 5 are your faults. Howard didn't mess up this track either.

9. Glass House- This song has more of a pop-rock sound, but I do think that this song is another good moment for this album. There are orchestrations on this song and I actually think Joe did a decent job drumming here for what the song is. The opening is a pop sound, but the chorus has a more polished version of the sound we're used to. This one is another highlight for sure. Mike's singing here is very good and the band actually locks in on this one! The effects and extra instruments add to this song instead of taking away from it! The orchestrations on this one aren't like Red's usual orchestrations, but they aren't as extreme as they were on If We Only. Overall, this is a great song, and I appreciate it!

10. The Moment We Come Alive- This song is good. There are some pop elements in here, but this one does work out well. This is the Red we've known all along but there are pop elements in there. There's no orchestra, but this song doesn't need it. A piano part fills in for it. I do think that they did well on this song. I don't have any complaints on this song. It actually gives you a sense of hope and the music is very well done! And actually, there are some orchestrations in the bridge, but they are harder to hear. I just noticed them as I was listening to it now. This is a good song and I think this would be the best way to close the album. Even with the bonus tracks.

Bonus Tracks
*Note: I don't care about the remixes at all so I'm not even going to talk about them.

11. Love Will Leave a Mark- This song should honestly be on the real album and then they shouldn't have had a deluxe edition. This one is heavy and in your face. Judging by the name, I thought it would be another ballad, but I was surprised to find a song that is heavy and that carries on the spirit of what we're used to hearing! It has that lack of an orchestra, but oh well! This song is GREAT!!! I still think that this should be on the real album! This is the only bonus track that is worth having. It isn't as good as the title track or If We Only, but it's right there with them! This song is more like Hide than like the rest of the songs on this album, but needless to say, it's GOOD!!!

12. As You Go- This song is Howard Benson GARBAGE! I know that Howard Benson produces crap, but this is a new low even for him! This song is almost a radio ballad. Every thing except Mike sounds electronic. It sounds fake other than Mike Barnes's voice. I just don't like this song and I don't like listening to it. It's a poor track.

Verdict- I know that I pretty much trashed half of the songs on the album, but you will hear some good in the album. I can recommend it to those who like radio music and to those who know that it's not the same Red we love. I can't recommend it to metal fans though. Not the whole album. The only song that would please fans like myself looking for some Red to headbang to would be If We Only. While Damage and the title track are heavy, it wouldn't please most metal fans like Red's old stuff. So it's all up to you. I personally think the bad on this album far outweighs the good. If you like Bon Jovi, you know the bad that Howard Benson does. Here it is at it's worst.
top_rant.title: Too Many Filler Tracks!

top_rant.text:
I don't know why His Master's Voice made this recording in the first place. After all Menuhin had made a stereo recording of Beethoven's Violin Concerto in 1960 with the Vienna Philharmonic under Constantin Silvestri, in excellent stereo sound and a publication which had been received with accolades in The Gramophone ([[ASIN:B00005UUOE Beethoven: Violin Concerto / Romances No. 1 & 2]]). So why re-do it six years later? And I think, in those pre-Naxos years, they even PAID the performers that they recorded, not just royalties on sales, but upfront, and hefty sums too when they were stars like these. Maybe EMI (the owning company of HMV) was hoping to bank on two of their greatest stars and on the universal success of Klemperer's complete Beethoven symphonies, recorded in the late 1950s to early 1960s. And Klemp' had never recorded Beethoven's Violin Concerto (he would do the complete Piano Concertos a year later with Barenboim - and there are no recordings left of him, studio or live, in the Triple Concerto).

If so, it misfired. The recording was generally received with more than reluctance, and to this day it appears to be always dismissed in favor of Menuhin's earlier versions, especially those with Furtwängler, the more famous one with the Philharmonia Orchestra from 1953 ([[ASIN:B000002SG3 Beethoven, Mendelssohn: Violin Concertos (Violin Concerto in D Major, Op. 61 / Violin Concerto in E Minor, Op. 64)]]) and, now that it has been reissued and is easily accessible on CD, their earlier recording from Lucerne in 1947 ([[ASIN:B000003XKA Beethoven: Violin Concerto; Romances Nos. 1 & 2]]).

The review of the original release published in the Gramophone issue of November 1966 by Edward Greenfield is very typical and deserves to be reproduced (and if you find that it strangely resembles the review posted by Colloredo of Caracas, don't infer that Edward Greenfield is still alive and living in Venezuela, but rather that Colloredo helped himself in the Gramophone electronic archive without citing his source):

"The point is that the special individual qualities of Menuhin and Furtwängler matched one another. Each brought out the best in the other, for their basic approach was identical, profoundly romantic and expressive but with an inner spiritual strength that was more elusive in emerging. I was hoping that the equally interesting combination of Menuhin and Klemperer would precipitate equally fine results, but I am afraid there is nothing like the same marriage of minds. One might have hoped that Menuhin's spontaneous imagination would lighten the impact of Klemperer and that Klemperer would give extra weight to Menuhin, but it has not worked that way. Admittedly Menuhin seems a degree or two more relaxed than he did in the Silvestri version made in Vienna, and the intonation is unquestionably better, but in neither case does that say very much. Though naturally there are many points of detail to admire, the overall impression is very much on the heavy side. One finds oneself objecting to the slow speed for the first movement, only to find that the basic tempo of both the earlier versions is a degree slower still. The fault lies here, I think, mainly with Klemperer, and no passage illustrates this more clearly than the beginning of the first movement coda after the cadenza. The pizzicato accompanying chords sound heavy not because they are loud or weighty but simply because they drag after the beat and the soloist seems to be pulling the players along.

In the slow movement Menuhin finds the mood of relaxation that was so conspicuously lacking in the Silvestri version, but go back to the Furtwängler and each phrase has a rapt sense of magic, of being created spontaneously in a way that makes the new version seem a little ordinary. In the finale the Silvestri version takes a turn for the better with Menuhin gaining confidence and a genuine spring in the rhythm. The new one is the slowest of the three, and characteristically Klemperer refuses to put a spring in the rhythm at all, so that at first it seems dull after the other two. But taken on its won it has a good bucolic solidity, and the middle D minor episode is firmer and stronger than in either of the previous versions. And interesting Klemperer point in the coda: instead of playing the emphatic crotchet plus quaver chordes at an even volume, he plays the last two comparatively quietly to allow the soloist's rising figure to come trhough."

And upon the first CD reissue, on EMI's mid-price Studio collection, in August 1985, Ivan March:

"Sir Yehudi Menuhin's recording of Beethoven's Violin Concerto dates from a decade earlier (1966) and does not show this artist at his best. Clearly there was no symbiosis in his partnership with Klemperer and when the slolo playing is sometimes rough in timbre, to the point of edginess, this is difficult to enjoy. The sound is brightly lit and not flattering. As it happens Menuhin's two earlier recordings have both been recently reissued. The 1954 mono performance with Furtwängler has been given CD status and was warmly welcomed by MEO, but more comparable in price is the Eminence reissue of the 1960 stereo version with a splendid accompaniment from the Vienna Philharmonic under Silvestri, which is in every way preferable to the later account".

Yet I find this Menuhin-Klemperer version much better than its reputation.

First, the notion that Klemperer conducts slow, let alone TOO slow, must be seriously qualified. It is true that he takes slightly longer to reach the first movement cadenza than Furtwängler in 1953 (20:12 vs 19:49) or 1947 in Lucerne (19:44) and significantly longer than Silvestri (19:14). But in fact, for the first part of the first movement, up until the re-entry of the violin on its opening rising motive, Klemperer's timing is EXACTLY the same as Furtwängler's: 10:40 in Lucerne, 10:50 with the Philharmonia, 10:50 with Klemperer. So it's only thereafter that Furwängler, in typical Furtwängler fashion, tends to step up his tempo, while Klemperer, in typical Klemperer fashion, remains steady as a rock.

Greenfeld's review seems serious enough, but when you listen to the recordings you realize that much of it is crap. "The basic tempo of both the earlier versions is a degree slower still"? (and here Greenfield is referring, of course, to Furtwängler 1953 and Silvestri 1960, not to Furtwängler 1947). No it's not! Basic tempo of Furtwängler 1953 was exactly the same (he reached the end of the orchestral introduction in 3:22, to Klemperer's... 3:20), and basic tempo of Silvestri in 1960 was significantly faster (though not really in the overture, which he conducted in 3:17). Precisely, one of the main differences between the Silvestri and the Klemperer version, is that the first remains very strict in tempo even in the more lyrical passages and Menuhin deploys his lyricism within the discipline of that strict tempo, while the other takes the time to relax and let the violin melodies soar more freely (that's what Greenfield alludes to when he writes, with justification, that "Menuhin seems a degree or two more relaxed than he did in the Silvestri version made in Vienna").

Not that this invalidates Greenfield's contention that, overall, Klemperer conducts a slower version; in fact, it reinforces the notion. That said, such expansive tempos may have seemed more shocking to some ears in 1966 than they do now. Prior to Klemperer, very few recordings had crossed the 20-minute threshold to reach the cadenza. Schneiderhan and Van Kempen were just there in 1953 (20:02), Oistrakh was way over with Cluytens in 1958 (20:42), Ferras-Sargent took 20:10 in 1959, Kogan-Silvetri 20:21 the same year, and Suk-Konwitschny had broken the 21-minute barrier in 1962 (21:10). But Menuhin-Klemperer seemed to have opened the dams. Came Ferras-Karajan in 1967 (21:07), Suk-Boult in 1970 (21:22), Zukerman-Barenboim in 1977 (20:35). When the famed version of Perlman-Guilini came out in 1981, it was hailed in The Gramophone ("no finer version available", they said - which of course left all those that were no more available) and the reviewer didn't complain that its first movement was too slow, although they reached the cadenza in 20:17. By the way that reviewer was Edward Greenfield.

Of course there is more to the feeling of slowness than just timing. But precisely, the claim against Klemperer and his purported slowness leaves entirely aside the very distinctive feature of his art of conducting, which makes him one of the greats of the 20th century: not just the deliberation, but the massive power he invests in the orchestra. And that's one massive advantage over Furtwängler's 1953 version, where by dint of the sonics but also the interpretation the orchestral tutti are much mellower. I hear none of the heaviness pointed by Greenfeld, because other than investing the tutti with massive power, Klemperer always keeps the phrasings crisp and accented. To me this doesn't sound "slow" or "heavy", it sounds immensely powerful.

But how could Greenfeld have heard that? At the end of his review of the Klemperer version, he confesses that he's "only heard the mono as yet"! Jesus!!!! And you are going to call a Rembrandt a fake based on a black and white photo??? Critics...

In fact, there are many advantages of this first movement with Klemperer over the two previous ones: over Furtwängler 1953, the stereo sonics and the sense of orchestral power; over Silvestri, the greater flexibility of tempo and greater songfulness in the lyrical passages. And to that, you may add that the 1966 sonics are an improvement, in clarity in impact, over even those of 1960.

And that thing of Greenfield with the pizzicati after the cadenza (23:09): frankly, I don't know what he is talking about, what he's heard or thought he'd heard - maybe again an effect of listening to the mono recording? There's no dragging of Klemperer here, there is the customary slow and meditative going out of the cadenza, and on the contrary as soon as the bassoon re-enters at 23:51, Klemperer adopts a rather flowing tempo where others prefer to retain the very expansive mood of meditativeness.

More crap from Greenfield: that Menuhin's "intonation is unquestionably better" than in 1960 with Silvestri. Obviously Ivan March heard the opposite. To my ears they are both wrong. Certainly the tone is not luminous as it was in 1947, there are a few edgy moments verging on wiriness in the runs and double stops of the Finale, Menuhin enters in Kreisler's first movement cadenza with more gentleness and less vibrancy than in 1960 (although this may be only the effect of a slightly more distant microphone placement), but you can find the same kind of - truly minuscule - tonal blemishes already in 1960 and even in 1953, and they don't add up to anything near the painful listening experience described by March. In fact, looking for tonal differences or trying to check on some I think I've heard, I am jumping from one version to the others, and the more carefully I listen and compare, the more, like the legendary desert mirage, they seem to elude me. Truth is, contrary to reputation (and sometimes fact), there is NO notable tonal deterioration between 1953, 1960 and 1966. The only implicit pointer that there might have been some technical deterioration in the 1966 Menuhin is that he plays a much-abridged version of the Kreisler 3rd movement cadenza, a decision for which I can find no obvious rationale: certainly not timing constraints of an LP side: even with the Cadenza played complete, Side B wouldn't have been longer than side A. This is not the greatest and purest violin tone in the world, but it is nothing to rant about and more than acceptable (I find the much-hailed Szigeti much more painful to hear in his 1947 recording with Bruno Walter, to say nothing of his 1961 remake with Dorati), and Menuhin deploys his customary lyricism and songfulness in the upper reaches.

"In the slow movement Menuhin finds the mood of relaxation that was so conspicuously lacking in the Silvestri version"? Crap again. With Klemperer the slow movement takes 10:09 to unfold (up to the cadential flourish leading to the Finale), and it was 10:13 with Silvestri, both find the very same "mood of relaxation", which is in fact a return to the way Menuhin did it with Furtwängler in 1947 (10:37). In fact Silvestri is even slightly MORE spacious than Klemperer in the first part of the movement, it is only with the movingly hushed passage with "raindrop" pzzicatti accompaniment (reached in 6:00 to Silvestri's 6:16) that Menuhin-Klemperer are now slightly more held-back. It is in 1953 with Furtwängler again that Menuhin strayed from that raptly held-back unfolding, with a more flowing and not so deeply-felt 9:23 duration. That's one of the main reasons to prefer Menuhin-Furtwängler 1947 over 1953. 1947 is possibly Menuhin's most beautiful and intense Larghetto, but all is relative: those with Silvestri and Klemperer are beautiful in their own right. The only thing you can say against Klemperer is that there is some artificial spotlighting of the woodwinds: the clarinet at 1:13 sounds as if he were seated next to the soloist. Beautiful, but not realistic.

I can't fault Greenfield on his assertion that Klemperer's Finale is the slowest of the three; in fact, it is, up to the cadenza, the slowest version I've ever encountered, and if the movement's total timing doesn't give that impression, it is only because, as already mentioned, Menuhin plays a vastly abridged version of Kreisler's cadenza. But still, Greenfield WAS crap when he claimed that there, the Silvestri version took "a turn for the better with Menuhin gaining confidence and a genuine spring in the rhythm". It is the opposite in fact, and before Klemperer, Menuhin's version with Silvestri was the second slowest ever that I had encountered after the one of Schneiderhan with Van Kempen in 1953. Even compared to the expansive and mellow Furtwängler in 1953, it was sucked of any spirit of dance, and was the most exceptionable part of the 1960 recording. In fact, I find the still slower Finale with Klemperer preferable: first, because it is, precisely, even more radical: as long as you are going to have the slowest ketchup in the world, have it REALLY the slowest. Second, because that very radicalism of approach serves character much better. Of the Silvestri version, I wrote that "the bounce that Menuhin manages to retain in his phrasings, his sweet lyricism in the more lyrical passages and the vigor of the orchestral outbursts are no real substitute" to the lost siprit of dance. With Klemperer, the massive power, the gruff and clumsy vigor ARE interesting substitutes. It calls for the same kind of comment that I made already about Klemperer's Finale of the Emperor Concerto with Barenboim, or the Scherzo and Finale of his Eroica: it evokes images of one of the hippos of Fantasia musingly hop-scotching, followed by the rest of the posse. It might have been impossibly elephantine and plodding - and it probably is, in fact - yet I don't find it "dull" at all. Whatever you think, it is unique, the Finale of the Violin Concerto as you've never heard it.

I don't know why HMV made this recording in the first place, but I know why they were right to do it: because, where many performers, when they re-record a work, are happy to offer exactly the same interpretation as before (Heifetz or Grumiaux come to mind), Menuhin here offers one that is very different from his previous ones, and one that is highly valuable to boot. In all, I find that the comparison of Menuhin's various recordings, and the observation of how different they are, paints an interesting and unexpected image of the violinist. Not one to impose, like Heifetz or Grumiaux, HIS unchanging views on his different conducting partners, but rather one ready and eager to blend into THEIR views. If the versions with Furtwängler are so Furtwänglerian, if the first movement with Silvestri is so strict in tempo and so expansive and massively powerful with Klemperer and if the Finale with Klemperer sounds so much like Klemperer's other Beethoven, it seems to be because Menuhin arrived at the recording sessions and said, "okay Wilhelm, okay Constantin, okay Otto, how would you like to conduct it? It'll be fine for me, I'll be happy to fit in, and say what I wish to say within your conception, rather than against it". That's a good definition of "the meeting of minds", and of making music together. As long as shelve space permits, I am not parting with this one, and if things get really bad, it's the one I'll keep with Lucerne 1947. Now if things get REALLY bad....

Menuhin-Klemperer's recording was first reissued on EMI's mid-price Studio collection in 1985, with no complement, [[ASIN:B00000DOGQ Beethoven: Violin Concerto in D Major, Op. 61]]. This edition here is from 1992 but it uses the same 1985 transfer. It adds the same Two Violin Romances, recorded with the Philharmonia Orchestra under John Pritchard in 1960, that EMI has also used as a complement to the Silvestri-conducted Concerto in the edition mentioned in the first paragraph, and that way all tastes and preferences are met. There, Menuhin illustrates, with Pritchard as he did already with Furtwängler in 1953 (a recording reissued by Testament as a complement to their 1947 Lucerne Concerto), an approach that is way too solemn and sentimental for the music's own good, and even more in Op. 50 than in Op. 40. Once you've heard Kremer and Harnoncourt's way more flowing approach, there's no turning back ([[ASIN:B000000SG9 Ludwig van Beethoven: Violin Concerto in D major, Op. 61 / Romance in G major, Op. 40 / Romance in F major, Op. 50 - Gidon Kremer / Chamber Orchestra of Europe / Nikolaus Harnoncourt]]). The Romances offer a welcome complement for the Menuhin completist, but it is the Klemperer-conducted Concerto that warrants purchase, with or without complement.

*******************************************************************
And a post-script from August 17: mp3 buyers, BEWARE! First of all, the mp3 download page to which this CD is linked is credited as Menuhin's version with Silvestri, not Klemperer. And anyway, the timings of the three tracks makes it INCOMPATIBLE WITH BOTH

1st movement Silvestri 23:29 Klemperer 24:24 download 23:14 NO WAY
2nd movement Silvestri 10:28 Klemperer 10:23 download 9:51 NO WAY
3rd movement Silvestri 10:49 Klemperer 10:07 dowload 11:09 NO WAY

Who is playing on the download page, I don't know. Its timings are compatible with none of the Menuhin versions I know. Who would play a Finale in a few seconds short of 11:09? This is either VERY slow, or with a very long cadenza.

This is not the first time I encounter these mis-links on Amazon - in fact, another one is provided, precisely, by the CD and purported mp3 download of Menuhin's Beethoven with Silvestri, see my review, and something here is starting to badly smell like scam and wilful deception. This is harmful to the performers' reputation (the opposite really of the famous Joyce Hatto scam: not trying to pass off great versions for those of an obscure performer, but trying to pass off recordings of dubious provenance for those of great artists) and deceitful to customers. I hope Amazon is a victim of this and not a party to it: this download is marketed by a "Hallmark" label and they may be the culprit. But I've had another case recently, with Gould and Bernstein's Beethoven Piano Concerto No. 3 & 4 on Sony's Bernstein Century edition (ASIN B000058TKA), and when I alerted Amazon to a gross mislink with another recording of the same by fifteenth-rate perfomers, I was rudely bullied off by two different interlocutors, so I'm starting to have doubts about the implication of Amazon in this. I've sent them a message to alert them to this one too (see comments), we'll see if there is a pattern.
top_rant.title: much better than its reputation (me against the critics). MP3 BUYERS, BEWARE!

top_rant.text:
I don't know why His Master's Voice made this recording in the first place. After all Menuhin had made a stereo recording of Beethoven's Violin Concerto in 1960 with the Vienna Philharmonic under Constantin Silvestri, a fine interpretation in excellent sound which had been received with accolades in The Gramophone ([[ASIN:B00005UUOE Violin Concerto]]). So why re-do it six years later? And I think, in those pre-Naxos years, they even PAID the performers that they recorded, not just royalties on sales, but upfront, and hefty sums too when they were stars like these. Maybe EMI (the owning company of HMV) was hoping to bank on two of their greatest stars and on the universal success of Klemperer's complete Beethoven symphonies, recorded in the late 1950s to early 1960s. And Klemp' had never recorded Beethoven's Violin Concerto (he would do the complete Piano Concertos a year later with Barenboim - and there are no recordings left of him, studio or live, in the Triple Concerto).

If so, it misfired. The recording was generally received with more than reluctance, and to this day it appears to be always dismissed in favor of Menuhin's earlier versions, especially those with Furtwängler, the more famous one with the Philharmonia Orchestra from 1953 ([[ASIN:B000002SG3 Beethoven, Mendelssohn: Violin Concertos (Violin Concerto in D Major, Op. 61 / Violin Concerto in E Minor, Op. 64)]]) and, now that it has been reissued by Testament and is easily accessible on CD, the earlier one from Lucerne in 1947 ([[ASIN:B000003XKA Beethoven: Violin Concerto; Romances Nos. 1 & 2]]).

The review of the original release published in the Gramophone issue of November 1966 by Edward Greenfield is very typical and deserves to be reproduced (and if you find that it strangely resembles the review posted by Colloredo of Caracas, don't infer that Edward Greenfield is still alive and living in Venezuela, but rather that Colloredo helped himself in the Gramophone electronic archive without citing his source):

"The point is that the special individual qualities of Menuhin and Furtwängler matched one another. Each brought out the best in the other, for their basic approach was identical, profoundly romantic and expressive but with an inner spiritual strength that was more elusive in emerging. I was hoping that the equally interesting combination of Menuhin and Klemperer would precipitate equally fine results, but I am afraid there is nothing like the same marriage of minds. One might have hoped that Menuhin's spontaneous imagination would lighten the impact of Klemperer and that Klemperer would give extra weight to Menuhin, but it has not worked that way. Admittedly Menuhin seems a degree or two more relaxed than he did in the Silvestri version made in Vienna, and the intonation is unquestionably better, but in neither case does that say very much. Though naturally there are many points of detail to admire, the overall impression is very much on the heavy side. One finds oneself objecting to the slow speed for the first movement, only to find that the basic tempo of both the earlier versions is a degree slower still. The fault lies here, I think, mainly with Klemperer, and no passage illustrates this more clearly than the beginning of the first movement coda after the cadenza. The pizzicato accompanying chords sound heavy not because they are loud or weighty but simply because they drag after the beat and the soloist seems to be pulling the players along.

In the slow movement Menuhin finds the mood of relaxation that was so conspicuously lacking in the Silvestri version, but go back to the Furtwängler and each phrase has a rapt sense of magic, of being created spontaneously in a way that makes the new version seem a little ordinary. In the finale the Silvestri version takes a turn for the better with Menuhin gaining confidence and a genuine spring in the rhythm. The new one is the slowest of the three, and characteristically Klemperer refuses to put a spring in the rhythm at all, so that at first it seems dull after the other two. But taken on its won it has a good bucolic solidity, and the middle D minor episode is firmer and stronger than in either of the previous versions. And interesting Klemperer point in the coda: instead of playing the emphatic crotchet plus quaver chordes at an even volume, he plays the last two comparatively quietly to allow the soloist's rising figure to come trhough."

And upon this first CD reissue, on EMI's mid-price Studio collection, in August 1985, Ivan March:

"Sir Yehudi Menuhin's recording of Beethoven's Violin Concerto dates from a decade earlier (1966) and does not show this artist at his best. Clearly there was no symbiosis in his partnership with Klemperer and when the slolo playing is sometimes rough in timbre, to the point of edginess, this is difficult to enjoy. The sound is brightly lit and not flattering. As it happens Menuhin's two earlier recordings have both been recently reissued. The 1954 mono performance with Furtwängler has been given CD status and was warmly welcomed by MEO, but more comparable in price is the Eminence reissue of the 1960 stereo version with a splendid accompaniment from the Vienna Philharmonic under Silvestri, which is in every way preferable to the later account".

Yet I find this Menuhin-Klemperer version much better than its reputation.

First, the notion that Klemperer conducts slow, let alone TOO slow, must be seriously qualified. It is true that he takes slightly longer to reach the first movement cadenza than Furtwängler in 1953 (20:12 vs 19:49) or 1947 in Lucerne (19:44) and significantly longer than Silvestri (19:14). But in fact, for the first part of the first movement, up until the re-entry of the violin on its opening rising motive, Klemperer's timing is EXACTLY the same as Furtwängler's: 10:40 in Lucerne, 10:50 with the Philharmonia, 10:50 with Klemperer. So it's only thereafter that Furwängler, in typical Furtwängler fashion, tends to step up his tempo, while Klemperer, in typical Klemperer fashion, remains steady as a rock.

Greenfeld's review seems serious enough, but when you listen to the recordings you realize that much of it is crap. "The basic tempo of both the earlier versions is a degree slower still"? (and here Greenfield is referring, of course, to Furtwängler 1953 and Silvestri 1960, not to Furtwängler 1947). No it's not! Basic tempo of Furtwängler 1953 was exactly the same (he reached the end of the orchestral introduction in 3:22, to Klemperer's... 3:20), and basic tempo of Silvestri in 1960 was significantly faster (though not really in the overture, which he conducted in 3:17). Precisely, one of the main differences between the Silvestri and the Klemperer version, is that the first remains very strict in tempo even in the more lyrical passages and Menuhin deploys his lyricism within the discipline of that strict tempo, while the other takes the time to relax and let the violin melodies soar more freely (that's what Greenfield alludes to when he writes, with justification, that "Menuhin seems a degree or two more relaxed than he did in the Silvestri version made in Vienna").

Not that this invalidates Greenfield's contention that, overall, Klemperer conducts a slower version; in fact, it reinforces the notion. That said, such expansive tempos may have seemed more shocking to some ears in 1966 than they do now. Prior to Klemperer, very few recordings had crossed the 20-minute threshold to reach the cadenza. Schneiderhan and Van Kempen were just there in 1953 (20:02), Oistrakh was way over with Cluytens in 1958 (20:42), Ferras-Sargent took 20:10 in 1959, Kogan-Silvetri 20:21 the same year, and Suk-Konwitschny had broken the 21-minute barrier in 1962 (21:10). But Menuhin-Klemperer seemed to have opened the dams. Came Ferras-Karajan in 1967 (21:07), Suk-Boult in 1970 (21:22), Zukerman-Barenboim in 1977 (20:35). When the famed version of Perlman-Guilini came out in 1981, it was hailed in The Gramophone ("no finer version available", they said - which of course left all those that were no more available) and the reviewer didn't complain that its first movement was too slow, although they reached the cadenza in 20:17. By the way that reviewer was Edward Greenfield.

Of course there is more to the feeling of slowness than just timing. But precisely, the claim against Klemperer and his purported slowness leaves entirely aside the very distinctive feature of his art of conducting, which makes him one of the greats of the 20th century: not just the deliberation, but the massive power he invests in the orchestra. And that's one massive advantage over Furtwängler's 1953 version, where by dint of the sonics but also the interpretation the orchestral tutti are much mellower. I hear none of the heaviness pointed by Greenfeld, because other than investing the tutti with massive power, Klemperer always keeps the phrasings crisp and accented. To me this doesn't sound "slow" or "heavy", it sounds immensely powerful.

But how could Greenfeld have heard that? At the end of his review of the Klemperer version, he confesses that he's "only heard the mono as yet"! Jesus!!!! And you are going to call a Rembrandt a fake based on a black and white photo??? Critics...

In fact, there are many advantages of this first movement with Klemperer over the two previous ones: over Furtwängler 1953, the stereo sonics and the sense of orchestral power; over Silvestri, the greater flexibility of tempo and greater songfulness in the lyrical passages. And to that, you may add that the 1966 sonics are an improvement, in clarity in impact, over even those of 1960.

And that thing of Greenfield with the pizzicati after the cadenza (23:09): frankly, I don't know what he is talking about, what he's heard or thought he'd heard - may again an effect of listening to the mono recording? There's no dragging of Klemperer here, there is the customary slow and meditative going out of the cadenza, and on the contrary as soon as the bassoon re-enters at 23:51, Klemperer adopts a rather flowing tempo where others prefer to retain the very expansive mood of meditativeness.

More crap from Greenfield: that Menuhin's "intonation is unquestionably better" than in 1960 with Silvestri. Obviously Ivan March heard the opposite. To my ears they are both wrong. Certainly the tone is not luminous as it was in 1947, there are a few edgy moments verging on wiriness in the runs and double stops of the Finale, Menuhin enters in Kreisler's first movement cadenza with more gentleness and less vibrancy than in 1960 (although this may be only the effect of a slightly more distant microphone placement), but you can find the same kind of - truly minuscule - tonal blemishes already in 1960 and even in 1953, and they don't add up to anything near the painful listening experience described by March. In fact, looking for tonal differences or trying to check on some I think I've heard, I am jumping from one version to the others, and the more carefully I listen and compare, the more, like the legendary desert mirage, they seem to elude me. Truth is, contrary to reputation (and sometimes fact), there is NO notable tonal deterioration between 1953, 1960 and 1966. The only implicit pointer that there might have been some technical deterioration in the 1966 Menuhin is that he plays a much-abridged version of the Kreisler 3rd movement cadenza, a decision for which I can find no obvious rationale: certainly not timing constraints of an LP side: even with the Cadenza played complete, Side B wouldn't have been longer than side A. This is not the greatest and purest violin tone in the world, but it is nothing to rant about and more than acceptable (I find the much-hailed Szigeti much more painful to hear in his 1947 recording with Bruno Walter, to say nothing of his 1961 remake with Dorati), and Menuhin deploys his customary lyricism and songfulness in the upper reaches.

"In the slow movement Menuhin finds the mood of relaxation that was so conspicuously lacking in the Silvestri version"? Crap again. With Klemperer the slow movement takes 10:09 to unfold (up to the cadential flourish leading to the Finale), and it was 10:13 with Silvestri, both find the very same "mood of relaxation", which is in fact a return to the way Menuhin did it with Furtwängler in 1947 (10:37). In fact Silvestri is even slightly MORE spacious than Klemperer in the first part of the movement, it is only with the movingly hushed passage with "raindrop" pzzicatti accompaniment (reached in 6:00 to Silvestri's 6:16) that Menuhin-Klemperer are now slightly more held-back. It is in 1953 with Furtwängler again that Menuhin strayed from that raptly held-back unfolding, with a more flowing and not so deeply-felt 9:23 duration. That's one of the main reasons to prefer Menuhin-Furtwängler 1947 over 1953. 1947 is possibly Menuhin's most beautiful and intense Larghetto, but all is relative: those with Silvestri and Klemperer are beautiful in their own right. The only thing you can say against Klemperer is that there is some artificial spotlighting of the woodwinds: the clarinet at 1:13 sounds as if he were seated next to the soloist. Beautiful, but not realistic.

I can't fault Greenfield on his assertion that Klemperer's Finale is the slowest of the three; in fact, it is, up to the cadenza, the slowest version I've ever encountered, and if the movement's total timing doesn't give that impression, it is only because, as already mentioned, Menuhin plays a vastly abridged version of Kreisler's cadenza. But still, Greenfield WAS crap when he claimed that there, the Silvestri version took "a turn for the better with Menuhin gaining confidence and a genuine spring in the rhythm". It is the opposite in fact, and before Klemperer, Menuhin's version with Silvestri was the second slowest ever that I had encountered after the one of Schneiderhan with Van Kempen in 1953. Even compared to the expansive and mellow Furtwängler in 1953, it was sucked of any spirit of dance, and was the most exceptionable part of the 1960 recording. In fact, I find the still slower Finale with Klemperer preferable: first, because it is, precisely, even more radical: as long as you are going to have the slowest ketchup in the world, have it REALLY the slowest. Second, because that very radicalism of approach serves character much better. Of the Silvestri version, I wrote that "the bounce that Menuhin manages to retain in his phrasings, his sweet lyricism in the more lyrical passages and the vigor of the orchestral outbursts are no real substitute" to the lost siprit of dance. With Klemperer, the massive power, the gruff and clumsy vigor ARE interesting substitutes. It calls for the same kind of comment that I made already about Klemperer's Finale of the Emperor Concerto with Barenboim, or the Scherzo and Finale of his Eroica: it evokes images of one of the hippos of Fantasia musingly hop-scotching, followed by the rest of the posse. It might have been impossibly elephantine and plodding - and it probably is, in fact - yet I don't find it "dull" at all. Whatever you think, it is unique, the Finale of the Violin Concerto as you've never heard it.

I don't know why HMV made this recording in the first place, but I know why they were right to do it: because, where many performers, when they re-record a work, are happy to offer exactly the same interpretation as before (Heifetz or Grumiaux come to mind), Menuhin here offers one that is very different from his previous ones, and one that is highly valuable to boot. In all, I find that the comparison of Menuhin's various recordings, and the observation of how different they are, paints an interesting and unexpected image of the violinist. Not one to impose, like Heifetz or Grumiaux, HIS unchanging views on his different conducting partners, but rather one ready and eager to blend into THEIR views. If the versions with Furtwängler are so Furtwänglerian, if the first movement with Silvestri is so strict in tempo and so expansive and massively powerful with Klemperer and if the Finale with Klemperer sounds so much like Klemperer's other Beethoven, it seems to be because Menuhin arrived at the recording sessions and said, "okay Wilhelm, okay Constantin, okay Otto, how would you like to conduct it? It'll be fine for me, I'll be happy to fit in, and say what I wish to say within your conception, rather than against it". That's a good definition of "the meeting of minds", and of making music together. As long as shelve space permits, I am not parting with this one, and if things get really bad, it's the one I'll keep with Lucerne 1947. Now if things get REALLY bad....

I don't have this early CD reissue but a later one, [[ASIN:B00000DOBH Beethoven: Violin Concerto / Romances Nos. 1 & 2]], where the Concerto is paired with the same Violin Romances under John Pritchard that EMI has retained as a complement to the Silvestri-conducted Concerto in the edition mentioned in the first paragraph, and that way all tastes and preferences are met. The transfer is the one of 1985 and the price of that edition make it a steal. That said, in the Romances Menuhin illustrates with Pritchard, as he did already with Furtwängler in 1953 (a recording reissued by Testament as a complement to their 1947 Lucerne Concerto), an approach that is way to solemn and sentimental for the music's own good. The Romances offer a welcome complement for the Menuhin completist, but it is the Klemperer-conducted Concerto that warrants purchase, with or without complement.
top_rant.title: much better than its reputation (me against the critics)

top_rant.text: This ultra-talented young lady never ceases to amaze me!!From start to finish this album is a definite masterpiece!!Alicia had already set the bar for my expectations from her EXTREMELY high when I first heard the touching emotion-soaked "Goodbye"from her debut album!! "Goodbye" is one of my favorite songs of the last 15 years,PERIOD!!Because with its emotion-filled lyrics,Alicia made me(vividly)recall the exact moment of my very first heartbreak..Amazingly Alicia pulled this same sort of feat off again with "Teenage Love Afair"in which she caused me to feel nostalgia about my first"puppy-love" These following lyrics were instrumental in doing this:"I love our secret meetings on the 5th floor staircase,I'm gonna give you this letter of all the things I can't SAY." "Cant WAIT to get home baby,dial your number.."Daydream about you all day in school,CAN'T concentrate,wanna have your voice in my ear 'til Momma come and say:"It's TOO late." "3rd Base":"Better pump the brakes and baby slow down,I gotta go HOME now!" this following line is something that I bet EVERY young couple did:"I write your name in my book,your last name,my first,I'm your"Misses"!!"Alicia perfectly mirrors the seriousness in which we held our"puppy-love"regardless of the fact that all of our elders and parents blew our 1st-time"romance"off as:"goofy,nose-wide-open fleeting kid-stuff"when she so emphatically sings:"It's a matter of EXTREEEME importance,my first teenage love affair!!" The title track starts the album off with vintage Alicia Keys(beautiful)piano-play.."Go Ahead" is a FIERY"woman-fed-up"type of track,where Alicia has finally experienced the proverbial"straw" that broke the camels' back..Very convincing lines that show that Alicia is RESOLUTE in her decision are:"No NEED to apologize,don't TRY to"make-it-right!" and"NOTHING you can SAY or can DO,can make me CHANGE my mind!!" This following line is one I can REALLY empathize with:"Broken promises,my heart you stole,LIES you told,got the best of me,DIGGIN' in my heart-of-gold!!" And these aingst-filled lines:"I see you for who you are,know all I NEED to know,boy you DISSAPOINTED me!!" "Soon enough time REVEALS,the weight of what is REAL!!" "You point your finger,trying to JUSTIFY your mistakes,a change IS gone' be made,so you'd BEST,be on your way!!"The(barely audible)sad-sounding voice stating:"My man don't want me no more,he said he needed his time,to clear his mind,meanwhile I'm LOSING mine!!" at the opening of"Where Do We Go From Here"with its' accompanying vaudevillian music is a GREAT touch!! Alicia continues with her vintage mature/adult emotional content with:"Maybe you WON'T go,maybe you'll stay,I'm gonna miss you,EITHER way!!(THINK about what she's actually SAYING on that line!!)"It's such a LONELY road!!" SuperWoman"UN-like Karyn Whites' and Vivian Greens'songs of the same name has Alicia proudly declaring that she IS in fact a "superwoman"while encouraging/urging other women to undauntedly EMBRACE their myriad responsibilities!"Thing About Love"has Alicia displaying great maturity and thoughtfulness in making"statements"describing love:"Love,it will come FIND you,just to REMIND you,of WHO you are." "Oh love,it will FORSAKE you,threaten to BREAK you,TAKE what you got!!" "Love,it will EMBRACE you,totally AMAZE you,so you don't give up..""These very descriptive lines are both POWERFUL and TRUE!!Every single line Alicia used in describing love is one I can readily apply to my own experiences with love!!Alicia got straight-up ingenious(and"deep") on this song!! Alicia sounds really distraught as she sings:"Tell me,I am not the ONLY one going through it all,sometimes I FEEL like,I am the only one,going through it all!" Alicia then sounds confident and triumphant with:"But it's time for me to SHINE!!"Like You'll Never See Me Again"is a wonderful song with a great point and an excellent SOLUTION to any stagnant relationship.."Lesson Learned"is another great song with some really profound and thought-provoking lyrics such as:"A life perfect aint perfect,if you don't know what struggle's for,falling down aint falling down if you don't CRY when you hit the floor!"the song carries an excellent point:no experience is a TOTAL loss or complete waste of ones'time,if you LEARNED something FROM it. The most EXCELLENT/powerful,eye-opening line on this song(which reveals the PRIME reason why a lot of women seem to get"walked all over"by the man they love)is:"You give it one more chance"JUST like the time BEFORE,but he ALREADY KNOWS,you'd give a HUNDRED more!! This following line is VERY encouraging and displays a"TRIUMPHANT"Alicia with:"It's called the"past"cause I'm gettin'PAST and I aint NOTHING like I was BEFORE,you oughta see me NOW!!""Alicia confidently states:Yes I was"burned"but I call it a lesson LEARNED!" The songs'opening line is sang with such emotion,that the"sadness"in it is almost TANGIBLE:"He broke my heart,and now it's raining,just to RUB-IT-IN.." "No One"is a wonderful song that has Alicia declaring that nothing and No ONE will be able to interfere with the growth of this promising relationship."Reckless Love"is a thoughtful song where Alicia is longing for the BLUE-hot RAW passion that was present at the start of her current relationship,which has apparently "cooled-off.." This is a song that EVERYONE in any long-term relationship should be able to identify with!! POWERFUL lyrics include:"I want that CRAZY love,that"Off-the-wall-WON'T-stop-'til-I get ENOUGH" kinda love!!" "I want THAT love!"  "I NEED that love!!" "Let's go BACK there,let's take it there!!" "I want that"I dont-really-CARE-we-can-have it-ANYWHERE" kinda love!!" "Sure Looks Good To Me"has Alicia throwing caution to the wind,electing instead to act on appearances alone.."On"Tell you something"Alicia shows that she will not risk not conveying her feelings about someone. "I Need You"is so very POWERFUL lyrically as Alicia EXPERTLY compares her and her loves'need for each other with the very forces of nature itself and lifes' opposing concepts that wouldn't EXIST without its' opposite..The song starts out with Alicia cleverly applying human attributes to natural phenomena and inanimate things:"The sand LOVES when,the waves come." "The sky can't WAIT,for the light of the Sun." After stating this Alicia turns the attention back to how her relationship should also be as "sure"and unchanging as her allegories pertaining to nature,with:"So how COULD you,look me in my eye,and NOT see what,what I feel INSIDE?!?" "Tell me how could you,DOUBT the fact that I,I LOVE you!!" Alicia then equally very cleverly(and truthfully)states how certain things couldn't exist without other things..How they "need"another thing to simply exist:"North needs South,East needs West,and"no"needs YES,YES,YES!!" She continues:"Up needs Down,Night needs Day and even this  rather "disturbing"fact:"Life NEEDS Death!!" "Prelude to a kiss"with its'BEAUTIFUL piano has Alicia battling with unsurety to act on simple emotion.This song has some very touching lines:"Sometimes I feel like I don't BELONG anywhere,it's gonna take so LONG,for me to get somewhere..""Imagine there was no tommorrow,I couldn't IMAGINE that I couldn't see your face,there'd be no LIMIT to my SORROW,so all I can say.." "Sometimes I feel so heavy-hearted,but I CAN'T explain,'cause I'm so GUARDED!!" "That's a LONELY road to travel,and a HEAVY load to bear,and it's a LONG,LONG road to"heaven",but I gotta get there!!" "Can you send me an Angel to GUIDE me?!?" "Tell You Something"features Alicia musing on the penalty of NOT conveying how you care for someone. Alicia is a MASTER at summing a point up,with:"Cause it will all mean NOTHING,if I don't say SOMETHING,before it all goes AWAY!!"and the emphatic:"Won't wait'til it's TOO LATE!!" "Sure Looks Good To Me"wraps the album up with a"BANG" The songs' first line is CLASSIC:"Life is cheap,bittersweet,but it"tastes"GOOD to me!!followed with:"I'm gonna risk it ALL,the FREEDOM to fall.."and"Take my turn,CRASH and BURN,thats how it's 'SPOSED to be!!"I really enjoy how the INTENSITY of the song steadily increases from the near whisper of Alicias' voice as she starts with:"Deep in my mind......"and finally culminating with Alicia almost SCREAMING:"So WHO are we ANYWAY!!" I'm really IMPRESSED with Alicias sheer emotion as she so DESPERATELY pleads:"So DON'T rain on MY"parade"!!"and the heart-felt(very factual)declaration:"Life's TOO short,to waste ONE day!!" I just LOVE the ingenious way that this song rises to its' crescendo!! The "Yeah,yeah,yeah"repeated throughout this song really adds to its' emotional feel!!I LOVE these following lines:Deep in my mind,I'm secure with"gettin' by",wanna see the"light"before I die,before I lie in an empty space.." "The "darkness" comes an' I keep tellin' my soul,an' me an' myself,we turn around,we're gettin' old.." "But the lightning crashing,FOOLISH emotions!!" "I feel like I'm seeing the world INSIDE of me,but I can tell you that I know,it's getting EASIER to breathe!!" "The bruises and the beauty of this moment that were feeling.." This is an EXCELLENT well-written intelligent/thoughtful album!!
