top_screaming.text:
WINNER OF THE EDITOR'S CHOICE AWARD BY GRAMOPHONE MAGAZINE IN 2008, THIS TOUR-DE-FORCE MOZART WIND SERENADES [WIND SEXTETS] K.361, K.375, K.388 & DIVERTIMENTI K.240, K.252, K.253, K.270 CD BY THE AMADEUS WINDS CHAMBER ENSEMBLE IS FOUND IN MANY MOZART AUDIOPHILE'S CD/MP3 COLLECTION!

CD REVIEWER NOTE TO AMAZON.COM READERS: I TYPE WITH LARGE CAP LETTERS TO READ WHAT I WRITE BECAUSE I AM A 100% DISABLED DECORATED VIETNAM VETERAN AND AM ALMOST BLIND WITH MACULAR DEGENERATION. I APOLOGIZE FOR ANY INCONVENIENCE CAPS MAY CAUSE YOU. ~PATRICK HILL, MOZART SCHOLAR & AUTHOR~

WOLFGANG AMADEUS MOZART [1756 - 1791]
WIND SERENADES K.361, K.375, K.388
DIVERTIMENTI K.240, K.252, K.253, K.270
PERFORMED BY CONCERTMASTER CHRISTOPHER HOGWOOD &
THE AMADEUS WINDS CHAMBER ENSEMBLE

CD REVIEW
THIS SUPERB 2-CD SET FEATURES THE AMADEUS WINDS DELIVERING A SPELLBINDING PERFORMANCE OF MOZART'S WIND SEXTETS WITH HIS MOST SERENE SERENADES AND DIVERTIMENTI COMPOSED AS ENTERTAINMENT MUSIC!

THE AMADEUS WINDS ARE RENOWNED AS ONE OF THE WORLD'S TOP RATED PERIOD INSTRUMENT CHAMBER MUSIC ENSEMBLES AND THEY DELIVER A WORLD CLASS PERFORMANCE OF MOZART'S WIND SERENADES AND DIVERTIMENTI ON THIS 2-CD SET THE WAY MOZART WOULD HAVE EXPECTED THEM TO BE PLAYED AND TO BE HEARD! SOFTER THAN MODERN CLASSICAL INSTRUMENTS, THE SOUND QUALITY OF THE AMADEUS WINDS'S PERIOD INSTRUMENTS EXHIBITS CHAMBER MUSICAL PERFECTION SO THE AUDIENCE HEARS EVERY MUSICAL NOTE COMPOSED BY MOZART.

MOZART'S SERENADE GRAN PARTITA K.361 HAS BEEN REGARDED FOR THE LAST 250 YEARS AS ONE OF HIS GREATEST CLASSICAL MUSIC MASTERPIECES. THERE IS A MAGICAL MOMENT IN THE FILM AMADEUS IN WHICH THE VILLAN ANTONIO SALIERI LISTENS FOR THE FIRST TIME TO MOZART'S GRAN PARTITA K.361. SALIERI'S TEAR JERKING DESCRIPTION OF GRAN PARTITA'S THIRD MOVEMENT ADAGIO IS QUOTED AS FOLLOWS: "ON THE PAGE IT LOOKED NOTHING. THE BEGINNING SIMPLE, ALMOST COMIC. JUST A PULSE - BASSOONS AND BASSET HORNS - LIKE A RUSTY SQUEEZEBOX. THEN SUDDENLY - HIGH ABOVE IT - AN OBOE, A SINGLE NOTE, HANGING THERE UNWAVERING, TILL A CLARINET TOOK OVER AND SWEETENED IT INTO A PHRASE OF SUCH DELIGHT! THIS WAS NO COMPOSITION BY A PERFORMING MONKEY! THIS WAS A MUSIC I HAD NEVER HEARD. FILLED WITH SUCH LONGING, SUCH UNFULFILLABLE LONGING, IT HAD ME TREMBLING. IT SEEMED TO ME THAT I WAS HEARING A VOICE OF GOD."

ABOUT MOZART'S DIVERTIMENTI & SERENADES ENTERTAINMENT MUSIC
IN THE 18TH CENTURY, ONE OF MOZART'S SPECIALTIES WAS COMPOSING FESTIVE ENTERTAINMENT MUSIC CALLED DIVERTIMENTI AND SERENADES TO BE PLAYED AS BACKGROUND MUSIC BY A SMALL CHAMBER MUSIC ENSEMBLE AT OUTDOOR SOCIAL EVENTS SUCH AS NAME DAYS, BIRTHDAYS, NEW YEAR'S FESTIVITIES, WEDDINGS, AND SCHOOL GRADUATION CELEBRATIONS WHERE PARTY GUESTS ENJOYING A DRINKS AND MEALS. THE WELL-TO-DO PATRON'S OF MOZART'S ENTERTAINMENT MUSIC [ALSO CALLED CHAMBER MUSIC] WERE USUALLY THE ARISTOCRACY IN MOZART'S NATIVE SALZBURG AND VIENNA. AT SOME ENTERTAINMENT MUSIC OCCASIONS, MOZART HIMSELF OFTEN PLAYED THE SOLO VIOLIN, VIOLA, AND PIANO PASSAGES OF HIS DIVERTIMENTI AND SERENADES.

ALTHOUGH MOZART'S DIVERTIMENTI AND SERENADES WERE FUN LIGHT HEARTED CHAMBER MUSIC UNLIKE HIS SERIOUS SYMPHONIES AND CONCERTOS WHICH WERE INTENDED TO BE PERFORMED IN A THEATRE OR CONCERT ROOM, MOZART'S ENTERTAINMENT MUSIC [ALSO CALLED CHAMBER MUSIC] OFFERS CLASSICAL MUSIC ENTHUSIASTS THE SAME BEAUTY, CHARM, AND UNIQUE QUALITY AS HIS MORE SERIOUS MATURE CLASSICAL WORKS.

THE HUGE ADVANTAGE OF LISTENING TO MOZART'S DIVERTIMENTI AND SERENADES PERFORMED BY A SMALL CHAMBER MUSIC ENSEMBLE VERSUS A LARGE ORCHESTRA IS THE SUPERIOR PERFECT SOUND OF PERIOD INSTRUMENTS HEARD BY THE AUDIENCE THAT IS TONALLY EXCELLENT, CLOSE UP, AND NATURALLY BALANCED!

ABOUT CHRISTOPHER HOGWOOD & THE ACADEMY OF ANCIENT MUSIC [AAM]
CHRISTOPHER HOGWOOD IS A WORLD RENONWED ENGLISH CONDUCTOR, CONCERTMASTER, HARPSICHORDIST, WRITER, AND SCHOLAR OF MUSIC. IN 1973, CHRISTOPHER HOGWOOD FOUNDED THE ACADEMY OF ANCIENT MUSIC [AAM] IN LONDON ENGLAND AS THE WORLD'S FIRST PERIOD-INSTRUMENT CLASSICAL MUSIC ORCHESTRA THAT IS RATED #1 BY CLASSICAL MUSIC AUDIOPHILES. SINCE 1973, THE ACADEMY OF ANCIENT MUSIC HAS PERFORMED 500 CONCERTS ACROSS SIX CONTINENTS AND OVER RECORDED OVER 250 CLASSICAL MUSIC CDS CLEARLY DEMONSTRATES THE ACADEMY OF ANCIENT MUSIC'S PRE-EMINENCE AS THE WORLD'S FINEST PERIOD INSTRUMENT CLASSICAL MUSIC ORCHESTRA!

ABOUT AMADEUS WINDS CHAMBER ENSEMBLE
FOUNDED IN 1983 BY THE FAMED OBOIST STEPHEN HAMMER TO PERFORM MOZART'S MOST POPULAR CHAMBER MUSIC ON PERIOD INSTRUMENTS, THE AMADEUS WINDS CHAMBER ENSEMBLE MUSICIANS ARE:
*STEPHEN HAMMER & MARC SCHACHMAN [OBOE]
*ERIC HOEPRICH & LAWRENCE MCDONALD [CLARINET]
*DENNIS GODBURN & ROBIN HOWELL [BASSOON]
*LOWELL GREER & WILLIAM PURVIS [HORN]
top_screaming.title: RATED 5 STARS #1 CHAMBER MUSIC CD MOZART WIND SERENADES K.361,K.375,K.388/DIVERTIMENTI K.240,K.252,K.253,K.270 by AMADEUS WINDS

top_screaming.text:
PROCOL HARUM, TO PUT IT BLUNTLY, STARTED OFF WONDERFULLY WEIRD WITH A WHITER SHADE OF PALE, AND GOT EXPONENTIALLY WEIRDER WITH SEEMINGLY EVERY NEW RELEASE. THIS IS NOT MEANT AS AN INDICTMENT, BUT RATHER AS AN ENDORSEMENT OF THEIR MELODICALLY POETIC APPROACH TO PROGRESSIVE ROCK (?) MUSIC.

GARY BROOKER, RATHER THAN PURPORTING TO BE A SAVANT (A LA KEITH EMERSON OR RICK WAKEMAN), PLAYS THE PIANO WITH A CLASSICALLY TRAINED, ALBEIT EMPHATIC, RESERVE - A TOOL FOR THE COMPOSITION OF A GREATER WORK. BROOKER KNOWS WHEN TO BOB AND WHEN TO WEAVE - IN SHORT, WHEN TO YIELD TO A MORE POWERFUL INSTRUMENT (TROWER'S GUITAR, WILSON'S DRUMMING), OR TO A POTENT LYRIC - OR TO HIS OWN CONSIDERABLE VOCAL BREADTH.

TRUE, THE ONE CONSTANT TO PROCOL HARUM'S PECULIARITY IS THE LYRIC, AS CONSTRUCTED BY THE MAD POET, KEITH REID. AS THE ONLY LYRICIST I CAN THINK OF CONSIDERED (AND DESERVEDLY SO) AN OFFICIAL MEMBER OF THE BAND, REID PAINTS SOME OF THE MOST ENIGMATIC AND YES, PRETENTIOUS PORTRAITS IN ALL OF ROCK MUSIC.

ROBIN TROWER WILL ALWAYS BE THE QUINTESSENTIAL GUITARIST OF PROCOL HARUM. TROWER ALWAYS WEAVES HIS MELODIES INTO THE CONTEXT OF THE MATERIAL. FEW GUITARISTS ARE CAPABLE OF THE RANGE TROWER TYPICALLY EXERCISES - FROM WANTON BEAUTY TO LURID EMPHASIS, WITH SOME OF THE SAME RESERVE EMPLOYED BY BROOKER.

B.J. WILSON ON PERCUSSION, AND CHRIS COPPING ON ORGAN AND BASS KEYBOARD, PROVIDE AN EFFICIENT, UNCOMMONLY TASTEFUL RHYTHM SECTION - ANOTHER KEY COMPONENT TO PROCOL'S UNIQUE BLEND. WILSON CANNOT BE IGNORED FOR HIS ROLE IN SHAPING PROCOL'S SIGNATURE SOUND - QUICK SNARE DRUMMING, A SLAPPING, FLAT BASS DRUM, ALWAYS BALANCED BY TINNY CIMBALS TO CREATE A DISTINCTIVE, DRIVING BACKBEAT ENGINE. CHRIS COPPING WOULD LATER TRANSCEND HIS YEOMAN EFFORT HERE FOR A STRONGER ROLE IN THE SHAPING OF GRAND HOTEL, PROCOL HARUM'S ELEGANT FOLLOW-UP TO BARRICADES.

BROKEN BARRICADES, THEIR 1971 RELEASE, IS PERHAPS, ON BALANCE, THEIR GREATEST WORK. TYPICALLY, THE TITLE CUT IS SURE TO STRIKE A DIFFERENT CHORD WITH EVERY LISTENER, DUE TO ITS AMBIGUOUS LYRIC AND SORROWFUL MELODY. [I, FOR INSTANCE, HAVE NEVER BEEN ABLE TO LISTEN TO THE TUNE WITHOUT VISIONS OF THE SEIGE OF TROY - BUT THAT'S JUST ME]; OF COURSE, YOU WON'T GET ANY ADVICE FROM EITHER REID OR BROOKER ON SONG INTERPRETATION - AND THAT'S THE BELLY OF THE BEAST THAT IS PROCOL HARUM.

THE ALBUM BEGINS WITH SIMPLE SISTER, APOCALYPTIC PROCOL - BROOKER'S VOCAL AND PIANO FRAMING A TERRIFIC BACKBEAT, A DEFT UNDERPINNING FOR THE BROOKER-PENNED LEAD AND BASS RIFFS. MEMORIAL DRIVE IS ITS MATCHING BOOKEND (THOUGH A REID/TROWER COMPOSITION) WITH ANOTHER SUPERB BACKBEAT AND STILL ANOTHER COOL TROWER HOOK. POWER FAILURE IS MORE OF THIS SAME POWERFUL HARUM GROOVE, WITH WILSON'S PERCUSSION MIXED TO THE FOREFRONT AND CAPPED BY BROOKER'S OVERDUBBED CHORUS.

AS ALWAYS, PROCOL HARUM DISPLAYS A WONDERFUL SENSE OF HUMOR AMIDST ALL THAT LYRICAL TRAGEDY AND MELODIC DRAMA, AND NOWHERE IS IT BETTER SHOWN THAN ON THIS RELEASE. LUSKUS DELPH IS A NONE TOO WELL DISGUISED, LEWD PAEN TO THE FEMALE ANATOMY.

POOR MOHAMMED AGAIN FEATURES TROWER'S WRITING TALENT, IN AN EARSTWHILE ROCKER WITH A SAUCY TROMBONE/SLIDE GUITAR MIX. TROWER'S TRIBUTE TO THE THEN RECENTLY DEPARTED JIMI HENDRIX, SONG FOR A DREAMER, PRESAGES HIS ESOTERIC LEANINGS LATER REALIZED IN HIS SUCCESSFUL SOLO CAREER. PLAYMATE OF THE MOUTH IS BROOKER WITH HIS TONGUE IN CHEEK, BUT HE DOESN'T SCRIMP ON HIS SIGNATURE PRODUCTION STYLINGS, AS TROMBONES AGAIN ABOUND.

FOR PROCOL HARUM, THE DRAMA IS CREATED BY THE MUSIC; THE STORY SPAWNED FROM THE LYRICS. BROOKER NEVER SHIES AWAY FROM PRODUCTION, AND HIS USE OF HORN AND STRING ARRANGEMENTS IS INGENUOUS, VITAL AND BEATIFIC, NEVER STRAINED OR PRETENTIOUS. KEITH REID REFUSES TO ALLOW LOGIC (OR PRETENSE) TO CORRAL HIS REMARKABLE ABILITY TO BEND A PRETTY PHRASE, HOWEVER NON-SENSICAL. TOGETHER, BROOKER AND REID DEFINE PROCOL HARUM, AND THEY BOTH SHINE ON BROKEN BARRICADES.
top_screaming.title: PROCOL'S EXQUISITE BEST

*BENJAMIN HUDSON (BASSOON)
*GUY ESHED (FLUTE)

ABOUT MOZART'S CLARINET QUINTET IN A [K.581 STADLER]
COMPOSED IN 1789 AT VIENNA FOR THE HIS FRIEND ANTON STADLER ONE OF THE 18TH CENTURY'S GREATEST CLARINETISTS OF VIENNA'S ROYAL COURT ORCHESTRA, MOZART'S CLARINET QUINTET K.581 IS SCORED IN FOUR [4] GLORIUS MOVEMENTS FOR 1 BASSETT CLARINET AND A STRING QUARTET OF 2 VIOLINS, 1 VIOLA AND 1 CELLO. MOZART'S CLARINET QUINTET IN A [K.581 STADLER] IS RENOWNED FOR ITS BEAUTIFUL LYRICAL MELODIES! MOZART COMPOSED HIS CLARINET QUINTET K.581 WITH A WIDE VARIETY OF MOODS, DIVERSITY OF MUSICAL TEXTURES, AND SOPHISTICATED RICHNESS TO ENABLE THE CLARINETIST TO DISPLAY HIS VIRTUOSO TALENTS AS MUCH AS POSSIBLE.

ABOUT MOZART'S QUINTET FOR PIANO & WINDS IN E [K.452]
WOLFGANG MOZART'S 1784 LETTER TO HIS FATHER LEOPOLD MOZART READS: "I CONSIDER MY QUINTET FOR PIANO & WINDS [K.452] THE FINEST WORK I HAVE EVER COMPOSED IN MY LIFE. I ONLY WISH YOU COULD HAVE HEARD IT AND HOW BEAUTIFULLY IT WAS PERFORMED!"

COMPOSED IN 1784 AT VIENNA, MOZART'S QUINTET K.452 IS SCORED IN THREE [3] MOVEMENTS FOR PIANO, CLARINET, OBOE, BASSOON, AND HORN. MOZART'S QUINTET K.452 CREATES FIVE [5] EQUAL INTSRUMENT VOICES THAT COMBINE AND CONTRAST IN VARIOUS WAYS MOZART'S BRILLIANT CONCERTANTE STYLE.

MOZART'S K.452 IN UNIQUE AMONG HIS WORKS BECAUSE THERE ARE FOUR [4] DISSIMILAR WIND INSTRUMENTS COMPETING WITH THE SOLO PIANO. WITH HIS UNRIVALED CLASSICAL MUSIC GENIUS, WOLFGANG MOZART COMPOSED HIS QUINTET K.452 IN A SPECIAL WAY THAT RESPECTS BOTH THE CAPABILITY AND CHARACTER OF EACH OF THE FIVE [5] INDIVIDUAL INSTRUMENTS. IN MOZART'S QUINTET K.452, NONE OF THE FIVE INSTRUMENTS [PIANO, CLARINET, OBOE, BASSOON, HORN] ARE PROMINENT OR SUBORDINATED.

EACH OF MOZART'S QUINTET K.452'S FOUR [4] WIND INSTRUMENTS WERE DESIGNED WITH LIMITATIONS THAT RESTRICT THE EXTENT TO WHICH THEIR UNIQUE TONAL SOUNDS CAN MINGLE WITH OTHER INSTRUMENTS. IN MOZART'S QUINTET K.452, SHORT MOTIFS WITH BRIEF CORD PASSAGES ARE LINKED TOGETHER IN MELODIES CONTRASTED AGAINST THE SOLO PIANO AS EITHER AN ACCOMPANIMENT, OPPONENT, OR PARTNER.

ABOUT MOZART'S FLUTE QUARTET NO.1 IN D [K.285]
COMPOSED IN 1777 AT MANNHEIM, MOZART'S FLUTE QUARTET K.285 IS SCORED IN THREE [3] MOVEMENTS FOR FLUTE, VIOLIN, VIOLA, CELLO, AND ONE OF THE MOST HAUNTINGLY BEAUTIFUL WORKS EVER WRITTEN FOR FLUTE!

ABOUT MOZART'S ADAGIO FOR ENGLISH HORN & STRING TRIO [K.580A]
MOZART'S ADAGIO K.580A IS RECONSTRUCTED FROM A FRAGMENT BY A CLASSICAL MUSICIAN AND NOT WORTHY OF MY REVIEW BECAUSE MOZART DID NOT COMPOSE THIS ENTIRE WORK.

Here is another set of 80 songs from various female artists and girl groups from the early '60's . These artists practically took over the charts after the deaths of Buddy Holly , Richie Valens , and The Big Bopper in a February 1959 plane crash. This period has been referred to as "the day the music died" by Don Mclean on his song "AMERICAN PIE" . I like a lot of these songs ,although a lot of them never made it to the charts . I am entering the mono / stereo status of all of these songs for all of the STEREOPHILES (!) out there !!! OH YES , I AM A STEREOPHILE !!
NOTE : Disc two (2) track ten (10) "AM I THE SAME GIRL" - BARBARA ACKLIN is a vocalization of YOUNG-HOLT UNLIMITED's instrumental hit "SOULFUL STRUT" .
top_screaming.title: "BE MY BABY 2 -MORE MUSIC FROM THE FABULOUS GIRLS OF THE '60'S'" BY VARIOUS FEMALE ARTISTS(2012)

top_screaming.text:
WELL , WELL , WELL I MUST SAY THAT I WAS SADLY MISTAKEN.USUALLY WHEN A NEWCOMER STEPS IN WITH THEIR DEBUT IM LIKE O' HERE WE GO ,,, ANOTHER ONE HIT WONDER!!!!! BUT THAT WAS DEFINITLY NOT THE CASE WITH MISS. CIARA HARRIS' "GOODIES". I MEAN THE TITLE OF THE ALBUM IS WHAT YOU GET EXACTLY,,, THIS IS A GREAT ALBUM . AND TRUST ME I KNOW,, I PURCHASED IT ON THE FIRST DAY AND HAVE BEEN BANGIN' IT EVER SINCE... I REALLY ENJOY THE ALBUM AND ITS VARIETY... FROM THE EVERLASTING FIRST SINGLE "GOODIES" TO THE LAST SONG "THE TITLE" YOU GET ,, GREAT SONGS TO DANCE TO,, RIDE TO ,, GROOVE TO AND CHILL TO.. NOTICE I DIDNT SAY MAKE LOVE TO BECAUSE UNLIKE SOME OF CIARA'S COMPETITION,,, SHE IS ONLY 18 AND IVE READ SOME REVIEWS WHERE THEYV'E SAID ITS A KIDDIE ALBUM AND THERE ARE NO "SERIOUS" SONGS... I MEAN COME ON YALL GIVE THE YOUNG WOMAN TIME TO GROW... AND LIKE USHER,,, CIARA WILL HAVE HER LITTLE CONFESSIONS ALBUM LATER,, JUST GIVE HER TIME... BUT BACK TO THE SUBJECT... "GOODIES" IS A GREAT ONE FROM BEGINNING TO END. THE ONLY REASON I GAVE IT 4 STARS IS BECAUSE I WISHED I COULD HAVE HEARD HER VOCAL RANGE MORE,,, I MEAN IF UR LOOKING FOR AN ALBUM WITH LIKE 10 BALLADS AND TWO CLUB BANGERS,,, THEN DONT EVEN BUY "GOODIES"... THE ALBUM CONSISTS OF ONE BALLAD AND MID TO UPTEMPO TRACKS BUT DONT BE ALARMED IT IS DEFINITLY WORTH THE LISTEN... NOW AS FAR AS THE TRACKS FROM BEST TO LESS....

"GOODIES", THE REMIX FEAT. T.I. AND "1,2 STEP" WILL BE EXCLUDED.

"THE TITLE"-- TO ME THIS IS THE BEST SONG ON THE ALBUM,, SHE SAMPLES L.T.D'S "LOVE BALLAD" AND DOES A GREAT, GREAT JOB.. THIS SONG SPEAKS OF A YOUNG WOMAN WHO HAS A CRUSH ON A YOUNG MAN,, AND SHES TELLING HIM SHE WANTS THE TITLE WHICH MEANS "LOVER",, "FRIEND" AND SO ON... THIS SONG SHOULD DEFINITLY BE THE FOLLOW UP TO "1,2,STEP". -5/5-

"NEXT TO YOU"-- NOW R. KELLY DID A GREAT JOB IN ARRANGING THIS SONG,, EVEN THOUGH HE SAYS 2 SENTENCES ITS A BEAUTIFUL SONG. IT SPEAKS OF A YOUNG WOMAN WHO WANTS TO BE WITH HER MAN BUT SHES SCARED OF BEING WITH HIM BECAUSE SHE WILL PROBABLY DO SOMETHING OUT OF HER NATURE.... CUTE LITTLE LOVE SONG-5/5-

"THUGSTYLE"-- NICE MELLOWED OUT SONG WITH A CUTE LITTLE SAYING "LA LA LA LA ETC." THIS SONG IS SO CUTESY... ITS SPEAKS OF A YOUNG WOMAN WHO LOVES HER MANS THUGSTYLE,,, HE MAKES HER SMILE AND SHE IS JUST IN LOVE WITH HIM.. SHE SNEAKS HIM IN WHILE THE PARENTS ARE AWAY,, BUT SHE KEEPS HIM WONDERING BY SAYING "DONT MOVE TOO FAST AND DONT MOVE TOO SLOW".

BEAUTIFUL JOB BY CIARA... AND A LOVELY CHORUS...-5/5-

"OOH BABY"-- THIS IS A TIGHT MID TEMPO TRACK WITH A TIGHT BEAT AND CUTE CHORUS,,, COULD ALSO BE A SINGLE.. ITS ABOUT A YOUNG WOMAN WHO IS SO INFATUATED WITH THIS YOUNG MAN,,, SHE HAS HIS BACK AND HE IS LIKE A DRUG TO HER,, SHE HAS TO HAVE HIM AND SHE CANT SAY NO TO HIM... THIS IS A TIGHT , TIGHT SONG.-5/5-

"AND I" -- THE ONLY BALLAD,, IS IT JUST ME OR DOES THE PRE-CHORUS SOUND LIKE "A WOMAN'S WORTH"? THIS SONG IS SO PRETTY ... IT SPEAKS ON THIS YOUNG WOMAN WHO WANTS A MAN WHO WILL SACRAFICE FOR HER AND WILL DO ANYTHING FOR HER... ONE THAT WONT BREAK HER HEART AND VICE VERSA.. I LOVE THIS SONG ALOT... I WILL ADMIT IT HAD TO GROW ON ME THOUGH...-5/5-

"OH"--NOW THIS IS A STREET SONG REPPING HER HOOD ,, WHICH I CAN REALTE TO BECAUSE HER AND I BOTH ARE FROM ATLANTA...(ATL).. I MEAN THIS SONG IS SELF EXPLANATORY,,,BUT FOR THOSE OF YOU WHO HAVENT HEARD IT,, SHES TALKING ABOUT HER HOOD,,, LUDA'S VERSE IS HOT AS WELL.. NICE BEAT BY JAZZE TOO.. COULD ALSO BE A SINGLE...--5/5-

"OTHER CHICKS"-- TIGHT SONG AND DIFFERENT... ITS ABOUT A GIRL WHO LIKES THIS YOUNG MAN AND HE HAS OTHER FEMALES BUT HE LIKES HER AND SHES TELLING HIM FORGET ABOUT THEM OTHER CHICKS CAUSE IMMA BE THE ONLY ONE THAT WILL BE DOWN FOR YOU....NICE NICE SONG

-5/5-

"LOOKIN' AT YOU"-- NICE UPTEMPO,, CLUB BANGER ABOUT A GIRL WHO IS FEELIN THIS GUY AT THE CLUB AND YALL KNOW THE REST.. TIGHT CHOROUS AND BEAT... -4/5-

"PICK UP THE PHONE"-- I LIKE THIS SONG BUT THE SUBJECT IS CRAZY TO ME ,, SHOOT IF HE DONT PICK UP CALL THE NEXT ONE... ITS LIKE THE GIRL IN THE SONG HAS NOTHING BETTER TO DO THAN CALL THIS BOY... BUT ITS AN ALRIGHT SONG-3/5-

"HOTLINE" -- ONE OF MY LEAST FAVS BUT ITS A GOOD SONG,, ITS A LITTLE CORNY BUT I LIKE THE BEAT AND THE VERSES...-3/5-

ALL IN ALL "GOODIES" IS A GREAT ALBUM WITH A VARIETY OF JAMS.. TRUST ME... CIARA HARRIS IS THE NEXT BIG THING.....

top_exclaim.text: This is a pretty good CD. The band is made up of members from Entombed & Dismember, & the sound is something in between the 2. This was a great CD that I could just sit around & chill to while drinking a few beers. My favs on here are "Slowly Burnt to Death," "Unsane...," and "Sent Home in a Box." This album not quite as fast as some death metal, in fact the title song is kinda slow & sludgy, but its still cool as $hit. It's even got a bong hit solo in it (420 FOREVER!!!)! But this is definitly one of those times where it sounds great JUST the way it is... If you like this CD, I recommend Entombed "Left Hand Path" &/or "Clandestine," and Dismember "Indecent & Obscene/Peices" &/or "Like an Everflowing Stream." DEATH METAL RULES!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

top_five_star_obscene.text:
This is a review for the deluxe edition of the album, and I would suggest that everyone buys the deluxe version, it is definitely worth it for the bonus tracks.

Let me begin this review by stating that this is the best Judas Priest record since 'Painkiller'. The one small gripe that stops it from getting a perfect score from me is the shoddy production that muffles Scott Travis' double bass (especially noticeable on the song 'Metalizer'). However, when playing the physical CD copy of the album on a good stereo system, the production isn't bad enough to disrupt the listening experience.

To the performance of each individual on this album;

Rob Halford:

Despite what some would have you believe, Rob's vocals are not weak on this album. In fact, for mine he is simply nailing his mid range vocals and, thankfully, he sticks to this mid range for much of the record.

His highs on this record are hit and miss, from great on 'Halls of Valhalla' to not-so-great on 'Metalizer' and with “Battle Cry' we get a bit of both. Luckily, he keeps the shrieking to a minimum on this record and it really helps his performance.

For a singer on the north side of 60, his performance is stellar.

Glenn Tipton:

Unfortunately, age seems to be catching up with Mr. Tipton. Fortunately for us, he seems wise enough to realize this and leaves most of the soloing to the newcomer in the axe department. Still, at 67, one can live with a sloppy solo or two from the great man. His performance on the album, while not the greatness of yesteryear, is serviceable.

Richie Faulkner:

And so we come to the star of this show. Without a doubt, this man is the breath of fresh air Priest needed to revitalize themselves. Richie simply shreds on this album and it was a very wise (and brave) decision from Halford and Co to allow him to handle the majority of the solos on this album.

Fortunately, Richie just simply 'gets' the whole Priest vibe. From his great stage presence on the 'Epitaph' DVD to his performance on 'Redeemer', it is clear to me that Richie deserves his place in this band. It is amazing to think that someone who is half the age of Tipton can replace a 30+ year veteran guitarist and fit in so seamlessly.

Any doubts over Priest minus K.K Downing have now been put to bed in my eyes and I really feel sorry for those who dismiss this album simply because of K.K's absence, because they will be missing out on a great Priest album.

Ian Hill:

Is it just me, or is this the loudest Ian's bass has been turned up in the mix for ages? It is especially heavy on 'Secrets of the Dead'.

Anyway, not much to say here other than the man is as solid as he has ever been over their 40 year career. Possibly one of the best, most underrated bass players ever.

Scott Travis:

A beast, pure and simple. Even with the double bass muffled in the mix you can tell he is a beast. Probably my second favorite metal drummer of all time behind Nicko McBrain of Maiden fame.

With that out of the way, lets move onto the songs themselves.

Dragonaut – A great, fast paced opener with a good chorus and some nice solos that remind me a bit of 'Between the Hammer and the Anvil'. Good choice to start the album with. 9/10

Redeemer of Souls – The title track of the album and a decent one at that. Some definite 'Hell Patrol' vibes here, but it is the catchy-as-hell chorus and Richie Faulkner that saves it from being simply a so-so song. 7/10

Halls of Valhalla – Epic. Simply Epic. The best track on the album. Halford's performance on this track alone should put to rest any notion that he has 'lost it' as far as I’m concerned. The ascending scream towards the end of the track puts other younger vocalists to shame and Richie's solos are fantastic. This will no doubt go down as one of my all time favorite Priest tracks, it's that good. 20/10!!

Sword of Damocles – Definitely different for Priest. Loved the chorus instantly, but the rest of the track is a bit of a grower, still yet to make up my mind as to how good it really is. Another great performance by Halford here though. 7.5/10

March of the Damned – To those that say Halford sounds tired on this track, I beg to differ somewhat. Personally, I think he is purposely trying to sing in a more morose/sullen way to fit the tone of the song which, unfortunately, makes him come across as sounding like a tired Ozzy clone. Thankfully the song is saved by the 'Metal God' riff-age and some more nice solo's from Richie. I also think this song will translate better in the 'live' environment. 7/10

Down in Flames – Some people seem to dislike this song more than I’d expect. To me it is a nice, straightforward, stick-it-to-the-man metal number with a good, albeit repetitive chorus and a very good vocal performance by Halford. I don't know exactly why, but I really dug this track from the first listen, probably thanks to the sweet intro. Maybe that means it will wear thin quickly or maybe not, who knows. But right now I rate it 8/10

Hell and Back- This track has been a grower. Another mid-paced, straightforward number that I didn't like as much as 'Down in Flames' initially, but it is certainly beginning to grow on me. I like the acoustic sounding intro and the main riff of the song is pretty good. Really like the ending of the song too, chorus isn't much to write home about though. 7/10

Cold Blooded – I love this song. Sounds quite different for Priest at times and the dark ploddy nature of the song only enhances it in my opinion. Another excellent performance by Halford, Richie carves it up again and it features what is probably Tipton's best solo on the album. 8.5/10

Metalizer – I've heard this referred to as the 'Hell Rider' of this album, both in sound and in the fact that the song is disappointing. Well, lucky for me I am one of the few Priest fans who likes 'Hell Rider'. I really don't like Halford's high pitch vocals when he sings 'Goes for your throat' in the chorus however, or the fact that this song really shows how muffled Scott Travis' double bass really is. Not as terrible as some would make you believe, but definitely not one of the best on the album. 6.5/10

Crossfire – I'm sorry, but his song bores the hell out of me. I don't know, maybe it is just too bluesy sounding for me and the chorus is pretty crappy. The middle section of the song is okay, but overall it's just...meh. 4/10

Secrets of the Dead – Another different sounding song for Priest and another great, epic sounding song. Slow, dark and heavy with a strong bass sound, some trippy solos, and some interesting lyrics. I particularly like the spoken word section;

'We never learn from our mistakes
Imperfect till the end
You can't pick up the pieces
And the hearts we break can't mend

As if they knew the gift they brought
Was wasted on this earth
They searched out for a better race
On whom to bring rebirth'

Shows that the band isn't afraid to try a few different things, even this late into their career. Third best track on the main album. 9.5/10

Battle Cry – This. Is. Priest! Could have fit on 'Painkiller' easily. Great riffs and nice solo. Brilliant mid range vocals and, whilst the high pitch 'battle cry' in the chorus is kind of cringe-worthy, the last two shrieks at the end of the song are pretty good. F'n love the lyrics in this song. Makes you want to don your armor and charge into battle with sword held high! 10/10

Beginning of the End – Let us hope this song title doesn't suggest that the end of Priest is too nigh. On another note, this is a great mellow way to wind down the album. Not my favorite Priest ballad, that distinction belongs to 'Beyond the Realms of Death', but a nice song none-the-less. 7/10

Snakebite – Has a bit of an AC/DC feel to it. Can't say I'm overly fond of it, don't particularly like the chorus. Maybe it will grow on me. 5/10

Tears of Blood – Nice, up-beat chugging riff and another good performance from Halford. Chorus is catchy and solos are good from both guitarists. Straightforward and solid, liked it straight away much like 'Down in Flames'. 7.5/10

Creatures – How I wish this song was on the main CD in place of 'Crossfire'. The chorus is simply screaming to be performed live, but being a bonus track I am highly doubtful it will ever be played. One can only wish, I suppose. Absolutely love Richie's solo in this song. 10/10

Bring It On – Another straightforward slab of metal in the vein of 'Down in Flames', 'Hell and Back' and 'Tears of Blood'. Nothing terrible here, nice solos again. 7/10

Never Forget – An ode to Priest fans everywhere and the song that hoses down the foreboding title of the ballad on the main disc by suggesting they'll be around for a little while yet. Better than 'Beginning of the End'. 8/10

So, there it is. I may be in the minority here, and I know it is only early days, but right now this is one of my top 5 favorite Priest records. I honestly don't understand how anyone can find this album boring as even the slower songs, with the exception of 'Crossfire', are interesting to me. So, here's cheers to a great album and fingers crossed for 'Creatures' to be played live!!
top_five_star_obscene.title: Hail the Redeemer!

top_five_star_obscene.text:
J. D. Simo has figured it out. A perfectly wonderful guitarist/singer/songwriter, Simo did the smart thing, and managed to hook up with a rhythm section which is his equal, and they make what I call 'a glorious noise.'

SIMO is their debut album, and it's a very auspicious birth. It's going to get called things like retro, '60s, this that and the other, and deservedly so, but it's really just great rock, played by a cool bunch. Together a little more than a year, they've been unleashing their hard rock fury over audiences, and garnering a great deal of attention along the way.

Straight out of the box they evoke the halcyon tones of the best of the sixties - Aoh is a driving rock and roller that barrels along like a freight train. Produced by bassist Frank Swart at Fry Pharmacy in Nashville, and he got it just right. It's raw as raw gets, and the mix is great - the rhythm section is front and center, leaving Simo to weave his magic over the top like a surfer riding a torpedo. He manages to stay on top, and it attests to his power as a singer and player. Swart and drummer Adam Abrashoff are a hyperkinetic coupling who never back down. They take Simo's Yardbirdsian intro and go off into an experiential ride down the best side of another era. I can throw all kinds of cliches at this, and they wear them very well. These fellas play like they mean it - no pretense, just great and obvious rock and roll.

Shake It is the album's first single, and it's not an obvious choice, but what the hell is a single now anyway - Simo's vocal is piped through a wash of grind, and it has a great garage groove, but garage bands generally couldn't play this well. That's the biggest difference between the American garage scene of the '60s and the truly great bands of that era - like this bunch, they could play. Simo isn't a techie shredder, he's more along the lines of gutsy hard rock, filled with verve and passion.

Dynamics play big here, and it's great to hear them passing around the lead - Abrashoff is a pounder who has enough finesse to sound right - not the quantized crap you hear on most computer soaked metal and rock, but more like someone who loves to play the drums like a musical instrument. Swart dances in between Simo and Adam's aggression with some very melodic swoops and swoons and a nice round tone that never clashes with the guitarist's brighter Marshall tones. When J. D. cranks up his wah pedal towards the end, Swart climbs dizzily up his neck, and they sound perfect together. Yes, a glorious noise. If you didn't know it, you'd never know Fool For You was a Curtis Mayfield tune - they own it on this cover.

They segue right into Young Man, Old Man and it's a gentle acoustic intro that will bring to mind The Who circa Tommy, or maybe Stevie Winwood - Simo has assimilated the era, and I'm not certain that he hasn't simply time traveled to the now to screw with us. Damned glad he did. And I'm glad he brought Swart and Abrashoff - they turn this acoustic intro into a fiery rock exhibition. This is big room rock - born to be played in large rooms to religiously fervent fans of real rock. The end of this song is transcendent - it would be great in any era, but I feel like it's never been so necessary as now.

Clementia's Lament is born in one of Dante's hells, then is soon lifted by some Mid-Eastern rhythms from Simo's right hand, and Swart's brutal bass. They seldom play lockstep, but they know when to, and that makes all the difference. Simo's solo is psychotically beautiful, and it winds into a very melodic section that suggests the wistful melodic abilities of Eric Carmen's best works. Look him up if you don't know him, fer crissakes. Have I mentioned that this is a glorious, glorious noise? Great rock peaks out from under the mush, the mire, and these sad times.

Then it's off and into a slow blues that is Greeny approved. This is almost too period correct, and while I may have opted for a cleaner vocal, I will admit to being a very picky bastard, and it ain't my record, right? They do nail it - the rhythm section s loosely leaden, and Simo is a masterful axeman, who can't be faulted. Abrashoff is one of the few drummers I've heard in a while who really gets cymbals - his punctuation is sublime. What's On Your Mind then gently waltzes out with a beautiful bit of nylon picking - nice. Maybe that's Peter Green's requiem.

Simo rarely seems to use any effects save for his wah pedal and on The Same Thing he gives a university level dissertation on its use. While the rhythm section plays a big burlesque, Simo dances elegantly into the stratosphere with some big cat moans. He also whips out some great psychedelic slide work that both Jimi and Jimmy would approve. Towards the end he goes into a nice skronk that let's us catch our breath before the big end. Yup, a glorious noise.

Thank You Tony Jones is a fabulous wind-up that has me guessing that Tony Jones implies Elvin and Mr. Williams. The rockingest drum solo song since Moby Dick.

Evil - that's how it ends, and damned if it's not. Howlin' Wolf knew this was going to happen, believe me. He may have grumbled about rock, but there's no way he would not have dug this. It's the big LZ finish, and SIMO has killed it - they've made a record that is at once nostalgic, and immediate. I hope this catches on as well as it should - we need some big rock bands who realize there ain't no shame in growing from the blues into something more glorious. I was told this week that one of this era's biggest blues rockers was embarrassed by rocking out on a big stage with a rock band. And that's a goddamned shame - the only reason the blues guys didn't sound like this was the equipment just wasn't there yet. They would have loved this - it's a glorious noise.

Don't be afraid of the rock - it shall set you free.

Join me at Rock Guitar Daily with Tony Conley
top_five_star_obscene.title: SIMO - A Glorious Noise

top_five_star_obscene.text:
To all those critics of power metal i sincerely hope you die a horrible death. Sorry for that unsavoury attitude but i am just totally pissed at someone,a 'friend', who put me off power metal for a while until i regained my senses and came back to stand strong behind one of my favourite metal genres!

I'll quickly discuss my history with power metal. This album was actually the first power metal album i bought in my life. And i'll tell you straight off i loved every moment of it. Before listening to this i really never knew power metal i mean sure i'd heard of Stratovarius and Helloween but didn't really know them or their sound at all. I had very little concept of what power metal was. I was under the more British and American spell that heavy metal had to be as loud and aggressive as possible so i was mainly into metalcore-ish stuff, death metal and thrash, although i predominantly like thrash metal. To tell you the truth when i was listening to some of the stuff i was then, like Cannibal Corpse (who i slightly despise now) i didn't really like it that much, it was generic to me didn't stand out or nothing, unlike say Slayer or something. I wanted more melody, more clean singing but still maintaining that speed, heavy riffing and great solos. I basically wanted something that was new and progressive with refreshing tunes.

I bought this album and was amazed at the brilliant musicianship showcased by the guitarists notably Mr Luca Turilli, the vocals were beautiful thanks to Fabio Leone's soft touch and soaring abilities, the wonderful keyboard by Alex Strapoli, the drums were insanely fast bordering on blast beats in places due to Alex Holzwarth's precision skill and the orchestra, AN ORCHESTRA!! I NEVER KNEW THAT BANDS WERE COLLABORATING WITH ORCHESTRAS!! This was a great thing for me because one of my other loves is classical music of all sorts. Rhapsody are god damn amazing musically, structurally, lyrically and as people!! All the songs on this album are fantastic and build up to that hell of a finisher the title track epic of 13mins!!! This album helped me spawn quite a healthy power metal collection featuring bands like Primal Fear, Edguy, Sonata Arctica, Gamma Ray, Helloween, Strato and Nocturnal Rites oh and of course Rhapsody!My verdict on the album is that for a power metal fan or some one whole likes epic music this is a MUST HAVE!!SYMPHONY OF THE ENCHANTED LANDS!

Don't want to rant but i want to address the issue of people who criticise power metal. Power metal in my opinion is modern heavy metal that has progressed lightyears since the '80s. Power metal contains all the things about metal that metalheads love. Fast paced heavy riffing, brilliant wild solos, story or fantasy themed lyrics, double bass drumming, licks a plenty, dual guitars, talented singers with trademark voices and lots of energy! Please tell me what else heavy metal is about? It's not just about being loud, noisy and drunk, sure thats fun and there are plenty power metallers that advocate that eg Edguy lol. Power metal bands always and i mean ALWAYS cite the metal greats as their influences Priest, Maiden, Accept, Metallica, Queensryche, Fates Warning, Helloween etc and follow in their footsteps. I gurantee you every power metal band you listen to will make you think 'Boy that sounded like Maiden' or like with Primal Fear 'F*** this is Judas Priest man!!'.. Power metal has an uplifting spirit to it as well and a sense of being a metal community-- often inviting black metal singers,death metal singers and female vocalists to collaborate with them-- refer to Tobias Sammet's master piece the Metal Opera by Avantasia for a great example of this or Kamelot's the Black Halo. And so freakin what if they wear leather and clad and look like a bunch of sissy boys, metal kinda has a history of that if you look back on it Maiden, Priest, Manowar and KISS to name some-- for christ sake look at the back of Number of the Beast they don't look like fat muscle men do they??

My 'friend' as i spoke of earlier begged me to burn her a copy of my Rhapsody cd when i ranted and raved about it. Then when i did and met her again she's like it's really cheesy. Tell me wtf is cheesy about singing with a great voice about a story of Tolkien proportions using heavy metal and adding all these elements like orchestras, narration and SFX to enhance the experience?? NOTHING!! And here's the irony she listens to bands that 'aren't cheesy' like Greenday, Red Hot Chilli Peppers and Pink!!??? What a load of crap man i hate to tell that bitch but 'love' and 'heartache' are the most cheesy and overused cliches in the music industry!!

My final word: POWER METAL IS GREAT!! RHAPSODY ROCK!! And buy this CD no regrets believe me!

Hope that was helpful.

\m/
top_five_star_obscene.title: Epic Power Metal... nothing can surpass this

top_five_star_obscene.text:
A new Cock Sparrer CD is here after ten long years, and not only does it sound great, it tips well and can be applied directly to warts. I hesitate calling them the first Oi band as it's not my fight, and I mostly agree with the assessment Sparrer and others were proto-Oi bands. Sparrer have been going at it for thirty odd years, tower over their peers as musicians, and in Here We Stand have recorded the best album of their career.

The only complaint I have with Here We Stand is how the tracks were ordered (I just got word the American release will address this issue). Sparrer deliver three types of songs: thoughtful laments on what once was and is either no more or has gone wrong, funny tales of corner pub numbnuttery, and positive anthems of pride. They write catchy tunes in their sleep, as do The Buzzcocks. You somehow expect more than that from Cock Sparrer. I graded eight of the fourteen tracks eight. Here We Stand is front-loaded with eights that hit similar themes. The best tracks, and many of their best don't take themselves seriously, don't kick in until the middle. The best are "Spirit Of '76", "Last Orders", "Suicide Girls", "Sussed", "Don't Stop", "Despite All This" and "Too Late".

With Cock Sparrer you can always rely on simple melodies improved upon to the point of excellence, rollicking bass guitar, soaring guitar chords and clever single note progressions, well-written humor, great sing-alongs, and they do some of their best work during what's esoterically known at The Truck Driver's Gear Change.

Cock Sparrer lyrics are the best. From "Last Orders": "Four pints of lager later things went from bad to worse/ She came back from the toilet, caught me going through her purse/ She said forget the taxi, you're gonna need a hearse/ There's time for one last round// One tequila, two tequila, three tequila four/ I knew I wasn't drunk I had a good grip on the floor/ I puckered up to kiss her but she punched me in the jaw/ Time for one last round". From "Suicide Girls": "For a lifetime or a single night/ You might get into her world/ That'd be strictly by invite/ She's with the Suicide Girls// Just don't try to take her away/ You'll be dead before you fail// Get in the back seat and know your place/ She's with the Suicide Girls."

Here We Stand is the best effort so far from the best Oi band the universe has burped up so far. You can still be a punk and not like Here We Stand, but you'd be a part-time punk at best.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Best Sparrer Album Ever

top_five_star_obscene.text:
When we were kids Nick scared the baby crap out of us with Birthday Party. We grew up with him and weren't quite as scared because The Bad Seeds' stories were night-time fables told by a benevolent campfire master. Just leave the light on, we'll be OK even if dreaming of being buried under ten feet of snow white snow.
Now we're all grown up. Middle age, actually and Nick's still right here in the room. Maybe he's exorcised his personal demons. Maybe he hopes we have too. Yeah right.
Grinderman wakes us from the complacency of midlife. We stopped bitching and fighting and we started whining. Like old men on the porch. "In my day there were fights at every show." Blah,blah,blah.
But Nick reminds us that we're still pissed off. And it's OK to be pissed. Maybe we've slowed down. But we're not dead. Yet. And neither is Nick.
When we were 21 we had to juggle girls like Naomi Watts juggling for King Kong. OOOps, dropped a few. Now, after break-ups and divorces and rehabs the woman don't seem as ethereal, sweet and horny. Do they? We've got the "No P-ssy Blues" and thank God Nick is still here with us to commiserate. Play it over and over on the way home from the party. Loud. And don't forget to feed the cat before you go to bed. Alone. Thanks Nick for sticking around.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Life IS Pain

top_five_star_obscene.text: Everyone on here who says " oh buy this album instead cause these guys suck " is crap. I have all of Obituary's albums including Frozen In Time, I have Dissection, Marduk, Amon Amarth, Old Inflames, Dismember, At The Gates. This is not Emo crap, because I hate that crap. This is good melodic poppy metal, much like Edguy, Hammerfall or most of the european power metal scene. This is good straight ahead rock/metal. Out of all the crap today out from America KSE, Shadows Fall, and Unearth ar the best of this melodic AMerican scene. If you like tech thrash from america THen buy Nevermore or Jag Panzer. Which i also own. When Darkness Falls, Rose Of Sharyn, and End of Heartache are the best songs on here. This is a great album for anyone trying to get into metal period because its a starting point to develop your tastes for flashy riffs, straight head groovy riffs, screaming, actual singing, everything. These guys incorporate every aspect of metal into their arsenal and maybe they dont do it the bst but these guys have brought elements of power, thrash, death,black, and melodeath metal and made it poopy at the same time. Its that simple. Their is so much great metal out there and alot of underground stuff from sweden and germany and finland. In order to get into that stuff you have to start with something that will ease you into it. END OF HEARTACHE IS THAT ALBUM.
top_five_star_obscene.title: The Final End Of Heartache review. Period PEOPLE!!!!

top_five_star_obscene.text:
1981-82 was not only busy years for the Whispers, but they were great years. This music makes me feel ashamed of being born in the early 80s and growing up to most of the crap thats out in black music. The team kept the formula going by givin' us something to dance to and romance to, but only this time each album side seperated the type of songs.

Every track is good and its good enough to have a single on its own, but the late Dick Griffey was'nt havin' that (lol). "In the Raw" is funky messenger groove (#4 on the R&B charts) about "being yourself." What great Whispers' groove is not complete is without a short rap verse from the Scott brothers (Hip-Hop has caught on to them as it recently had for The Sylvers, Stevie Wonder, and Teena Marie). "Turn Me Out" and "Emergency" (#22 on the R&B charts) were groovy, yet galactic tunes that work up quite a sweat, thanks once again to Leon Sylvers III and some of his siblings and members of Dynasty who wrote and played background instruments on all the dance songs on the album. Although it appears that many of those songs Leon put together sounds like much of the SOLAR roster, The Whispers have their own melodies that work with these songs and the song writers knew what they were doing.

Side B was strictly for romancin' and The Whispers produced the entire side that includes the smoooth title cut and "Small Talkin'" which are my favorites from the Romancin' side. Unfortunatley, SOLAR Records never managed to put out any ballads as singles for the Whispers on this album and the ballad heavy follow up, Love For Love. Like much of the roster at the time, I think Dick Griffey wanted the focus to be on more dance music to appeal to the younger crowd so only "In The Raw" and "Emergency" were released as singles. He had just signed a distribution deal with Elektra Records. After a rushed release (This Kind Of Lovin') done a few short months before Love Is Where You Find It was released, Dick needed a slightly different approach to recoup the horrible sales from the previous album as well as simular lackluster sales from late summer 81" releases from Shalamar and Lakeside. He marketed The Whispers more toward dance songs while marketed Lakeside more toward ballads for their late 81" releases (Lakeside scored a top 5 R&B hit in early 1982 with "I Want To Hold Your Hand"). Other than the unusual choice of singles, the entire album was excellent and my favorite album by the band. It also topped the R&B charts (their second and last album to do so in their careers) and it was followed by another successful tour and three promotional videos that were played on BET during its early years. The Whispers will repeat the "Dancin', Romancin'" formula for their 1984 album, So Good and Babyface will use a simular formula for his 1986 debut album, Lovers.
top_five_star_obscene.title: In The Raw

top_five_star_obscene.text:
In 1990, Madonna had released her sixth album entitled, I'M BREATHLESS. This album, not a soundtrack to the film, "Dick Tracy", but contains music from and inspired the film, is simply great. It most certainly does not disappoint.

HE'S A MAN
A great way to start off this "Dick Tracy" inspired album. If you listen closely to the very start of the song, you'll a clip from the film, "Dick Tracy"

SOONER OR LATER
This song was performed by Madonna twice in the film. She performs an unplugged version (not this version) in the beginning before "Big Boy Caprice" (played by Al Pacino in his most annoying role yet) captures Lips Manlus. The second time she performs this song (this version) is when "Big Boy Caprice" has his grand opening at Lips Manlus' club, after taking over the club. Great jazzy song. Even my father likes this one.

HANKY PANKY
The album's second & final single. Without a music video to support it, this song hit the #10 spot (Billboard Hot 100). I had the pleasure of seeing Madonna perform this classic on RE-INVENTION TOUR in 2004, and she did an awesome job of it.

I'M GOING BANANAS
Love this tune.

CRY BABY
Not bad.

SOMETHING TO REMEMBER
A beautifully performed ballad. Expect to see this tune to pop as the title track to Madonna's 1995 greatest ballads album entitled, SOMETHING TO REMEMBER.

BACK IN BUSINESS
Not bad.

MORE
An acoustic/rehearsal version of this song was performed in the film by Madonna as "Breathless Mahoney". After "Big Boy Caprice" takes over Lips Manlus' club, as well as taking Breathless (who was Lips' flame) as his girl, he has Breathless and her back-up dancers / singers rehearse this for opening night. BIG BOY CAPRICE (To Breathless about 88 Keys): "The next time I crush fingers and turn them into pretzels, I just might do the same thing to your face".

WHAT CAN YOU LOSE (Performed by Madonna & Mandy Patinkin)
This duet between Madonna and Mandy Patinkin was also featured in the film, "Dick Tracy". Mandy Patinkin, himself, even had a supporting role in the film, as the pianist, "88 Keys". This song is rather good, but I wish Mandy would stick with what he's good at: Acting.

NOW I'M FOLLOWING YOU (Pt. 1) (Performed by Madonna & Warren Beatty)
Only over a minute long, but this song is really catchy.

NOW I'M FOLLOWING YOU (Pt. 2) (Performed by Madonna & Warren Beatty)
A great remix of "Now I'm Following You" (Part 1).

VOGUE
The album's first single. My favorite song off of this album. This song definitely defines Madonna of the 1990s and definitely defines Pop Music of the '90s. Great dance song here.

This is another one of Madonna's greatest albums. Mainly, every Madonna fan had bought this album only for "Vogue". I know I did, but after hearing the whole album, it became one of my favorites. Great album here.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Madonna ... I'M BREATHLESS (1990)

top_five_star_obscene.text:
There's not a guitarist out there who can play Jimi Hendrix's stuff better than the man himself - so what does Robert Dick think he's doing? Well, first of all, Mr Dick doesn't play the guitar. He plays the flute. And believe me, this ain't your maiden Aunt Mildred's pipe, (unless she was hanging around with John Cage).

Robert Dick is probably the best, and certainly the most adventurous flutist in jazz today, (or yesterday for that matter). On "Third Stone From the Sun," Mr Dick takes on Mr Hendrix's "Pali Gap," "Purple Haze," "Voodoo Child" and, of course, "Third Stone...," as well as three originals. He plays the flute, alto flute, bass flute, F contrabass flute and an A flat piccolo. He overdubs, plays chords, uses circular breathing - flutters and wails - and generally tears up the landscape. He is more than adequately assisted by Jerome Harris on various guitars, Jim Black on drums, the Soldier String Quartet and, on "Third Stone...," Marty Ehrlich on bass clarinet.

New World Records has done an excellent job on the sonics: Clean and precise, with just a hint of warmth.

"Third Stone..." is exciting, cutting edge jazz - but it is not for the faint of heart.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Not For the Faint of Heart

top_five_star_obscene.text: This collection of Dick van Dyke's favorite songs will definately make you "Put on a Happy Face!"  The songs on this album include NICE WORK IF YOU CAN GET IT* EASY STREET* PUT ON A HAPPY FACE* WALKING AND WHISTLIN' BLUES* BABY WON'T YOU PLESE COME HOME* ANY PLACE I HANG MY HAT IS HOME* AIN'T WE GOT FUN* THEY ALL LAUGHED* WIVES AND LOVERS* LAZYBONES* MY BABY JUST CARES FOR ME* WHEN YOU WANT 'EM, YOU CAN'T GET 'EM WHEN YOU'VE GOT 'EM, YOU DON'T WANT 'EM* and I'VE GOT A CRUSH ON YOU. The original album came out in 1963 around the same time he was doing the movie version of Bye Bye Birdie.  The original title was "Songs I Like by Dick van Dyke."  Dick van Dyke is a very talented man and a great singer and that is shown very well in his only full solo album.  It's hard to find, but very much worth the search.  If you like Dick van Dyke's singing, you should check out the original Broadway cast recording and motion picture soundtrack of Bye Bye Birdie.  His other musical credits include the soundtracks to Mary Poppins and Chitty Chitty Bang Bang. Another intersting musical venture of his was the 45rpm recod he put out in the late 50's.  It has two songs on it: THREE WHEELS ON MY WAGON and ONE PART DOG, NINE PARTS CAT. It is more for the sake of comedy than music, but its pretty neat to have.  All in all, I love this Cd ("Put on a Happy Face"), and just about everthing else he's done over the years.
top_five_star_obscene.title: I like Dick van Dyke!

top_five_star_obscene.text:
This CD is an excellent one. In my opinion, it isn't quite as good as Devil's Night, but for me to give this CD 5 stars must be saying something good. Nevertheless, this is a very impressive follow-up to Devil's Night. So I'll give you the rundown on the tracks.
1. Git Up - The opening track is pure adrenaline. Eminem begins what is to be a promising album, with Swifty and Kuniva bringing exciting rhymes to the song. 5/5
2. Loyalty - A damn good song. Obie Trice guest appears on this track, but D12 members Swift and Proof do it for me. This is an excellent track. 4/5
3. Just Like U - It's all Bizarre for this one. I didn't really take an interest in this one when I first heard it, but after a few more listens, D12's resident freak had me enjoying this a bit more. 4/5
4. I'll Be Damned - This track's a damn good one. The chorus is hella catchy and is funny in places. Great track. 5/5
5. Dude (Skit) - Funny as hell skit that leads into My Band. They should have this instead of the less-than-funnier intro for the actual video. Hilarious! 5/5
6. My Band - Yeah, everyone's heard this song, but it's still a great track. Never seems to lose it's charm. 5/5
7. U R The One - A love song from D12?! I never thought it'd be possible, but this works alright. Not D12, but not bad. 4/5
8. 6 In The Morning - I love this song. The chorus is great, and the verses are terrific, especially Kon Artis' rap is excellent, accompanying some pretty spooky music. 5/5
9. How Come - Another favourite, the lyrics are very deep, and the music is very memorable. A great track that encompasses the excellent skills of Eminem, Proof and Kon Artis. A dark song, but one that will be remembered. 5/5
10. Leave Dat Boy Alone - Sounds like a Western song... but I like it. Very cool song, with excellent lyrics, especially Swifty's... Very chilling... 5/5
11. Get My Gun - Now this is a song!! One part crazy rap, one part violent rap, and one part comedy rap. All killer! 5/5
12. Bizarre (Skit) - Pretty funny, for a skit. 4/5
13. Bitch - Now this is one hell of a funny song!! Eminem speaks gibberish, Kuniva and Kon Artis show their impeccable lyrical timing and humour create one hilarious verse and the chorus? Well, what can I say? It's crude, sure, but it's frickin' funny too! 5/5
14. Steve's Coffee House (Skit) - Steve King, the recording engineer for the tracks on the album shows us his musical side. Unfortunately, it's pretty crap. Nonetheless, it's funny! 4/5
15. D12 World - Pretty good song. Not as great as the others, but alright. Kinda reminds me of "That's How..." from Devil's Night. 4/5
16. 40 Oz. - A good song, although I don't listen to it too often. Still, it's a likeable one. 4/5
17. Commercial Break - First we had Obie Trice, now we introduce Young Zee, and he delivers some tight, if pretty bland, beats. 4/5
18. American Psycho II - A worthy sequel to the first song, with B-Real from Cypress Hill in the chorus. An eerie song, and one that'll keep you awake at night in fear... or not. 4/5
19. Bugz 97 - This is an earlier rhyme from former D12 member Bugz (R.I.P.). It's a pretty good rhyme actually, and he will be remebered. 4/5
20. Good Die Young - The tribute to Bugz. A heartfelt song, but it's just so good, lyrical-wise. Respect to Bugz. The best song on the album. 5/5
21. Keep Talkin' - The final song, and they finish with a bang. Violent, and gritty, but good. 5/5
So all in all, this is an excellent album. I recommend you buy it immediately!
top_five_star_obscene.title: A very worthy CD to add to your collection

top_five_star_obscene.text:
Unlike the earlier releases on Atlantic (Stay Hard and The Pack is Back), Raven had complete control over the production of Life's a Bitch. There were no record execs pressuring them to sound commercial and "sell, sell, sell". No heavy-handed producer was brought in to take over the project and make them use synthesizers and horn sections (ala The Pack is Back). In fact, Atlantic had all but given up on Raven and pretty much let them do whatever they wanted on their final Atlantic release. The result was quite possibly the best Raven album of their career.

Start to finish, Life's a Bitch shows Raven in peak form. The songs are loud, insane, and catchy. The guitar solos are larger than life. The arrangements are fresh and varied. You have 2-and-half minute slam-fests like "You're a Liar" and epic prog-like rockers like "On the Wings of an Eagle". Of course the usual Raven lunacy is on proud display in "Overload", "Pick Your Window", "Life's a Bitch", and "Playing with the Razor". There honestly is not a weak track on here. No filler, no shortcuts, no disposable over-produced yawners. This is Raven doing penance for the earlier Atlantic releases, which quite frankly really weren't their fault.

You can imagine being young starving musicians, coming to a new country, with a new record deal, and your bosses telling you what to do. Raven was at the mercy of Atlantic and did what they were told and recorded The Pack is Back. But for that, the band was summarily dismissed as sell-outs by the fans and dumped by Atlantic. It's unfortunate, because since then all Raven has done has put out one stellar release after another.

Life's a Bitch is the meaner, wiser, hungrier re-birth of Raven. No fan of NWOBHM metal should miss this. It truly was a landmark recording for this British trio.
top_five_star_obscene.title: This is Raven done right

top_five_star_obscene.text:
There are many reviews that go into great depth and I agree with many...especially some of the comments made by "Cowpunk1". I started listening to The Damned in about 83 or so via tape cassettes from friends. My first purchases of the Damned was in 1985, which consisted of the "Black Album" and "Another Great Album" (best of). At the time I lived in H.B. and was into the hardcore scene but was hopelessly infected by the Damned and their sound, which I became addicted to. Their sound ranges from catchy hooky rhythms to haunting theatrical sounds.

I hate to make this comparison, but the Damned remind me of the Beatles...don't freak out...give me a chance. I am an Elvis (sun records era) man not a Beatles fan, but the Beatles were talented and through out their career experimented and changed styles with great artistic and popular success.

I like the original sound of the Damned as well as Grave Disorder and all the albums in between. They truly showed what talent coupled with real artistic creativity can accomplish...30 years of a wide range of styles and sounds all of which are "bitichin".

They say the "Black Album" is an experimental album delving into the psychedelic...uh ok..probably..It is a great album and I recommend it highly. If I were allowed only 10 albums/cds to listen to for the rest of my life, 3 would be by the Damned (Machine gun etiquette, Black Album, Another great CD by the Damned)

For the person who is not familiar with the Damned, but would like to know what they are comparable to, I have bad news. They have changed so much over the years (in a good way)that I don't know what to compare them to. For a band like The Cult, it is easy to say The Doors, Stepppenwolf, and Led Zepplin all came together in about 1987 and created The Cult Album "Electric", but for the Damned...not so easy.

I hate tags like Goth, Gothic,or Goth Metal; I feel they are new terms and should be attached to acts like Marilyn Manson et al...or even Disturbed and Korn. The Damned has a range far surpassing any one style or genre. The original dark and brooding music "Death Punk" should connected to bands like Bauhaus, Joy Division, Siouxsie, and yes even the Smiths
top_five_star_obscene.title: A band on a journey...

top_five_star_obscene.text:
Her latest album, Rebel Heart, fuses a lot of genres but it is a pop album. I own the Super Deluxe edition which features additional songs that are not included on the regular or deluxe versions.
One thing about Madonna's style, especially since 2000 is her fusion of rock, pop, trance, hip hop, and R&B. Rebel Heart is no exception to this. I think this album of hers is her best release since her 2005 album, Confessions on a Dance Floor.

Here are all 23 tracks:
1. Living For Love
2. Devil Pray
3. Ghosttown
4. Unapologetic Bitch
5. Illuminati
6. Bitch I'm Madonna
7. Hold Tight
8. Joan Of Arc
9. Iconic
10. HeartBreakCity
11. Body Shop
12. Holy Water
13. Inside Out
14. Wash All Over Me
15. Best Night
16. Veni Vidi Vici
17. S.E.X.
18. Messiah
19. Rebel Heart
20. Borrowed Time
21. Addicted
22. Beautiful Scars
23. Graffiti Heart

Conceptually, Madonna describes this album as a two-in-one. The name Rebel Heart implies two sides to her persona: the Rebellious Madonna, and the Heartfelt sensitive Madonna. Musically, this album was created with a large number of producers, something Madonna has never done before on an album. Typically, Madonna works with one or two producers per album. The wide range of producers on Rebel Heart created songs that fuse multiple genres together:

Rock n Roll fused with Dance/Pop: The songs that have a guitar-based instrument on which the rest of the EDM and Madonna's lyrics are built, include Addicted, Rebel Heart, Joan Of Arc, Borrowed Time, Grafiti Heart, Devil Pray, Ghosttown, and Body Shop (which uses the Indian Sari instrument). Each of these songs start with the guitar hook. Some have acoustic guitar, and others, like Addicted, have the electric guitar as the central instrument. The producers fuse EDM beats on top of the guitar hooks, and in combination with Madonna's voice creates a rich blended listening experience.

These songs fuse piano bars reminiscent of the days of the Blues, with dance, pop, or R&B: HeartBreakCity, Messiah, Wash All Over Me. These songs are melancholy in nature, sort of like Madonna's version of the Blues, but fused with moderate paced pop drum beats, synthetics, and rich dramatic vocals. These songs produce a different feel from her guitar based tracks.

Her first single from the album, Living for Love, fuses the Gospel genre with pop and dance. It starts out with classic Madonna-esque pop sounds reminiscent of her 80s and 90s era. The track does include a choir of people singing the chorus in the background.

Her song, Unapologetic Bitch fuses a Reggae beat and Hip Hop. The base of the song is in Reggae style, with the Hip Hop beats and sounds added over it. The pace of the song is that of the Reggae style, moderate, not fast.

Her remaining tracks that I did not name, particularly Bitch I'm Madonna, Veni Vidi Vici, Iconic, and S.E.X. are very much Hip Hop and R&B infused tracks. She either raps on these tracks or sings and features guest rappers like Nicki Minaj and Nas.

In terms of Musicology, Madonna's Rebel Heart (Super Deluxe) edition is an excellent album for study because she successfully fuses multiple genres: Rock, Blues, Gospel, Pop, Dance, Hip Hop, R&B, Piano jazz. Additionally, the lyrics to these tracks are all written or co-written by Madonna, and her prowess to write powerful lyrics that captures her essence as a persona is as strong today as it was when she first hit the scene in 1981.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Rebel Heart Super Deluxe Edition - Her best work in a decade!
